questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring Substances Acidic Basic and Neutral
questions.prompt: Compare the properties of acids and bases, and explain how indicators detect them.
questions.answer: Acids and bases have contrasting properties. Acids generally taste sour (like lemon and vinegar) and turn blue litmus red, while bases taste bitter, feel slippery (like soap and baking soda solution) and turn red litmus blue. Because tasting unknown substances is unsafe, we detect acids and bases using indicators — substances that change colour depending on the nature of what they touch. Litmus is the most common, but natural indicators also work: turmeric stays yellow in acids but turns red in bases, and china rose and red cabbage juice change colour too. A neutral substance, such as pure water, does not change the colour of litmus.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring Substances Acidic Basic and Neutral
questions.prompt: What is neutralisation, and where do we see it in daily life?
questions.answer: Neutralisation is the reaction that occurs when an acid and a base are mixed in suitable amounts: they cancel each other's nature and form a salt and water, usually releasing some heat. This idea explains several everyday situations. When the stomach produces too much acid and causes discomfort, an antacid — which is a base — neutralises the excess acid. Farmers add lime, a base, to soil that is too acidic to make it suitable for crops. The treatment of factory waste before it is released often involves neutralising acids or bases so the water is safe.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring Substances Acidic Basic and Neutral
questions.prompt: A gardener finds the soil is too basic for a plant. Should they add an acidic or basic material, and why?
questions.answer: An acidic material, to neutralise the excess base and bring the soil closer to neutral.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring Substances Acidic Basic and Neutral
questions.prompt: Why does an ant sting feel better after rubbing baking soda on it?
questions.answer: An ant injects an acid; baking soda is a mild base that neutralises the acid, easing the sting.

questions.answer: A coil that acts as a magnet when current flows through it.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe the components of a simple electric circuit and the role of each.
questions.answer: A simple electric circuit needs a few components working together to form a complete path for current. The cell or battery is the source that supplies electricity. Connecting wires, made of a conducting metal, carry the current around the circuit. A device such as a bulb uses the current to do something useful, like giving light. A switch is included to open or close the circuit: when the switch is closed the path is complete and current flows, so the bulb glows; when it is open the path is broken and the bulb goes off. Current flows only when the whole circuit is closed.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain the heating and magnetic effects of electric current with examples.
questions.answer: Electric current produces several effects, two of which are especially useful. The heating effect occurs because current makes a wire hot as it passes through; this is used in electric heaters, irons, geysers and the filaments of bulbs. The magnetic effect means that a wire carrying current behaves like a magnet and can deflect a nearby compass needle. When the wire is wound into a coil and current flows, it becomes an electromagnet — a magnet that can be switched on and off — which is used in electric bells, cranes that lift iron, and many electrical machines.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why are the handles of electricians' tools covered with rubber or plastic?
questions.answer: Because rubber and plastic are insulators; they prevent current from passing into the user, keeping them safe.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: An electromagnet can be switched off but an ordinary magnet cannot. Why is this useful in a scrapyard crane?
questions.answer: The crane can pick up iron when current flows and drop it by switching off the current, controlling the magnetism at will.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The World of Metals and Non-metals
questions.prompt: Compare the physical properties of metals and non-metals, noting exceptions.
questions.answer: Metals and non-metals differ in many physical properties. Metals are usually lustrous (shiny), hard, malleable (can be hammered into sheets), ductile (can be drawn into wires), good conductors of heat and electricity, and sonorous (ring when struck). Non-metals are generally dull, brittle, poor conductors, and neither malleable nor ductile. However, there are notable exceptions that show these are general trends rather than strict rules: mercury is a metal but is liquid at room temperature, and graphite is a non-metal yet conducts electricity well. These exceptions are why we look at several properties together before classifying a substance.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The World of Metals and Non-metals
questions.prompt: Describe how metals and non-metals react with oxygen and acids.
questions.answer: Metals and non-metals can be distinguished by their chemical reactions. When metals react with oxygen they form metal oxides, which are usually basic in nature — for instance, iron slowly forms rust and magnesium burns brightly to give magnesium oxide. Non-metals react with oxygen to form oxides that are usually acidic, such as those formed when sulphur or carbon burns. With acids, reactive metals react to release hydrogen gas, which burns with a characteristic 'pop' sound, whereas most non-metals do not react with acids in this way. These contrasting reactions are a reliable guide to whether a substance is a metal or a non-metal.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The World of Metals and Non-metals
questions.prompt: A shiny solid is hammered into a thin sheet and conducts electricity. Metal or non-metal? Why?
questions.answer: A metal — lustre, malleability and conductivity are all metallic properties.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The World of Metals and Non-metals
questions.prompt: Why is graphite an unusual non-metal?
questions.answer: Unlike most non-metals, graphite conducts electricity, which is why it is used in pencils and electrodes.

questions.subject: science

questions.prompt: What is galvanising?
questions.answer: Coating iron with a layer of zinc to prevent rusting.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Changes Around Us Physical and Chemical
questions.prompt: Distinguish between physical and chemical changes, giving examples and signs.
questions.answer: A physical change is one in which no new substance is formed and which is usually reversible; the material may change in size, shape or state but remains the same substance. Examples include melting ice, dissolving sugar in water and tearing paper. A chemical change produces one or more new substances and is usually difficult to reverse; examples include burning paper, cooking food, souring of milk and rusting of iron. Several signs point to a chemical change: a new substance with different properties, a change in colour, a gas being given off, heat or light produced or absorbed, or a solid settling out. Recognising these signs helps tell the two kinds of change apart.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Changes Around Us Physical and Chemical
questions.prompt: Explain rusting, the conditions needed for it, and how it can be prevented.
questions.answer: Rusting is a chemical change in which iron slowly reacts with oxygen in the presence of moisture to form a reddish-brown substance called rust, which flakes away and weakens the metal. Both air (oxygen) and water are needed for rusting to occur, which is why iron rusts faster in damp conditions. Rusting can be prevented by keeping these away from the iron: painting or oiling/greasing forms a protective layer, and galvanising coats the iron with a layer of zinc that protects it. Using rust-resistant alloys such as stainless steel is another way to avoid the problem.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Changes Around Us Physical and Chemical
questions.prompt: Why does cut fruit turning brown count as a chemical change?
questions.answer: Because the fruit reacts with oxygen in the air to form a new, differently coloured substance — a sign of a chemical change.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Changes Around Us Physical and Chemical
questions.prompt: Iron gates are painted regularly. How does this prevent rust?
questions.answer: Paint forms a barrier that keeps oxygen and moisture away from the iron, so the rusting reaction cannot occur.

questions.prompt: What are hormones?
questions.answer: Chemical messengers made by endocrine glands that control body changes.

questions.answer: Because the skin becomes more active and needs regular care.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Adolescence A Stage of Growth and Change
questions.prompt: Describe the changes that take place during adolescence and what controls them.
questions.answer: Adolescence is the stage of life between childhood and adulthood during which the body and mind gradually mature. Physically, there is a rapid increase in height and changes in body shape, and new features called secondary characteristics develop, differing between boys and girls. These changes are controlled by hormones — chemical messengers made by endocrine glands and carried in the blood — which direct growth and the other developments of this stage. Importantly, these changes begin at different ages for different people, and this variation is entirely normal. Understanding that the changes are natural and hormone-driven helps adolescents face them with confidence rather than worry.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Adolescence A Stage of Growth and Change
questions.prompt: Explain why good nutrition, hygiene and emotional support matter during adolescence.
questions.answer: Because the body grows quickly during adolescence, the needs of the body increase. A balanced diet rich in proteins, vitamins and minerals, with enough water and regular exercise, provides the materials and energy for healthy growth. Personal hygiene becomes especially important, as the skin becomes more active; regular washing keeps the body clean and healthy. Adolescence also brings stronger and sometimes rapidly changing emotions, which is a normal part of growing up. Sharing feelings with trusted family members, teachers or friends, getting enough sleep, and staying active all help adolescents cope well and feel supported during this stage of change.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Adolescence A Stage of Growth and Change
questions.prompt: Two classmates of the same age look very different in height. Is this a problem? Explain.
questions.answer: No — adolescents grow and change at different ages and rates, so differences in height between peers are completely normal.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Adolescence A Stage of Growth and Change
questions.prompt: Why might an adolescent need more food than a young child?
questions.answer: Because the body is growing rapidly during adolescence, it needs extra nutrients and energy to support that growth.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Name the three modes of heat transfer.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: How does heat from the Sun reach us?
questions.answer: By radiation, which needs no medium.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain conduction, convection and radiation with an example of each.
questions.answer: Heat is transferred in three ways. Conduction is the flow of heat through a solid from its hotter part to its cooler part, without the material moving — for example, the handle of a metal spoon becomes warm when the other end is in hot tea. Convection is the transfer of heat in liquids and gases by the actual movement of the heated material: heated fluid becomes lighter and rises while cooler fluid sinks, as when water heats in a pan. Radiation is the transfer of heat without any material in between, which is how the Sun's heat reaches the Earth across empty space. Together, these three modes explain almost all the heating we observe in nature.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: How do the sea breeze and land breeze form?
questions.answer: Sea and land breezes are caused by convection on a large scale, because land and water heat up and cool down at different rates. During the day, the land heats up faster than the sea, so the warm air over the land rises and the cooler air from over the sea moves in to take its place — this cool wind blowing from sea to land is the sea breeze. At night, the land cools faster than the sea, so now the warmer air is over the water; air then moves from the land to the sea, producing the land breeze. These daily breezes are why coastal areas often feel pleasant.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why are cooking pots often given wooden or plastic handles?
questions.answer: Because wood and plastic are poor conductors (insulators), so the handle stays cool enough to hold while the pot heats by conduction.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does a room's ceiling feel warmer than its floor?
questions.answer: Warm air is lighter and rises by convection, collecting near the ceiling, while cooler, denser air stays low.

questions.subject: science

questions.answer: Motion covering equal distances in equal intervals of time.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is a pendulum useful in clocks?
questions.answer: Because each swing takes the same time, so it keeps regular time.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain speed and the difference between uniform and non-uniform motion, with examples.
questions.answer: Speed measures how fast an object moves and is found by dividing the distance travelled by the time taken: speed = distance ÷ time, measured in units like m/s or km/h. Motion is described as uniform when the object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time, so its speed stays the same — for example, a car moving steadily at 50 km/h. Motion is non-uniform when the speed changes from moment to moment, such as a bus speeding up and slowing down in city traffic. On a distance–time graph, uniform motion appears as a straight slanting line, while non-uniform motion gives a curved line.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: How is time measured, and how does a simple pendulum help?
questions.answer: Time is measured in units of seconds, minutes and hours, with the second as the basic unit. Since any regularly repeating event can be used to mark time, people have measured time using the Sun, sand timers and water clocks in the past. A simple pendulum — a small weight hanging from a fixed point — is especially useful because each complete to-and-fro swing, called an oscillation, takes the same amount of time. This steady, repeating motion allowed pendulums to be used in clocks to keep accurate time, illustrating the link between regular motion and the measurement of time.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Two cars cover the same distance, but one takes less time. Which is faster, and why?
questions.answer: The one taking less time, because for the same distance a shorter time means a higher speed (distance ÷ time).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: If a pendulum's swing always takes the same time, what happens to the number of swings in one minute if you do not change the pendulum?
questions.answer: It stays the same, since each swing takes a fixed time — that constancy is what makes it a good timekeeper.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe nutrition and digestion in humans.
questions.answer: Nutrition is the process by which animals take in food to obtain energy and materials for growth and repair. In humans, the food taken in must be broken down into simpler substances before the body can use it, and this breakdown is called digestion. Food travels through the digestive tract — from the mouth, down the food pipe, into the stomach, and then into the intestines — where digestive juices act on it at each stage. The simple substances produced are absorbed into the blood, mainly in the intestine, and carried to all parts of the body, while the undigested material is removed. In this way the body gets the nutrients it needs from food.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain respiration, transport and excretion as life processes in animals.
questions.answer: Three life processes work together to keep an animal's body running. Respiration is the process in which cells use oxygen to release energy from digested food, producing carbon dioxide; we breathe in to supply oxygen through the lungs and breathe out to remove carbon dioxide. Transport, carried out by the circulatory system, uses blood pumped by the heart through blood vessels to carry oxygen and digested food to every part of the body and to bring wastes back. Excretion removes the wastes the body produces — in humans the kidneys filter wastes from the blood to form urine, and carbon dioxide leaves through the lungs. Together, these processes supply energy, deliver materials and keep the body clean.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does the body need both a transport system and a respiratory system?
questions.answer: Breathing supplies oxygen, but the blood transport system is needed to carry that oxygen to every cell and bring carbon dioxide back to be removed.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: If the kidneys stopped working, why would the body be harmed?
questions.answer: Wastes would build up in the blood instead of being removed as urine, making the internal environment harmful.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is photosynthesis?
questions.answer: The process by which green plants make food using sunlight, carbon dioxide and water.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What does xylem carry?
questions.answer: Water and dissolved minerals, upward from the roots.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is transpiration?
questions.answer: The loss of water as vapour from leaves through stomata.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain photosynthesis, including its raw materials and products.
questions.answer: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make their own food. It takes place mainly in the leaves, where the green pigment chlorophyll captures energy from sunlight. Using this energy, the plant combines carbon dioxide taken from the air through the stomata with water absorbed from the soil by the roots, producing food in the form of a sugar and releasing oxygen as a by-product. The raw materials are therefore sunlight, carbon dioxide and water, and the products are food and oxygen. Because of this, green plants are the original source of food and oxygen for almost all living things.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe how materials are transported in a plant and the role of transpiration.
questions.answer: A plant moves materials using two transport tissues. Xylem carries water and dissolved minerals upward from the roots to the leaves, while phloem carries the food made in the leaves to all other parts of the plant, including the roots. Transpiration, the loss of water as vapour from the leaves through tiny pores called stomata, plays an important role in this transport: as water evaporates from the leaves, it helps pull more water up through the xylem from the roots, rather like sucking through a straw. Transpiration also cools the plant. Because a plant can lose a lot of water this way, especially on hot days, it needs a steady supply of water from the soil.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is the leaf called the food factory of the plant?
questions.answer: Because leaves contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis, making the plant's food using sunlight, carbon dioxide and water.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why might a plant wilt on a very hot day even with some water in the soil?
questions.answer: Because transpiration removes water faster than the roots can replace it, so the plant loses water and wilts.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain how shadows are formed and what they tell us about light.
questions.answer: A shadow is the dark region formed behind an opaque object when it blocks the path of light. Because light travels in straight lines, it cannot bend around the object, so the region directly behind it receives no light and appears dark. A shadow shows only the outline or shape of the object, not its colour or surface detail, which is why shadows of differently coloured objects of the same shape look alike. The size and direction of a shadow change with the position of the light source — a light source close to the object or low down produces a longer shadow. Shadows are thus direct evidence that light travels in straight lines.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe the image formed by a plane mirror.
questions.answer: When light falls on the smooth, shiny surface of a plane mirror it is reflected, and this reflected light forms an image. The image in a plane mirror has several definite features: it is upright (the same way up as the object), it is the same size as the object, and it appears to be as far behind the mirror as the object is in front of it. The image is also laterally inverted, meaning left and right are swapped — this is why the word written on the front of an ambulance appears reversed except when seen in a mirror. Such an image cannot be caught on a screen, so it is called a virtual image.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does your shadow become longer in the early morning and evening?
questions.answer: Because the Sun is low in the sky then, so light strikes at a slant and the opaque body blocks a longer region of light.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is the word 'AMBULANCE' often written reversed on the front of the vehicle?
questions.answer: So that drivers ahead see it the right way round in their rear-view mirror, because a plane mirror laterally inverts the image.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What causes day and night?
questions.answer: The rotation of the Earth on its axis.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does the Moon have phases?
questions.answer: Because we see different amounts of its sunlit half as it orbits the Earth.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is a solar eclipse?
questions.answer: When the Moon comes between the Sun and Earth and blocks the Sun's light.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain how the rotation and revolution of the Earth cause day, night and the seasons.
questions.answer: The Earth has two main motions. It spins on its axis — an imaginary line through its poles — and this rotation takes about 24 hours; as it spins, the half facing the Sun experiences day while the half facing away experiences night, so rotation causes day and night. The Earth also moves around the Sun in an orbit, completing one revolution in about a year. Because the Earth's axis is tilted, different parts of the Earth receive the Sun's rays more directly at different points in this orbit. When a region is tilted towards the Sun it gets more direct sunlight and has summer; when tilted away it has winter. So the seasons arise from the Earth's revolution combined with the tilt of its axis.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe how solar and lunar eclipses occur, and why eye safety matters.
questions.answer: An eclipse occurs when the shadow of one heavenly body falls on another, and this depends on the Sun, Earth and Moon lining up. In a solar eclipse, the Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth, so the Moon's shadow falls on part of the Earth and the Sun is blocked from view for a short time during the day. In a lunar eclipse, the Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon, so the Earth's shadow falls on the Moon and the Moon darkens. During a solar eclipse one must never look directly at the Sun, even when it is mostly covered, because its light can permanently damage the eyes; safe viewing methods or special filters should always be used.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: If the Earth did not rotate, what would happen to day and night?
questions.answer: One side would have continuous day and the other continuous night, instead of the regular cycle we experience.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why do we always see nearly the same face of the Moon?
questions.answer: Because the Moon takes the same time to rotate once as it does to revolve once around the Earth, so the same side faces us.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: How can you tell a number is not a perfect square from its last digit?

questions.subject: maths

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: How do you find a cube root by prime factorisation?
questions.answer: Factor into primes, group equal factors in threes, and take one factor from each group.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Find the square root of 1764 by prime factorisation, showing each step.
questions.answer: 1764 = 2 × 2 × 3 × 3 × 7 × 7. Make pairs: (2×2), (3×3), (7×7). Take one number from each pair: 2 × 3 × 7 = 42. Therefore √1764 = 42.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain why 243 is not a perfect cube, then find the smallest number to multiply it by to make one.
questions.answer: 243 = 3⁵ = (3×3×3) × 3 × 3. One complete triple gives a 3, but two 3s are left over, so 243 is not a perfect cube. Multiplying by 3 makes 3⁶ = 729 = 9³, a perfect cube; so the smallest multiplier is 3.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Without computing, explain why 768 cannot be a perfect square.
questions.answer: 768 = 2⁸ × 3 = (pairs of 2) × 3. The single 3 has no pair, so the factorisation cannot be split fully into equal pairs; hence 768 is not a perfect square.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A square garden has area 529 m². Find its side and explain.
questions.answer: The side is √529. Since 23² = 529, the side is 23 m — the side of a square equals the square root of its area.

questions.answer: For the same base, aᵐ × aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What does a negative exponent mean?
questions.answer: a⁻ⁿ is the reciprocal of aⁿ, i.e. a⁻ⁿ = 1 ÷ aⁿ.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why do we use standard form?
questions.answer: To write very large or very small numbers compactly as k × 10ⁿ with 1 ≤ k < 10.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: State the laws of exponents for a common base and show one example of each.
questions.answer: Product: aᵐ × aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ (2² × 2³ = 2⁵). Quotient: aᵐ ÷ aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ (5⁴ ÷ 5² = 5²). Power of a power: (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ ((3²)² = 3⁴). Power of a product: aᵐ × bᵐ = (ab)ᵐ (2³ × 5³ = 10³). Each rule lets us simplify without fully expanding.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain how to write 0.00000305 in standard form, step by step.
questions.answer: Place the decimal after the first non-zero digit to get 3.05. Count how many places the decimal moved: 6 places to the right, so the power of ten is −6. Therefore 0.00000305 = 3.05 × 10⁻⁶, with k = 3.05 satisfying 1 ≤ k < 10.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Without a calculator, which is larger: 2¹⁰ or 10³? Justify.
questions.answer: 2¹⁰ = 1024 while 10³ = 1000, so 2¹⁰ is larger by 24. Comparing powers sometimes needs evaluating, not just looking at the exponents.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Show that aᵐ ÷ aᵐ supports the rule a⁰ = 1.
questions.answer: By the quotient law, aᵐ ÷ aᵐ = aᵐ⁻ᵐ = a⁰. But any non-zero number divided by itself is 1, so a⁰ must equal 1.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What is the difference between place value and face value?
questions.answer: Face value is the digit itself; place value is the digit multiplied by the value of its position.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain how the Roman number system works, with examples and a limitation.
questions.answer: Roman numerals use symbols I, V, X, L, C, D and M with fixed values, combined mainly by addition: XV = 10 + 5 = 15. When a smaller symbol is placed before a larger one it is subtracted: IV = 4, IX = 9, XL = 40. A limitation is that there is no zero and no place value, so writing and calculating with very large numbers is awkward compared with the decimal system.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Describe how the decimal place-value system represents numbers and why it is powerful.
questions.answer: The decimal system uses ten digits (0–9) and gives each position a value ten times the one to its right: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, and so on. A number like 4072 means 4×1000 + 0×100 + 7×10 + 2×1, where zero holds the empty hundreds place. Because only ten symbols and their positions encode any number, arithmetic operations like addition and multiplication follow simple, regular rules — which is why this system spread worldwide.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why is 2025 easier to write in decimal than in a purely additive system?
questions.answer: In decimal, place value lets four digits capture the whole value; an additive system would need many repeated symbols, making it long and error-prone.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: If we grouped in twos instead of tens, how would counting change?
questions.answer: We would use base two, where each place is twice the previous one and only the digits 0 and 1 appear; the same number would be written with more digits but the place-value idea stays the same.

questions.answer: They bisect each other (cross at their midpoints).

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What makes a square different from a rectangle?
questions.answer: A square has all four sides equal, while a rectangle only needs opposite sides equal.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: List the properties of a parallelogram and name its three special cases.
questions.answer: In a parallelogram: opposite sides are equal and parallel, opposite angles are equal, adjacent angles are supplementary, and the diagonals bisect each other. Its special cases are the rectangle (all right angles, equal diagonals), the rhombus (all sides equal, diagonals perpendicular), and the square (both — equal sides, right angles, equal perpendicular-bisecting diagonals).

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Show, using triangles, why a quadrilateral's angles sum to 360°.
questions.answer: Draw one diagonal of the quadrilateral. It divides the figure into two triangles. The angles of each triangle sum to 180°, and together these angles make up all four angles of the quadrilateral, so the total is 180° + 180° = 360°.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Can a quadrilateral have three obtuse angles? Explain.
questions.answer: Yes. Three angles just over 90° (say 100° each = 300°) leave 60° for the fourth, still summing to 360°. It cannot have four obtuse angles, since that would exceed 360°.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: The angles of a quadrilateral are in the ratio 1:2:3:4. Find them.
questions.answer: Let the parts be x, 2x, 3x, 4x. Their sum 10x = 360°, so x = 36°. The angles are 36°, 72°, 108° and 144°.

questions.explanation: A prime has exactly two factors: 1 and itself.

questions.prompt: State the divisibility rule for 9.
questions.answer: A number is divisible by 9 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 9.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What is the difference between a factor and a multiple?
questions.answer: A factor divides the number exactly; a multiple is obtained by multiplying the number by a whole number.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Write the prime factorisation of 60.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain how to find the HCF and LCM of 12 and 18 by prime factorisation.
questions.answer: Write each as primes: 12 = 2×2×3 and 18 = 2×3×3. For the HCF, take the common prime factors with the lowest powers: one 2 and one 3, giving HCF = 6. For the LCM, take all prime factors with the highest powers: 2×2 from 12 and 3×3 from 18, giving LCM = 2×2×3×3 = 36. Check: HCF × LCM = 6 × 36 = 216 = 12 × 18.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: List the divisibility rules for 2, 3, 4, 5, 9 and 10 with an example each.
questions.answer: Divisible by 2 if it ends in an even digit (e.g. 48). By 5 if it ends in 0 or 5 (e.g. 65). By 10 if it ends in 0 (e.g. 120). By 3 if the digit sum is divisible by 3 (e.g. 51, since 5+1=6). By 9 if the digit sum is divisible by 9 (e.g. 153, since 1+5+3=9). By 4 if the last two digits form a multiple of 4 (e.g. 316, since 16 is divisible by 4).

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A number is divisible by both 2 and 3. What other number must divide it, and why?
questions.answer: It must be divisible by 6, because 6 = 2 × 3 and the number contains both factors.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why does every composite number have at least one prime factor?
questions.answer: A composite number can be broken into smaller factors repeatedly; this process stops at primes, so the original number is built from prime factors.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why does the area model explain distribution?
questions.answer: A rectangle of width a and length (b+c) splits into areas a×b and a×c that add to a(b+c).

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Show how the distributive property makes 23 × 7 easy, and explain the area model behind it.
questions.answer: Write 23 as 20 + 3, so 23 × 7 = (20 + 3) × 7 = 20×7 + 3×7 = 140 + 21 = 161. The area model pictures a rectangle 7 units wide and 23 units long; splitting the length into 20 and 3 gives two rectangles of areas 140 and 21, whose sum is the whole area 161 — exactly what distribution computes.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Multiply (x + 5)(x + 2) step by step and state the rule used.
questions.answer: Using the distributive property twice, each term of the first bracket multiplies each term of the second: x×x = x², x×2 = 2x, 5×x = 5x, 5×2 = 10. Adding gives x² + 2x + 5x + 10 = x² + 7x + 10. The rule is (x + a)(x + b) = x² + (a+b)x + ab.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain why 99 × 47 can be found as 47 × 100 − 47.
questions.answer: Since 99 = 100 − 1, distribution gives 99 × 47 = (100 − 1) × 47 = 4700 − 47 = 4653, turning a hard product into an easy subtraction.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A square of side (x + 3) has what area, and how does distribution show it?
questions.answer: Its area is (x + 3)(x + 3) = x² + 3x + 3x + 9 = x² + 6x + 9; distribution multiplies each term of one factor by each term of the other.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Define direct proportion.
questions.answer: Two quantities are in direct proportion if they change together so that their ratio stays constant.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain the unitary method using the cost of notebooks, with a worked example.
questions.answer: The unitary method first finds the value of a single unit, then scales up. For example, if 6 notebooks cost ₹90, one notebook costs ₹90 ÷ 6 = ₹15. To find the cost of 10 notebooks, multiply: ₹15 × 10 = ₹150. By going through the value of one unit, we can find the cost of any number of notebooks reliably.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What does it mean for two quantities to be in direct proportion? Give the rule and an example.
questions.answer: Two quantities are in direct proportion when an increase in one causes a proportional increase in the other, keeping their ratio constant: x₁/y₁ = x₂/y₂. For instance, if a car uses 4 litres of petrol for 60 km, then for 90 km the petrol needed satisfies 4/60 = x/90, giving x = 6 litres. Doubling the distance would double the petrol used.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A map uses 1 cm to represent 5 km. How far apart in reality are two towns 7 cm apart on the map?
questions.answer: Using direct proportion, 1 cm → 5 km, so 7 cm → 7 × 5 = 35 km.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: If the ratio of boys to girls in a class is 3 : 2 and there are 18 boys, how many girls are there?
questions.answer: 3 parts = 18, so 1 part = 6, and girls = 2 parts = 12.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: How does compound interest differ from simple interest?
questions.answer: Compound interest is charged on the principal plus interest already added, while simple interest is only on the original principal.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain how fractions, decimals and percentages are connected, with an example.
questions.answer: They are three forms of the same value. To convert a fraction to a decimal, divide the numerator by the denominator; to turn a decimal into a percentage, multiply by 100; and to write a percentage as a fraction, place it over 100 and simplify. For example, 3/4 = 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75, and 0.75 × 100 = 75%, while 75% = 75/100 = 3/4. Recognising these 'disguises' lets us pick whichever form is easiest for a problem.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Find the compound interest on ₹5000 at 10% per annum for 2 years, and compare it with the simple interest.
questions.answer: For compound interest, the amount A = P(1 + R/100)ⁿ = 5000(1 + 10/100)² = 5000 × 1.1 × 1.1 = ₹6050, so the compound interest is 6050 − 5000 = ₹1050. The simple interest would be SI = (5000 × 10 × 2)/100 = ₹1000. Compound interest (₹1050) is ₹50 more than simple interest, because in the second year interest is also earned on the first year's interest.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Which is a better score: 18 out of 25 or 70%? Show your reasoning using percentages.
questions.answer: 18 out of 25 = (18/25) × 100 = 72%. Since 72% is greater than 70%, the score of 18 out of 25 is better — percentages let us compare across different totals.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why does compound interest exceed simple interest after the first year?
questions.answer: After the first year, compound interest is charged on the principal plus the interest already added, so it earns 'interest on interest', while simple interest is always on the original principal alone.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A ladder 13 m long leans against a wall with its foot 5 m from the wall. How high does it reach? Show the working.
questions.answer: The ladder is the hypotenuse (13 m) and the distance from the wall is one leg (5 m). The height reached is the other leg: height = √(13² − 5²) = √(169 − 25) = √144 = 12 m. So the ladder reaches 12 m up the wall.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain how to test whether a triangle is right-angled, using sides 7, 24, 25.
questions.answer: Identify the longest side as the possible hypotenuse (25). Square the two shorter sides and add: 7² + 24² = 49 + 576 = 625. Compare with the square of the longest side: 25² = 625. Since 7² + 24² = 25², the converse of the theorem tells us the triangle is right-angled.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Two sides of a right triangle are 9 and 12. Could the third side be 15 or could it be something else?
questions.answer: If 9 and 12 are the legs, the hypotenuse is √(81+144) = 15. But if 12 were the hypotenuse and 9 a leg, the other leg would be √(144−81) = √63 ≈ 7.9, so the answer depends on which side is the hypotenuse.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why is the hypotenuse always the longest side of a right triangle?
questions.answer: It is opposite the largest angle (the 90° angle), and in any triangle the longest side lies opposite the largest angle.

questions.subject: maths

questions.explanation: Speed and time are inversely proportional, so time halves.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What is inverse proportion?
questions.answer: A relation in which one quantity increases as the other decreases, keeping their product constant.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: How is inverse proportion different from direct proportion?
questions.answer: In direct proportion the ratio is constant; in inverse proportion the product is constant.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why are speed and time inversely proportional for a fixed distance?
questions.answer: Because their product is the fixed distance, so going faster takes less time.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain inverse proportion using workers and days, and give the rule.
questions.answer: Two quantities are in inverse proportion when increasing one decreases the other so that their product stays constant: x1 x y1 = x2 x y2. For a fixed job, more workers finish it in fewer days. If 10 workers take 6 days (product 60), then 15 workers take 60 / 15 = 4 days, since the total work (person-days) is unchanged. The product, not the ratio, stays the same.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: How can you decide whether two quantities are in direct or inverse proportion? Give an example of each.
questions.answer: Check what happens as one quantity increases. If the other increases too and their ratio stays constant, it is direct proportion - for example, the cost of rice rises in step with the quantity bought. If the other decreases and their product stays constant, it is inverse proportion - for example, the more workers there are on a fixed job, the fewer days it takes. Identifying the type tells you whether to keep the ratio or the product constant when solving.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Food stored for 20 soldiers lasts 30 days. If 10 more soldiers join, how long will it last?
questions.answer: Inverse proportion: 20 x 30 = 30 x T, so T = 600 / 30 = 20 days.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why does a job with twice as many workers not always take exactly half the time in real life?
questions.answer: Inverse proportion assumes every worker is equally efficient and work shares perfectly; in reality coordination, space and fatigue can change individual output, so the ideal halving is only approximate.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What is a net?
questions.answer: A flat figure that can be folded to form a solid.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Describe the faces, edges and vertices of a cube and a square pyramid, and check Euler's relation for each.
questions.answer: A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices: F + V − E = 6 + 8 − 12 = 2. A square pyramid has a square base and four triangular faces, giving 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices: F + V − E = 5 + 5 − 8 = 2. Both satisfy Euler's relation, which holds for every convex polyhedron.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain how nets and views help us understand solids, with examples.
questions.answer: A net is the solid 'unfolded' into a flat shape; folding it rebuilds the solid. For example, six squares joined in a cross fold into a cube, and a square with four triangles folds into a square pyramid. Views describe a solid as seen from different directions — top, front and side — so a cylinder seen from the top looks like a circle but from the front like a rectangle. Nets and views let us represent three-dimensional objects on flat paper.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A solid has 8 faces and 12 vertices. How many edges does it have?
questions.answer: By Euler's relation, 8 + 12 − E = 2, so E = 18 edges.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why can the same cube be drawn as different two-dimensional views?
questions.answer: Because each view shows only one direction; the top, front and side reveal different faces, so several flat pictures together describe the single solid.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: When can a network be traced in a single stroke?
questions.answer: When it has zero or two vertices of odd degree.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain why the sum of the degrees of a graph is always twice the number of edges.
questions.answer: Every edge joins exactly two vertices, so when we count the degree at each vertex, that edge is counted once at each of its two ends — a total of two. Adding the degrees of all vertices therefore counts every edge twice, giving sum of degrees = 2 × number of edges. A direct consequence is that the number of odd-degree vertices must be even, since the total of all degrees is an even number.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Describe the rule for tracing a network in one stroke, with an example.
questions.answer: A connected network can be drawn in one continuous stroke, without lifting the pen or repeating an edge, exactly when it has either zero or two vertices of odd degree. If there are no odd vertices, the trace can start anywhere and returns to the start; if there are two, the trace must start at one odd vertex and finish at the other. For example, a single square (each corner has degree 2, all even) can be traced in one stroke, but a network with four odd vertices cannot.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A network has degrees 4, 4, 3 and 1. Can it be traced in one stroke? Why?
questions.answer: It has exactly two odd-degree vertices (3 and 1), so yes — it can be traced in one stroke, starting at one odd vertex and ending at the other.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why is it impossible for a graph to have exactly three odd-degree vertices?
questions.answer: Because the number of odd-degree vertices must be even (the degrees sum to twice the edges), so three is impossible.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What are like terms?
questions.answer: Terms with exactly the same variable part, such as 4x and 9x, which can be combined.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: State the three standard identities and use one to evaluate 98² quickly.
questions.answer: The identities are (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b², (a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b², and (a + b)(a − b) = a² − b². To find 98², write 98 = 100 − 2 and use the second identity: 98² = (100 − 2)² = 100² − 2·100·2 + 2² = 10000 − 400 + 4 = 9604.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Explain factorisation and factorise (i) 6x + 9 and (ii) x² − 16.
questions.answer: Factorisation rewrites an expression as a product of factors, reversing expansion. (i) 6x + 9 has a common factor 3, so 6x + 9 = 3(2x + 3). (ii) x² − 16 is a difference of squares, x² − 4², which factorises using (a + b)(a − b) as (x + 4)(x − 4). Checking by expanding returns the original expression.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Without multiplying directly, find 53 × 47 using an identity.
questions.answer: Write as (50 + 3)(50 − 3) = 50² − 3² = 2500 − 9 = 2491, using the difference-of-squares identity.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why is (a + b)² not equal to a² + b²?
questions.answer: Expanding (a + b)² gives a² + 2ab + b²; the extra term 2ab comes from multiplying the two parts together, so it cannot be dropped.

questions.subject: maths

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Write the formula for the area of a trapezium.
questions.answer: Area = ½ × (sum of parallel sides) × height = ½ × (a + b) × h.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: How do you find the area of a rhombus from its diagonals?
questions.answer: Area = ½ × d₁ × d₂, half the product of the diagonals.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why must the height be perpendicular to the base?
questions.answer: Because area measures the surface covered straight across, a slanted length would overstate it.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Derive the area of a trapezium by splitting it into triangles.
questions.answer: Draw a diagonal of the trapezium; it divides the shape into two triangles with the same height h (the distance between the parallel sides). One triangle has base a and the other base b, so the total area = ½×a×h + ½×b×h = ½×(a + b)×h. This is the trapezium area formula.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A field is shaped like a quadrilateral. Explain how to find its area using one diagonal.
questions.answer: Draw a diagonal joining two opposite corners, splitting the field into two triangles. Measure the diagonal as the common base, and the perpendicular distances (offsets) from the other two vertices to this diagonal. Each triangle's area is ½ × diagonal × its offset; add the two areas to get the field's total area.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Two parallelograms have equal areas but different shapes. What must be true?
questions.answer: Their base × height products are equal, even if the individual bases and heights differ — e.g. base 8, height 3 and base 6, height 4 both give area 24.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: If a triangle and a parallelogram share the same base and height, how do their areas compare?
questions.answer: The triangle's area is exactly half the parallelogram's, since the triangle is half of a parallelogram on the same base and height.

questions.explanation: Investigations start from observation and curiosity.

questions.explanation: Changing one variable at a time keeps the test fair.

questions.explanation: Curiosity and openness define a scientific attitude.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring the Investigative World of Science
questions.prompt: What is a hypothesis?
questions.answer: A testable possible explanation for something we observe.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring the Investigative World of Science
questions.prompt: How is an observation different from an inference?
questions.answer: An observation is noticed directly; an inference is reasoned out from observations.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring the Investigative World of Science
questions.prompt: Why change only one variable in an experiment?
questions.answer: So we can be sure which factor caused any change — a fair test.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring the Investigative World of Science
questions.prompt: Describe the steps a scientist follows in an investigation, with an example.
questions.answer: A scientist first observes something, for example that plants near a window grow taller. Next comes a question: does more light make plants grow taller? Then a hypothesis: 'plants given more light grow taller'. The scientist designs a fair experiment, growing identical plants with different amounts of light while keeping water, soil and temperature the same, records the heights as data, and finally draws a conclusion from the evidence. If the data do not support the hypothesis, it is revised and tested again.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring the Investigative World of Science
questions.prompt: Explain what a fair test is and why repeating results matters.
questions.answer: A fair test changes only one factor (the variable being studied) while keeping all other conditions the same, so any difference in the outcome can be linked to that one factor. For instance, to test the effect of fertiliser, only the fertiliser amount changes. Repeating the test, and getting similar results each time, shows the findings are reliable and not due to chance or a one-off error, which makes the conclusion trustworthy.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring the Investigative World of Science
questions.prompt: Two students get different results from the 'same' experiment. How should a scientist respond?
questions.answer: Check how each test was done, look for differences in conditions or errors, and repeat the experiment carefully; reliable conclusions need consistent, repeatable evidence.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Exploring the Investigative World of Science
questions.prompt: Why is 'many people believe it' not good scientific evidence?
questions.answer: Science depends on testable evidence from observation and experiment, not on popularity; widely held beliefs can still be wrong until tested.

questions.explanation: Microbes are used to make antibiotics.

questions.explanation: Decomposers recycle nutrients from dead matter.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Invisible Living World Beyond Our Naked Eye
questions.prompt: Name the main groups of microorganisms.
questions.answer: Bacteria, fungi, protozoa and microscopic algae, plus viruses as a special group.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Invisible Living World Beyond Our Naked Eye
questions.prompt: Give two useful roles of microbes.
questions.answer: Making curd and bread (fermentation) and fixing nitrogen in soil (also making medicines).

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Invisible Living World Beyond Our Naked Eye
questions.prompt: How does salting preserve food?
questions.answer: Salt draws out water and creates conditions in which spoilage microbes cannot grow.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Invisible Living World Beyond Our Naked Eye
questions.prompt: Describe four useful roles of microorganisms.
questions.answer: First, in food: yeast ferments dough to make bread rise and bacteria set milk into curd. Second, in medicine: microbes are used to produce antibiotics and to prepare vaccines. Third, in farming: certain soil bacteria fix atmospheric nitrogen into a form plants can use, improving soil fertility. Fourth, in nature: decomposer microbes break down dead plants and animals, returning nutrients to the soil and keeping ecosystems running.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Invisible Living World Beyond Our Naked Eye
questions.prompt: Explain how microbes can be harmful and how we control them.
questions.answer: Harmful microbes (pathogens) cause diseases such as the common cold and typhoid, and others spoil food, making it unsafe. We control them through personal and food hygiene, by preserving food using drying, salting, refrigeration, boiling or chemical preservatives that stop microbes growing, and by vaccination, which trains the body to resist specific diseases. Clean water and proper cooking also reduce infection.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Invisible Living World Beyond Our Naked Eye
questions.prompt: Why does milk spoil faster when left outside than in a refrigerator?
questions.answer: Warmth lets microbes multiply quickly and sour the milk, while cold in the fridge slows their growth, keeping milk fresh longer.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Invisible Living World Beyond Our Naked Eye
questions.prompt: How can the same group of microbes be both friend and foe?
questions.answer: Bacteria, for example, help make curd and fix nitrogen (friend) but can also cause disease or spoil food (foe), depending on the species and situation.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Health is best described as:
options: complete physical, mental and social wellbeing
questions.answer: complete physical, mental and social wellbeing
questions.explanation: Health covers physical, mental and social wellbeing.

questions.explanation: Proteins build and repair body tissues.

questions.explanation: Fibre aids digestion and prevents constipation.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which habit best prevents communicable disease?
options: washing hands and drinking safe water
questions.answer: washing hands and drinking safe water
questions.explanation: Hygiene and safe water cut infection.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A diet with the right amount of all nutrients is called:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Give one example each of a communicable and a non-communicable disease.
questions.answer: Communicable: malaria (or common cold). Non-communicable: diabetes (or high blood pressure).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is a balanced diet important?
questions.answer: It supplies all nutrients in the right amounts for energy, growth, repair and protection.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is immunity?
questions.answer: The body's ability to resist and fight disease-causing germs.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe the main nutrients in a balanced diet and one deficiency disease for two of them.
questions.answer: A balanced diet contains carbohydrates and fats for energy, proteins for growth and repair, vitamins and minerals for protection and proper body functioning, plus fibre and water. Deficiencies cause specific diseases: too little vitamin C causes scurvy (bleeding gums, weakness), and too little iron causes anaemia (tiredness, pale skin). Eating a variety of foods — grains, pulses, vegetables, fruits, milk — helps avoid such deficiencies.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Compare communicable and non-communicable diseases and explain how communicable ones can be prevented.
questions.answer: Communicable diseases are caused by microbes and spread from person to person through air, water, food or carriers such as mosquitoes; examples include the common cold, tuberculosis and malaria. Non-communicable diseases do not spread between people and are often linked to lifestyle or the body, such as diabetes and high blood pressure. Communicable diseases are prevented by hygiene (washing hands, clean food, safe water), keeping surroundings clean, controlling carriers like mosquitoes, and vaccination, which strengthens immunity against particular germs.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Two people eat the same amount of food, but one is better nourished. How is this possible?
questions.answer: Nourishment depends on balance, not just quantity; the better-nourished person eats a variety of foods supplying all nutrients, while the other may eat mostly one type and miss key nutrients.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does keeping surroundings clean reduce diseases like malaria and dengue?
questions.answer: Clean surroundings without stagnant water remove the breeding places of mosquitoes that carry these diseases, so fewer carriers means less spread.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: An electric current flows only when the circuit is:
questions.explanation: Current flows only in a closed (complete) circuit.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The glowing of a bulb's filament is due to the:
questions.explanation: The filament glows because of the heating effect of current.

questions.subject: science

questions.explanation: A fuse melts and breaks the circuit for safety.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A current-carrying wire can deflect a nearby:
questions.explanation: The magnetic effect of current deflects a compass needle.

questions.subject: science

questions.subject: science

questions.subject: science

questions.explanation: The bell uses an electromagnet — the magnetic effect.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: An electromagnet becomes stronger if we:
questions.explanation: More turns (or more current) strengthens it.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Heat produced in a wire is greater when the current is:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is the heating effect of electric current?
questions.answer: The production of heat when current passes through a wire, used in heaters, irons and bulb filaments.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: How does a fuse protect a circuit?
questions.answer: Its thin wire melts when the current is too high, breaking the circuit before damage occurs.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is an electromagnet?
questions.answer: A coil wound on an iron core that acts as a magnet only while current flows through it.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain the heating effect of electric current and give three uses.
questions.answer: When current flows through a wire, the wire's resistance converts electrical energy into heat, so it warms up — the heating effect. The heat is greater for higher current, higher resistance (thin wire) and longer time. Uses include electric heaters and irons (high-resistance heating elements), incandescent bulbs (a thin filament glows white-hot), and electric fuses (a thin wire melts to break a dangerously high current).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe the magnetic effect of current and how an electromagnet and electric bell use it.
questions.answer: A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it and can deflect a compass needle (Oersted's discovery). Winding the wire into a coil around an iron core makes an electromagnet, which is magnetic only while current flows and grows stronger with more turns or more current. In an electric bell, current through an electromagnet pulls an iron armature to strike the gong; this movement breaks the circuit, the electromagnet loses its magnetism, the armature springs back, the circuit closes again, and the cycle repeats to ring the bell.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is the heating element of a heater made of a coiled thin wire rather than a thick one?
questions.answer: A thin wire has high resistance, which produces more heat for the same current; coiling packs a long length into a small space to give plenty of heat.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: If you switch off the current to an electromagnet holding iron nails, what happens and why?
questions.answer: The nails fall off, because an electromagnet is magnetic only while current flows; with no current there is no magnetic field.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A force is best described as a:
questions.explanation: A force is a push or a pull on an object.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which of these is a contact force?
questions.explanation: Friction acts only when surfaces touch, so it is a contact force.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The force that pulls a dropped ball to the ground is:
questions.explanation: Gravity pulls objects toward the Earth.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A comb rubbed on hair picks up paper bits due to:
questions.explanation: Charges on the comb exert an electrostatic (non-contact) force.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Lifting a bag uses mainly:
questions.explanation: Our muscles apply muscular force, a contact force.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Pressing soft dough into a new shape shows that a force can:
options: change the shape of an object
questions.answer: change the shape of an object
questions.explanation: A force can change an object's shape.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Two equal and opposite forces on a still box are said to be:
questions.explanation: Equal and opposite forces are balanced; motion does not change.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: An object's direction of motion changes only if a force is:
questions.explanation: An unbalanced (net) force changes the state of motion.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which acts without any contact?
questions.explanation: Magnetic force acts at a distance, so it is non-contact.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A force has:
questions.explanation: A force is described by both its size and its direction.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Give one example each of a contact and a non-contact force.
questions.answer: Contact: friction (or muscular force). Non-contact: gravitational (or magnetic/electrostatic) force.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: List three effects a force can produce.
questions.answer: It can change an object's speed, its direction, or its shape (and can start or stop motion).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What are balanced forces?
questions.answer: Equal and opposite forces on an object whose net effect is zero, so the motion does not change.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain the difference between contact and non-contact forces with two examples of each.
questions.answer: Contact forces act only when objects touch: muscular force (lifting a bag) and friction (a ball slowing on the ground). Non-contact forces act from a distance: gravitational force (a falling apple) and magnetic force (a magnet attracting iron). Electrostatic force, as between a charged comb and paper, is also non-contact.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe how forces affect the motion and shape of objects, with examples.
questions.answer: An unbalanced force can start a still object moving (pushing a cart), stop a moving one (braking), change its speed (pedalling faster), or change its direction (hitting a ball sideways). A force can also change shape without moving the object much, such as squeezing clay or stretching a rubber band. Balanced forces, being equal and opposite, leave the motion unchanged.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: When you push a heavy almirah and it does not move, is a force acting? Explain.
questions.answer: Yes. You apply a force, but friction from the floor pushes back with an equal and opposite force, so the forces are balanced and there is no motion.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why can the same kick make a ball both speed up and change direction?
questions.answer: A force has direction, so applying it at an angle to the ball's motion changes both how fast it moves and which way it goes — two effects from one force.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Pressure is defined as force per unit:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: For the same force, pressure is greater when the area is:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The pressure exerted by the air above us is called:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Wind blows from a region of:
questions.explanation: Air moves from higher to lower pressure.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Where wind speed is high, the air pressure is:
questions.explanation: High wind speed is linked to low pressure.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A force of 60 N on 3 m² gives a pressure of:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: As we go higher above the ground, atmospheric pressure:
questions.explanation: Less air above means lower pressure at higher altitude.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A cyclone whirls around a centre of:
questions.explanation: A cyclone has a low-pressure centre.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Heavy school bags hurt less with wide straps because wide straps:
questions.explanation: More area lowers the pressure on the shoulder.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Air moves as wind mainly because of differences in:
questions.explanation: Pressure differences set air in motion.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Write the formula for pressure.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does a nail have a pointed tip?
questions.answer: A pointed tip has a tiny area, so it creates very high pressure and pierces easily.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: In which direction does wind blow?
questions.answer: From a region of higher air pressure to a region of lower air pressure.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain how winds are formed due to uneven heating.
questions.answer: The Sun heats land, water and the air unevenly. Warm air expands, becomes lighter and rises, creating a low-pressure region. Cooler, denser air at higher pressure then flows in to replace the rising air. This horizontal movement of air from high to low pressure is wind. On a large scale, the strong heating near the equator and weaker heating near the poles set up global wind patterns.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe how a cyclone forms and list safety measures.
questions.answer: Over warm seas, water evaporates and warm moist air rises rapidly, dropping the pressure near the surface. Surrounding air rushes in and starts to whirl, condensation releases heat that strengthens the rising, and a fast-spinning system of winds around a very low-pressure centre — a cyclone — develops, bringing violent winds, heavy rain and storm surges. Safety measures: follow weather warnings, store drinking water and food, move to a sturdy shelter or cyclone centre, avoid the coast and damaged wires, and do not venture out during the calm 'eye'.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why do passengers feel their ears 'pop' when a plane climbs?
questions.answer: Atmospheric pressure falls as altitude increases, so the air pressure outside the eardrum drops while inside stays higher; the eardrum bulges until the pressures equalise, which we feel as a pop.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A tin can collapses when air is sucked out of it. Explain.
questions.answer: Removing the inside air lowers the inner pressure, so the unbalanced higher atmospheric pressure outside pushes the walls in and crushes the can.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Matter is made up of:
questions.explanation: All matter is composed of very tiny particles.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The spreading of one substance's particles through another is called:
questions.explanation: This mixing due to particle motion is diffusion.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Diffusion is fastest in:
questions.explanation: Gas particles move most freely, so diffusion is fastest in gases.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Particles are most tightly packed in a:
questions.explanation: Solids have closely packed particles in fixed positions.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A substance that flows and takes the shape of its container but keeps its volume is a:
questions.explanation: Liquids flow and take the container's shape with fixed volume.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: On heating, the spaces between particles usually:
questions.explanation: Particles move faster and spread out, so spaces increase (expansion).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The change of a solid to a liquid on heating is:
questions.explanation: Solid to liquid on heating is melting.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Sugar dissolving 'into nothing' in water shows that:
questions.explanation: Sugar splits into particles small enough to fit between water particles.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Gas particles, compared to solid particles, are:
questions.explanation: Gas particles are far apart and move freely.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Particles of matter are in:
questions.explanation: Particles are always in motion, faster when heated.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Give one piece of evidence that matter is made of particles.
questions.answer: A few crystals of potassium permanganate colour a large amount of water, showing matter is made of countless tiny particles.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is diffusion faster in gases than in liquids?
questions.answer: Gas particles are farther apart and move much faster and more freely than liquid particles.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: How do particles in a solid differ from those in a gas?
questions.answer: Solid particles are closely packed in fixed positions; gas particles are far apart and move freely.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe the arrangement and movement of particles in the three states of matter.
questions.answer: In solids, particles are tightly packed in fixed positions with strong forces of attraction, so solids have a fixed shape and volume and only vibrate in place. In liquids, particles are close together but can slide over one another, giving a fixed volume but no fixed shape. In gases, particles are far apart with very weak forces and move rapidly in all directions, so gases have neither fixed shape nor fixed volume and fill their container.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain, using particles, why substances expand on heating and can change state.
questions.answer: Heating supplies energy that makes particles move faster, so the spaces between them increase and the substance expands. With enough heat the particles gain sufficient energy to overcome their forces of attraction: a solid's particles break free of their fixed positions and it melts to a liquid, and on further heating the liquid's particles escape into the air as a gas (boiling). Cooling removes energy and reverses these changes.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Two gas jars, one with a coloured gas, are placed mouth to mouth and opened. What happens and why?
questions.answer: The colour spreads into both jars because gas particles are in constant motion and diffuse into one another until evenly mixed.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why can we walk through air easily but not through a wall?
questions.answer: Air is a gas with particles far apart and weak forces, so we push through easily; a wall is a solid with closely packed particles held by strong forces, which resist being pushed apart.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Nature of Matter Elements Compounds and Mixtures
questions.prompt: What is a pure substance?
questions.answer: Matter made of only one kind of particle with a fixed composition, such as an element or a compound.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Nature of Matter Elements Compounds and Mixtures
questions.prompt: Name the three classes of elements.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Nature of Matter Elements Compounds and Mixtures
questions.prompt: How can a mixture be separated?
questions.answer: By physical methods such as filtration, evaporation, magnetic separation or hand-picking.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Nature of Matter Elements Compounds and Mixtures
questions.prompt: Compare compounds and mixtures under composition, properties and separation.
questions.answer: Composition: a compound has a fixed ratio of elements, while a mixture has a variable proportion. Properties: a compound shows new properties different from its elements, while a mixture's components keep their own properties. Separation: a compound can be broken down only by chemical methods, whereas a mixture can be separated by physical methods like filtration or evaporation. Example: water is a compound; salt water is a mixture.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Nature of Matter Elements Compounds and Mixtures
questions.prompt: Classify matter with examples and explain each class.
questions.answer: Matter is either a pure substance or a mixture. Pure substances are elements (one kind of atom that cannot be split chemically, e.g. oxygen, gold) and compounds (elements chemically combined in fixed ratio with new properties, e.g. water, common salt). Mixtures are physical blends: homogeneous mixtures are uniform (salt solution, air) and heterogeneous mixtures are non-uniform (sand in water). Elements are further grouped as metals, non-metals and metalloids.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Nature of Matter Elements Compounds and Mixtures
questions.prompt: Brass is made by mixing copper and zinc and looks uniform. Is it an element, compound or mixture? Explain.
questions.answer: Brass is a homogeneous mixture (an alloy): the metals are blended physically in varying proportions and keep their metallic properties, with no fixed chemical ratio, so it is not a compound.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: Nature of Matter Elements Compounds and Mixtures
questions.prompt: Why is air considered a mixture and not a compound, even though it looks uniform?
questions.answer: Air's gases are present in variable proportions, keep their own properties, and can be separated physically (for example by cooling and distillation), so air is a homogeneous mixture rather than a compound.

questions.explanation: A saturated solution cannot dissolve more, so extra solute settles.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Amazing World of Solutes Solvents and Solutions
questions.prompt: Define a solution.
questions.answer: A homogeneous mixture of a solute dissolved in a solvent.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Amazing World of Solutes Solvents and Solutions
questions.prompt: What is solubility?
questions.answer: The maximum amount of a solute that dissolves in a fixed amount of solvent at a given temperature.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Amazing World of Solutes Solvents and Solutions
questions.prompt: How does a colloid differ from a true solution?
questions.answer: A colloid has larger particles that scatter light (Tyndall effect) but do not settle, while a true solution is clear and does not scatter light.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Amazing World of Solutes Solvents and Solutions
questions.prompt: Explain dilute, concentrated, unsaturated and saturated solutions.
questions.answer: A dilute solution contains only a little solute, and a concentrated solution contains a large amount, so these terms compare how much solute is dissolved. At a fixed temperature, an unsaturated solution can still dissolve more solute, while a saturated solution has dissolved the maximum it can hold; any extra solute then settles. Heating a saturated solution can let it dissolve still more, since solubility usually rises with temperature.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Amazing World of Solutes Solvents and Solutions
questions.prompt: Compare true solutions, colloids and suspensions.
questions.answer: A true solution (salt in water) has very tiny particles, is clear, does not settle, and does not scatter light. A colloid (milk) has medium-sized particles that stay dispersed, do not settle, and scatter a light beam, showing the Tyndall effect. A suspension (chalk in water) has large, visible particles that settle on standing and is heterogeneous. So particle size increases from solution to colloid to suspension.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Amazing World of Solutes Solvents and Solutions
questions.prompt: A beam of light passes invisibly through salt water but is visible in milk. Explain.
questions.answer: Salt water is a true solution with particles too small to scatter light, so the beam is invisible; milk is a colloid whose larger particles scatter the light (Tyndall effect), making the beam visible.

questions.subject: science
questions.chapterTitle: The Amazing World of Solutes Solvents and Solutions
questions.prompt: On a hot day, a saturated sugar solution is cooled. What may you observe and why?
questions.answer: Sugar crystals may appear, because solubility falls as temperature drops, so the cooled solution can no longer hold all the dissolved sugar and the excess separates out.

questions.explanation: Convex mirrors give a wide, erect view.

questions.explanation: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection.
