questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does 'No Men Are Foreign' argue for the oneness of humanity?
questions.answer: The poem argues powerfully that all human beings are fundamentally one, and that no people are truly foreign or strange. The poet points out that, beneath the surface differences of uniforms, languages and lands, all people are essentially the same. They all breathe the same air, are warmed by the same sun, and live off the same earth, and when they die they will all lie in the same ground. They share the same human experiences — the same hunger, the same labour, the same joys and sorrows, and the same capacity for love. By stressing these shared realities again and again, the poet shows that the divisions between nations are superficial compared with the deep unity of the human family. The poem therefore insists that we should see all people as our brothers and sisters, not as strangers or enemies.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the message of the poem regarding war and hatred?
questions.answer: The poem carries a strong message against war and hatred. The poet warns that when we are taught to hate, fear or fight people of other countries — when we are told they are our enemies — we are really betraying and harming our own fellow human beings, who are part of the same human family. War and hatred, he suggests, are forms of self-destruction, because in attacking 'foreigners' we damage humanity itself and dirty the one earth that we all share. The poem appeals directly to the reader's conscience, urging us to remember our common humanity and to reject prejudice and violence. Its message is that peace, understanding and brotherhood among all peoples are essential, and that hatred of other nations only brings harm to everyone, including ourselves.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the poet say that hating others is really harming ourselves?
questions.answer: Because all people belong to one human family and share one earth, so hatred and war against others ultimately damage humanity as a whole, including the haters themselves.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How can remembering our shared humanity help promote peace?
questions.answer: By focusing on what unites us — the same needs, feelings and earth — rather than our differences, people are more likely to live in understanding and harmony rather than conflict.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: Because the tree heals and sprouts new shoots, growing back from a simple cut.

questions.prompt: What must be done to truly kill it?
questions.answer: Pull out its roots, expose them and let them dry up.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the theme of the poem?
questions.answer: The strength and resilience of nature and of life.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does 'On Killing a Tree' show the resilience of nature?
questions.answer: The poem shows the resilience of nature by describing how difficult it is to truly kill a tree. The poet explains that a tree has grown slowly over many years, drawing nourishment from the earth, air, water and sunlight, and has become strong and deeply rooted. Because of this, a mere cut or blow cannot destroy it: the tree is able to heal its wounds and send out fresh green shoots from its bark and from near the ground, growing back again as strong as before. The poet makes it clear that to actually kill the tree, one must do far more — its roots have to be pulled out completely from the earth, exposed, and then left to dry and wither in the sun until all the life within them is gone. By detailing how much effort is needed, the poem powerfully highlights the strength, endurance and resilience of nature and of living things.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the tone of the poem add to its effect?
questions.answer: The poem is written in a calm, matter-of-fact, almost clinical tone, as if giving careful instructions on how to kill a tree. This detached tone is striking because it contrasts sharply with the violent and disturbing nature of what is being described — the deliberate destruction of a living tree. By describing each step coolly, from the failure of a simple cut to the final uprooting and drying out of the roots, the poet draws the reader's attention to just how much force and persistence are needed to end the tree's tenacious life. This contrast between the calm manner and the violent subject makes the poem all the more powerful, and it quietly leads the reader to admire the tree's resilience and, perhaps, to feel uneasy about the destruction of such strong, enduring life in nature.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the poet focus on the roots to kill the tree?
questions.answer: Because the roots are the source of the tree's life and strength; as long as they remain in the earth, the tree can heal and regrow, so only by uprooting them can it truly be killed.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What might the poem suggest beyond the literal killing of a tree?
questions.answer: It can suggest how difficult it is to destroy anything that is deeply rooted and well established, whether in nature or in life, since such things have great strength to survive.

questions.answer: Death, loss and the permanence of nature.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: A Slumber Did My Spirit Seal
questions.prompt: How does 'A Slumber Did My Spirit Seal' contrast illusion and reality?
questions.answer: The poem is built on a powerful contrast between an earlier illusion and a later, painful reality, expressed in its two short stanzas. In the first stanza, the speaker recalls a time when his mind was in a dreamlike, untroubled state, free of any thought of death. He was so taken with the woman he loved that he believed she was beyond the reach of time, certain that she could never grow old or be affected by the passing years. In the second stanza, this comforting illusion is shattered by reality: the loved one has died. She now lies completely still and lifeless, unable to move or feel, and has become part of the earth, turning with it each day among the rocks, stones and trees. The movement from the speaker's false belief in her immortality to the truth of her death gives the poem its deep emotional force.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: A Slumber Did My Spirit Seal
questions.prompt: What does the poem suggest about death and nature?
questions.answer: The poem reflects thoughtfully on death and the permanence of nature. Through the death of his loved one, the speaker confronts the truth that death is inescapable, even for those we cherish and imagine to be untouchable by time. Once she has died, she becomes still and silent, no longer a living, feeling person, and she merges completely with the natural world, turning with the earth among the rocks, stones and trees. While this brings a quiet sense of grief and loss, the poem also conveys a sense of nature's enduring permanence: the earth and its cycle go on, and the dead become part of this eternal natural order. In this way, Wordsworth blends sorrow at human mortality with a calm acceptance of nature's lasting presence and its quiet, ceaseless rhythm.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: A Slumber Did My Spirit Seal
questions.prompt: Why is the speaker's earlier belief described as a 'slumber'?
questions.answer: Because, like sleep, it was a dreamlike, unaware state in which he ignored the reality of death, imagining his loved one to be immortal.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: A Slumber Did My Spirit Seal
questions.prompt: How does the loved one becoming 'one with the earth' affect the poem's mood?
questions.answer: It brings both grief and a calm sense of acceptance, suggesting that in death she has joined the eternal, peaceful cycle of nature.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where is the boy lost?
questions.answer: At a spring fair, near the roundabout.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the boy want before he was lost?
questions.answer: Toys, sweets, balloons, flowers and a roundabout ride.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does he want after being lost?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How do the child's wishes change after he is lost, and what does this reveal?
questions.answer: Before he is lost, the little boy is full of desire at the fair. He longs for toys, sweets, a balloon, garlands of flowers and especially a ride on the roundabout, asking his parents again and again for these things. But the moment he turns and finds his parents gone, his whole world changes. Overcome by fear and grief, he runs about crying for them. When a kind stranger lifts him up and offers him the very things he had wanted earlier — the roundabout ride, sweets, balloons, a garland — the child refuses every single one, sobbing only that he wants his mother and father. This dramatic change reveals a deep truth: a child's love for its parents is far greater than any material attraction. The toys and treats that seemed so important lose all their charm once the security of his parents is lost, showing that love and belonging matter more than anything money can buy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Mulk Raj Anand show the value of parental love in the story?
questions.answer: Mulk Raj Anand shows the value of parental love by contrasting the child's behaviour before and after he is separated from his parents. At first, the boy is so absorbed in the delights of the fair that he repeatedly pesters his parents for toys, sweets and a ride, even feeling a little resentful when they refuse. The author thus presents him as an ordinary child, drawn to worldly pleasures. But when the boy suddenly realises his parents are gone, he is gripped by terror and sorrow, and all his earlier desires vanish in an instant. The kind man who finds him offers him everything he had craved, yet the child rejects it all, weeping only for his mother and father. Through this powerful reversal, Anand makes clear that the love and security of one's parents are priceless — far more valuable than any toy, sweet or amusement — and that this love is what a child truly treasures above all.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the child refuse the very things he had begged for earlier?
questions.answer: Because once he loses his parents, fear and grief replace his desire for toys; the security of his parents matters far more than any object, so the things no longer appeal to him.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the story suggest to parents and children alike?
questions.answer: It suggests that love and togetherness are far more important than material things, reminding us to value family bonds above worldly attractions.

questions.answer: Because Toto was too mischievous and troublesome to keep.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe some of Toto's mischievous adventures.
questions.answer: Toto, the little monkey, fills the household with mischief from the start. Brought home secretly by grandfather, he tears clothes and curtains, breaks dishes, and torments the other animals in the private zoo. Though tied up to keep him out of trouble, he manages to escape and create chaos. On a train journey he is smuggled along hidden inside a kit-bag, from which he bursts out to the alarm of the other passengers. In one of the funniest episodes, on a cold day Toto climbs into a large pot of warm water meant for a bath; he enjoys soaking in it, dipping in and out, until the water becomes too hot, whereupon he leaps out. Through these and other antics, Toto proves himself a beautiful but utterly naughty pet, and his adventures keep the household — and the reader — thoroughly entertained.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does grandfather finally decide to part with Toto, and what does the story show?
questions.answer: Grandfather is fond of animals and is delighted to add the pretty little monkey Toto to his private zoo. However, Toto's endless mischief soon makes him impossible to keep. He damages clothes, curtains and crockery, troubles the other animals, escapes when tied, and causes disorder wherever he goes. Despite the family's affection for him, the trouble and expense he creates become too much to manage, and grandfather realises that Toto simply cannot be kept as a household pet. He therefore sells Toto back to the very tonga-driver from whom he had bought him. The story shows, in a humorous and affectionate way, that wild animals like monkeys — however charming and entertaining — are not really suited to life as domestic pets, because their natural mischief makes them too difficult to handle.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is keeping a monkey like Toto impractical despite his charm?
questions.answer: Because his natural restlessness and mischief lead him to damage things, trouble other animals and create constant disorder, making him too difficult and costly to manage at home.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Ruskin Bond make the story humorous?
questions.answer: By describing Toto's clever, naughty antics — escaping, hiding in a kit-bag, bathing in warm water — in a light, affectionate tone that turns the chaos into comedy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does Mahendra leave?
questions.answer: Because fear from Iswaran's ghost story overpowers him.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Iswaran's storytelling affect Mahendra?
questions.answer: Iswaran's storytelling has a powerful effect on Mahendra. As a junior supervisor who moves from one lonely site to another, Mahendra depends on his devoted cook Iswaran not only for meals but also for entertainment, and Iswaran fills his evenings with dramatic, suspenseful tales told with great exaggeration — such as his supposed taming of a mad elephant. These stories are so vivid that they grip Mahendra's imagination. The turning point comes when Iswaran narrates a chilling ghost story about a woman who haunts the area as a female ghost. The tale plants deep fear in Mahendra's mind. Later, when he wakes one moonlit night and sees Iswaran outside, his frightened imagination convinces him that the ghost is real. Unable to bear this fear, Mahendra finally decides to leave the place altogether. Thus Iswaran's storytelling, meant to entertain, ends up unsettling Mahendra so much that it drives him away.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the story tell us about the line between imagination and reality?
questions.answer: The story shows how easily the line between imagination and reality can blur when a skilled storyteller is at work. Iswaran narrates everything — even ordinary events — with such drama, detail and exaggeration that his tales feel completely real to his listener. His stories of adventure are thrilling, but it is his ghost story that has the most dangerous effect. Once the idea of a haunting female ghost is planted in Mahendra's mind, his imagination takes over. On a moonlit night, the sight of Iswaran outside is enough to convince the frightened Mahendra that the ghost truly exists, even though there is no proof of anything supernatural. His fear becomes so real to him that he decides to leave. Through this, R.K. Laxman shows, with gentle humour, that a powerful story and the suggestion it creates can overpower reason, making people believe and fear things that exist only in their imagination.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does Mahendra come to believe in the ghost despite having no proof?
questions.answer: Because Iswaran's vivid ghost story plants the idea deeply in his mind, and his frightened imagination then interprets ordinary sights, like Iswaran in the moonlight, as evidence of the ghost.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What makes Iswaran such an effective storyteller?
questions.answer: His dramatic delivery, rich detail, suspense and exaggeration make even ordinary events thrilling, drawing the listener completely into the tale.

questions.answer: Sleep by day and work by night, and everything costs one coin.

questions.answer: Because the stake fits only a fat man and he has grown fat.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the guru save him?
questions.answer: By tricking the king into taking his place with a tale of rebirth as a king.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How do the foolish king and minister turn the kingdom upside down?
questions.answer: The king and minister of this kingdom are both completely foolish, and they govern by ignoring all reason. They issue senseless decrees that overturn the natural order of life. First, they reverse day and night, ordering that everyone must sleep during the day and carry out all work at night, punishing those who do otherwise. Second, they fix the price of everything — no matter what it is or how valuable — at the same single coin, so that a measure of rice costs the same as anything else. These absurd rules create chaos and injustice, and they attract greedy people who come to enjoy the unnaturally cheap goods. The foolishness of the rulers eventually leads to a ridiculous chain of events around a thief's death, in which blame is passed senselessly from person to person until an innocent man is condemned. In this way, the rulers' lack of common sense throws the whole kingdom into disorder.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the guru's wisdom save the disciple, and what is the moral?
questions.answer: The guru's wisdom saves the disciple through clever presence of mind. After a thief dies and the foolish chain of blame ends with the demand for an execution, the disciple — who had grown fat on the kingdom's cheap food — is seized because the prepared stake fits only a fat man. In his danger, he secretly sends for his guru, who had earlier wisely left the senseless kingdom. The guru returns and outwits the foolish king with a simple trick: he and the disciple begin to quarrel over who should be executed, and the guru announces that whoever dies on the stake at that special, auspicious moment will be reborn as a king. Hearing this, the greedy king and his minister, eager to gain a throne, insist on being executed themselves, and so they perish in place of the disciple. The guru and disciple then take charge and restore order. The moral is clear: wisdom, common sense and quick thinking can overcome even the greatest foolishness.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why was the guru wise to leave the kingdom while the disciple was foolish to stay?
questions.answer: Because the guru saw that a place without reason was dangerous, while the disciple, tempted by cheap food, ignored the risk — and indeed nearly lost his life.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the guru use the king's own foolishness against him?
questions.answer: He exploits the king's greed by claiming the one executed will be reborn a king, so the foolish king eagerly takes the disciple's place, defeating himself through his own folly.

questions.answer: A statue covered in gold with jewels, standing above the city.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Whom does the Prince help?
questions.answer: Poor people — a seamstress, a writer, a match-girl and others.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What happens to the swallow?
questions.answer: It dies in the cold after helping the Prince.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How do the Happy Prince and the swallow help the poor of the city?
questions.answer: Though he was carefree in life, the Happy Prince, now a statue able to see the whole city, weeps at the suffering of the poor and resolves to help them, with the swallow as his messenger. First, he asks the swallow to take the ruby from his sword-hilt to a poor seamstress whose child is sick. Next, he sends a sapphire from his eye to a struggling young writer who is cold and hungry, and the other sapphire to a little match-girl who has lost her matches and is afraid to go home. Finally, having given away his jewels, the Prince asks the swallow to strip the gold leaf from his body, piece by piece, and carry it to the poor and the hungry children of the city. In this way the Prince sacrifices all his beauty and riches, and the faithful swallow, delaying its journey to Egypt to serve him, helps relieve the misery of many — until, weakened by the cold, it gives its own life.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the story show that true worth lies in love and sacrifice rather than in riches?
questions.answer: The story shows that real worth lies in love and sacrifice through the fate of the Prince and the swallow. While the Prince was beautiful and adorned with gold and jewels, his true greatness appears only when he gives all of it away to help the suffering poor. Stripped of his gold leaf and gems, the statue becomes shabby and is pulled down by the councillors, who see no value in it once its outward splendour is gone — judging only by appearances. The swallow, too, gives up its life out of love and loyalty. Yet when God asks an angel to bring Him the two most precious things in the city, the angel brings not gold or jewels but the Prince's broken leaden heart and the dead swallow, and God declares them the most precious of all, rewarding them in paradise. This ending makes the message clear: kindness, compassion and self-sacrifice are far more precious than wealth or beauty.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why do the councillors fail to see the statue's true value?
questions.answer: Because they judge only by outward appearance and riches; once the gold and jewels are gone, they cannot see the goodness and love the Prince showed, which is his real worth.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does God consider the leaden heart and the dead bird most precious?
questions.answer: Because they represent selfless love and sacrifice for others, which are the highest treasures — far above gold or jewels.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Prashant survive the cyclone and what does he find on returning home?
questions.answer: Prashant, a nineteen-year-old visiting a friend in Ersama, is caught in a devastating super-cyclone. As fierce winds and floodwaters sweep away houses, trees, people and animals, he survives the worst of it by clinging desperately to a tree. He then manages to take shelter, along with other survivors, on the roof of a strong building, where they endure several terrible days with little or no proper food while the floodwaters rage around them. When the storm finally subsides, Prashant sets out on a difficult and dangerous trek through a ruined landscape of mud, water and corpses to reach his own village, dreading that his family may have perished in the disaster. To his enormous relief, he finds his family alive. This survival and reunion mark the turning point at which Prashant, instead of being crushed by the horror around him, finds the strength to help others.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Prashant help his community recover after the cyclone?
questions.answer: After surviving the cyclone and finding his family alive, Prashant rises above his own shock and grief to become a leader in his community's recovery. Seeing the suffering of the countless survivors around him, he takes the initiative to organise relief. He arranges for food, such as rice, to be obtained and distributed to the starving people, and helps set up a shelter for those who have lost their homes. With great compassion, he comforts and cares for the many children orphaned by the storm and the women widowed by it, forming a group to look after the children's needs. He works to bring the shattered community together and to rebuild their lives, even helping orphaned children find a place within families rather than being left alone. Through his courage, energy and kindness, the young Prashant becomes a source of hope and healing for the devastated people of Ersama.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What qualities make Prashant a true leader after the disaster?
questions.answer: His courage, compassion, resourcefulness and selflessness — surviving terrible danger and then putting others' needs before his own to organise relief and rebuild the community.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is it significant that Prashant helps orphans stay within families?
questions.answer: Because it shows wisdom and care for the children's emotional well-being, keeping them in a loving community rather than isolating them in institutions.

questions.answer: That she will die when it falls.

questions.prompt: Why does the last leaf never fall?
questions.answer: Because Behrman painted it on the wall.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is Behrman's masterpiece?
questions.answer: The painted last leaf that saved Johnsy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the last leaf save Johnsy's life?
questions.answer: Johnsy, seriously ill with pneumonia, loses the will to recover and ties her fate to an old ivy creeper outside her window, believing she will die when its last leaf falls. As the leaves drop one by one in the autumn winds, her hope fades and she waits to die. Her friend Sue tries to encourage her, but Johnsy keeps her eyes on the dwindling vine. Then, after a fierce, stormy and freezing night, when the last leaf should surely have fallen, it is still there clinging to the wall — and it remains, day after day, through wind and rain. Seeing the leaf endure such harsh weather and refuse to give up, Johnsy is filled with new hope and is ashamed of her own wish to die. Her will to live returns, and she gradually recovers. The leaf's quiet courage thus rekindles Johnsy's spirit and saves her life — though the truth behind the leaf is even more remarkable.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Behrman's sacrifice show the greatness of selfless love?
questions.answer: Behrman's sacrifice is the heart of the story and a powerful example of selfless love. He is an old, poor and gruff artist who has spent his life dreaming of painting a masterpiece but has never succeeded. When he learns from Sue of Johnsy's strange and dangerous belief that she will die when the last ivy leaf falls, he is deeply moved. On a bitterly cold, stormy night, while the real leaves are being torn away, the old man goes out and paints a single, lifelike leaf on the wall so that Johnsy will always see a leaf clinging there and will not lose hope. His plan works: Johnsy, seeing the leaf survive, regains her will to live and recovers. But in painting the leaf through that freezing, wet night, Behrman himself catches pneumonia and dies. He gives his own life so that the young artist may live, and the painted leaf becomes the masterpiece he had always longed to create — a masterpiece born not of ambition but of love and sacrifice.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the painted leaf called Behrman's masterpiece?
questions.answer: Because it was so lifelike that it fooled everyone, and more importantly, it saved a life through love and sacrifice — making it his greatest and truest work of art.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does Johnsy's recovery tell us about the power of hope?
questions.answer: It shows that hope and the will to live can be as important as medicine in fighting illness; once Johnsy regained hope, her body began to heal.

questions.answer: That it is made of people and belonging, not just a building.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: A House Is Not a Home
questions.prompt: How does the fire change the narrator's understanding of 'home'?
questions.answer: Before the fire, the narrator thinks of home mainly as the house he lives in, full of his familiar belongings, and he is preoccupied with feeling lonely and out of place at his new, bigger school. The fire shatters this. In the panic he tries to save his cat, which runs off in fright and is lost, and he watches his house and all his possessions burn — losing not only objects but the sense of security and identity tied to them. At first he feels devastated and emptier than ever. But then his classmates and teachers, including those he hardly knew, surprise him with kindness, donating clothes, books and supplies and offering warmth and friendship. This support makes him feel, for the first time, that he truly belongs. Through this experience the narrator comes to understand that a home is not simply a building or a collection of things, but the people who care for you and the sense of belonging they create.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: A House Is Not a Home
questions.prompt: How does the kindness of others transform the narrator's experience of loss?
questions.answer: The kindness of others turns the narrator's experience of loss into one of belonging and warmth. After the fire destroys his home and his cat goes missing, he is left grieving and feeling more isolated than before, having already struggled to fit in at his new school. He expects to face this loss alone. Instead, his fellow students and teachers respond with remarkable generosity: they collect and donate clothes, books and school supplies for him, and even classmates he barely knew reach out with sympathy and support. This unexpected compassion deeply moves him and changes everything. Where he had felt like an outsider, he now feels accepted and cared for, finally sensing that he belongs in his school community. The return of his lost cat adds to this healing. Thus, the kindness of others does more than replace lost possessions — it heals his loneliness and teaches him that real home and belonging come from people who care.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: A House Is Not a Home
questions.prompt: Why does the narrator feel he belongs only after losing his house?
questions.answer: Because the kindness shown to him after the fire makes him feel cared for and accepted, showing him that belonging comes from people, not from a building or possessions.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: A House Is Not a Home
questions.prompt: How does the lost-and-returned cat mirror the narrator's journey?
questions.answer: Like the boy, the cat is lost and frightened after the fire but eventually finds its way back; its return mirrors his own movement from loss and isolation to comfort and belonging.

questions.answer: Because Lushkoff is a liar begging with false stories.

questions.answer: Chopping wood and other odd jobs.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who truly reformed Lushkoff and how?
questions.answer: Olga, the cook, through her compassion and kind words.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Sergei respond to Lushkoff's begging, and what does he offer instead of money?
questions.answer: When Lushkoff the beggar approaches Sergei, he claims to be a poor, educated man — a former teacher — who has been driven to begging by misfortune. Sergei, however, remembers having seen this same man before, telling an entirely different hard-luck story, and so he realises that Lushkoff is a liar who begs by deceiving people. Angered by this dishonesty, Sergei scolds him sharply for lying and begging instead of working. Yet rather than simply turning him away, Sergei decides to give Lushkoff a chance to earn an honest living: he offers him work, asking him to chop wood. Although Lushkoff is weak and clearly unaccustomed to physical labour, he agrees out of shame and sets to work, though he does the job poorly. Sergei continues to provide him with various odd jobs and eventually helps him find proper employment, giving the beggar a path away from his dishonest life.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who really brought about Lushkoff's transformation, and what does this reveal about the power of compassion?
questions.answer: Although Sergei believed that giving Lushkoff work had reformed him, the truth, revealed years later, is quite different. When Sergei meets Lushkoff again and finds him a respectable notary with a steady job, Lushkoff confesses that it was not really the wood-chopping that saved him. The real cause of his transformation was Sergei's cook, Olga. Whenever Lushkoff was given work to do, Olga would actually do most of the chopping and other tasks for him; but more importantly, she would scold him for his idleness and dishonesty while also showing him genuine pity and kindness. Her compassion and her caring words touched Lushkoff's heart deeply and awakened in him the desire to become a better man. This reveals that compassion has a greater power to change a person than charity or harsh punishment alone. It was not money, nor even the work itself, but Olga's quiet, heartfelt kindness that truly reformed Lushkoff's life.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why was Olga's kindness more effective than Sergei's scolding or charity?
questions.answer: Because her genuine compassion touched Lushkoff's heart and made him want to change from within, whereas scolding or money could only address his behaviour on the surface.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does Sergei's plan still 'work' even though he was wrong about the reason?
questions.answer: Because his offer of work brought Lushkoff into contact with Olga, whose compassion reformed him; Sergei's good intention set the change in motion, even if the true cause was different.

questions.prompt: What is subject-verb agreement?
questions.answer: The rule that a verb must match its subject in number (singular or plural).

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is reported speech?
questions.answer: Reporting what someone said without using their exact words.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: When do we use 'an'?
questions.answer: Before a word beginning with a vowel sound, e.g. 'an apple', 'an hour'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Explain reported speech and the main changes made when converting direct to indirect speech.
questions.answer: Reported, or indirect, speech is a way of telling what someone said without quoting their exact words, and it usually requires several changes. First, the tense of the reported words generally shifts 'one step back' when the reporting verb is in the past: present simple becomes past simple ('I am' becomes 'he was'), present perfect becomes past perfect, and 'will' becomes 'would'. Second, pronouns change to match the new speaker's point of view (for example, 'I' may become 'he' or 'she', and 'you' may become 'me' or 'them'). Third, words referring to time and place are adjusted — 'today' becomes 'that day', 'tomorrow' becomes 'the next day', 'here' becomes 'there', and so on. The punctuation also changes: quotation marks are removed and, for statements, the word 'that' is often used. For questions, the reported clause takes the form of a statement, and reporting verbs like 'asked' are used. Applying these changes carefully turns direct speech into correct reported speech.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe the main writing formats studied in Class 9 and what each requires.
questions.answer: Class 9 English develops several writing formats, each with its own structure and tone. A paragraph is a short, focused piece on a single topic, with a clear opening sentence, supporting details and a closing line. Descriptive writing paints a vivid picture of a person, place or thing using sensory details, while narrative writing tells a story with a beginning, middle and end. A diary entry is personal and informal, written in the first person with the date, expressing one's thoughts and feelings about an event. Letters are of two main kinds: formal letters (such as applications or complaints) follow a fixed layout with sender's and receiver's addresses, date, salutation, a clear subject, body and formal closing, and use polite, businesslike language; informal letters (to friends or family) are warmer and more relaxed in tone. Notices are short, factual announcements written in a box with a heading, date and clear details. Following the correct format, layout and tone for each type is essential for scoring well and communicating clearly.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the verb change with subjects like 'each' and 'neither' even though they may refer to more than one thing?
questions.answer: Because grammatically these words are treated as singular — they focus on individuals one at a time — so they take a singular verb, regardless of how many people or things are involved.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why do time words like 'tomorrow' change in reported speech?
questions.answer: Because the report is usually made at a different time from the original speech, so 'tomorrow' must become 'the next day' to keep the meaning accurate from the reporter's point of view.

questions.prompt: Find the HCF of 6 and 20 by prime factorisation.
questions.answer: 6 = 2 × 3, 20 = 2² × 5; common prime is 2 with smallest power 2¹, so HCF = 2.

questions.answer: Every composite number can be factorised into primes uniquely, apart from the order of the factors.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Find the LCM and HCF of 6, 72 and 120 using prime factorisation.
questions.answer: 6 = 2 × 3, 72 = 2³ × 3², 120 = 2³ × 3 × 5. HCF = 2 × 3 = 6 (smallest powers of common primes). LCM = 2³ × 3² × 5 = 360 (greatest powers of all primes).

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Prove that 3 + 2√5 is irrational, given that √5 is irrational.
questions.answer: Assume 3 + 2√5 is rational = r. Then 2√5 = r − 3, so √5 = (r − 3)/2. The right side is rational (difference and quotient of rationals), which would make √5 rational — a contradiction. Hence 3 + 2√5 is irrational.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A sweet seller has 420 kaju barfis and 130 badam barfis. He stacks them so each stack has the same number and takes least area. What is the number that should be in each stack?
questions.answer: He needs the HCF of 420 and 130. 420 = 2² × 3 × 5 × 7, 130 = 2 × 5 × 13. HCF = 2 × 5 = 10. So 10 barfis per stack.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Can two numbers have 16 as their HCF and 380 as their LCM? Justify.
questions.answer: No. The LCM must be a multiple of the HCF, but 380 ÷ 16 = 23.75 is not a whole number, so such numbers cannot exist.

questions.answer: Yes. x² − (5 + (−3))x + (5)(−3) = x² − 2x − 15. Multiplying by 2 gives 2x² − 4x − 30, which has the same zeroes.

questions.prompt: Solve 3x − 5y = −2 and 2x + y = 4 by elimination.
questions.answer: Multiply the second by 5: 10x + 5y = 20. Add to the first: 13x = 18 ⇒ x = 18/13. Hmm; instead multiply second by 5 and add: 3x − 5y = −2 and 10x + 5y = 20 give 13x = 18, x = 18/13, then y = 4 − 2(18/13) = (52 − 36)/13 = 16/13. Solution: (18/13, 16/13).

questions.prompt: A boat goes 30 km upstream and 44 km downstream in 10 hours. In 13 hours it goes 40 km upstream and 55 km downstream. (Set up only.) Why is a pair of linear equations useful here?
questions.answer: Let boat speed be x and stream speed y. Upstream speed = x − y, downstream = x + y. The two time conditions give two equations in 1/(x−y) and 1/(x+y), which form a solvable pair of linear equations in those reciprocals.

questions.prompt: The product of two consecutive positive integers is 306. Form and solve the quadratic equation to find them.
questions.answer: Let the integers be x and x + 1. Then x(x + 1) = 306 ⇒ x² + x − 306 = 0. Splitting: x² + 18x − 17x − 306 = (x + 18)(x − 17) = 0. The positive value is x = 17, so the integers are 17 and 18.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A train travels 360 km at a uniform speed. If the speed had been 5 km/h more, it would have taken 1 hour less. Find its speed.
questions.answer: Let speed be x km/h. Time difference: 360/x − 360/(x + 5) = 1. This gives x² + 5x − 1800 = 0 ⇒ (x + 45)(x − 40) = 0. Taking the positive root, x = 40 km/h.

questions.answer: Equal roots ⇒ D = 0 ⇒ (−2k)² − 4(k)(6) = 0 ⇒ 4k² − 24k = 0 ⇒ 4k(k − 6) = 0. Since k ≠ 0, k = 6.

questions.prompt: In triangle ABC, D and E are points on AB and AC such that DE ∥ BC. If AD = 4 cm, DB = 6 cm and AC = 15 cm, find AE.
questions.answer: By BPT, AD/DB = AE/EC. Let AE = x, then EC = 15 − x. So 4/6 = x/(15 − x) ⇒ 4(15 − x) = 6x ⇒ 60 − 4x = 6x ⇒ 60 = 10x ⇒ x = 6 cm.

questions.answer: Ratio of sides = √(100/49) = 10/7. So the corresponding side = 20 × 7/10 = 14 cm.

questions.prompt: The angles of elevation of the top of a tower from two points at distances 4 m and 9 m from the base (on the same side) are complementary. Find the height of the tower.
questions.answer: Let the angles be θ and 90° − θ, and height h. Then tan θ = h/9 and tan(90° − θ) = cot θ = h/4. Multiplying: (h/9)(h/4) = tan θ · cot θ = 1, so h² = 36 ⇒ h = 6 m.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why do we often get 'nice' answers like 10√3 in these problems?
questions.answer: Because the questions use standard angles (30°, 45°, 60°) whose trig values are simple surds, the arithmetic stays clean.

questions.subject: maths

questions.explanation: The diameter equals the side, so radius = 10/2 = 5 cm.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: The longest chord of a circle is its?
questions.explanation: The diameter is the longest chord, passing through the centre.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A line that touches a circle at exactly one point is a?
questions.explanation: A tangent meets a circle at exactly one point.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: How many tangents can a circle have in all?
questions.answer: Infinitely many — one at every point of the circle.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: State the angle between a tangent and the radius at the point of contact.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: If one tangent from an external point is 9 cm, what is the other?
questions.answer: 9 cm — tangents from an external point are equal.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Prove that the tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact (state the idea).
questions.answer: Take the centre O and tangent at point P. Any point Q on the tangent other than P lies outside the circle, so OQ > OP. Hence OP is the shortest distance from O to the tangent, and the shortest distance is the perpendicular — so OP ⟂ the tangent.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Two concentric circles have radii 5 cm and 3 cm. Find the length of the chord of the larger circle that is tangent to the smaller one.
questions.answer: The tangent touches the smaller circle, so the radius (3 cm) is perpendicular to the chord and bisects it. Half-chord = √(5² − 3²) = √16 = 4 cm, so the full chord = 8 cm.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A quadrilateral ABCD is drawn so that all four sides touch a circle. Show that AB + CD = AD + BC.
questions.answer: Using equal tangents from each vertex, the tangent lengths satisfy AB + CD = (AP + PB) + (CR + RD) and AD + BC = (AS + SD) + (BQ + QC); equal tangents pair up to make both sums equal.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why can't a tangent be drawn from a point inside the circle?
questions.answer: Any line through an interior point must cross the circle at two points (a secant), so it can never touch at just one point.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: What is a segment of a circle?
questions.answer: The region between a chord and the arc it cuts off.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A chord of a circle of radius 10 cm subtends a right angle at the centre. Find the area of the corresponding minor segment. (π = 3.14)
questions.answer: Area of sector = (90/360) × 3.14 × 100 = 78.5 cm². Area of triangle = ½ × 10 × 10 = 50 cm². Segment = 78.5 − 50 = 28.5 cm².

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Find the area of a sector of radius 7 cm whose arc length is 11 cm. (π = 22/7)

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A square of side 14 cm has a circle inscribed in it. Find the area between the square and the circle. (π = 22/7)
questions.answer: Circle radius = 7 cm, area = 154 cm². Square area = 14² = 196 cm². Area between them = 196 − 154 = 42 cm².

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why do the arc, sector and circumference formulas all contain the fraction θ/360?
questions.answer: Because a full circle corresponds to 360°, an angle θ represents the fraction θ/360 of the whole, so each whole-circle quantity is scaled by that fraction.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A solid is in the shape of a cone mounted on a hemisphere, both of radius 7 cm, the cone's height being 24 cm. Find the total volume. (π = 22/7)
questions.answer: Cone volume = ⅓πr²h = ⅓ × (22/7) × 49 × 24 = 1232 cm³. Hemisphere volume = (2/3)πr³ = (2/3) × (22/7) × 343 ≈ 718.67 cm³. Total ≈ 1232 + 718.67 = 1950.67 cm³.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A metallic sphere of radius 6 cm is melted and recast into a cylinder of radius 4 cm. Find the height of the cylinder.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: How many spherical lead shots of radius 1 cm can be made from a solid cube of lead of edge 11 cm? (π = 22/7)
questions.answer: Cube volume = 11³ = 1331 cm³. One shot = (4/3)π(1)³ = 88/21 cm³. Number = 1331 ÷ (88/21) = 1331 × 21/88 ≈ 317 shots.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why do you not include the joined circular faces when finding the surface area of a cone-on-cylinder?
questions.answer: Those faces are hidden inside the combined solid, so they are not part of its exposed (outer) surface area.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: The frequencies of classes 0–10, 10–20, 20–30, 30–40 are 6, 10, 8, 6. Find the modal class and explain how you would compute the mode.
questions.answer: The highest frequency is 10 in the class 10–20, so that is the modal class. Then apply Mode = l + [(f₁ − f₀)/(2f₁ − f₀ − f₂)]h with l = 10, f₁ = 10, f₀ = 6, f₂ = 8, h = 10, giving Mode = 10 + [(10 − 6)/(20 − 6 − 8)]×10 = 10 + (4/6)×10 ≈ 16.67.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Can the mean, median and mode of a data set all be equal? When?
questions.answer: Yes — for a perfectly symmetric distribution (such as a normal-shaped data set), the mean, median and mode coincide.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Why is the median sometimes preferred over the mean?
questions.answer: The median is not affected by extreme values (outliers), so it represents skewed data better than the mean.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: One card is drawn from a deck of 52 cards. Find the probability that it is (i) a red card, (ii) a face card.
questions.answer: (i) There are 26 red cards, so P(red) = 26/52 = 1/2. (ii) Face cards (J, Q, K of each suit) number 12, so P(face card) = 12/52 = 3/13.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A bag contains 5 red, 4 green and 3 blue balls. One ball is drawn at random. Find the probability it is (i) green, (ii) not blue.
questions.answer: Total = 12 balls. (i) P(green) = 4/12 = 1/3. (ii) Not blue means red or green = 9 balls, so P(not blue) = 9/12 = 3/4 (or 1 − 3/12).

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: Two coins are tossed together. Find the probability of getting at least one head.
questions.answer: Outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT (4 total). 'At least one head' excludes only TT, so favourable = 3. P = 3/4.

questions.subject: maths
questions.prompt: A number is chosen at random from 1 to 25. Find the probability that it is a multiple of 5.
questions.answer: Multiples of 5 from 1 to 25 are 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 — that's 5 numbers. P = 5/25 = 1/5.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A balanced chemical equation obeys the law of:
questions.explanation: Equal atoms on both sides reflect conservation of mass.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Two substances combine to form one product.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ on heating is a:
questions.explanation: One compound splits into two — decomposition.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu is a:
questions.explanation: More reactive iron displaces copper — displacement.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Oxidation is defined as:
options: gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen
questions.answer: gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen
questions.explanation: Oxidation = gain of oxygen / loss of hydrogen.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Reduction is defined as:
options: loss of oxygen or gain of hydrogen
questions.answer: loss of oxygen or gain of hydrogen
questions.explanation: Reduction = loss of oxygen / gain of hydrogen.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The unpleasant smell of oily food kept for long is due to:
questions.explanation: Rancidity is the oxidation of fats and oils.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Rusting of iron is an example of:
questions.explanation: Rusting is corrosion — slow oxidation of a metal.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: In Na₂SO₄ + BaCl₂ → BaSO₄ + 2NaCl, the white precipitate is:
questions.explanation: Barium sulphate (BaSO₄) is the insoluble white precipitate.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Respiration in our body is a:
questions.explanation: Respiration releases energy, so it is exothermic (and a redox/oxidation process).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A reaction in which two or more substances combine to form a single product is a?
questions.explanation: In a combination reaction, A + B → AB.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A reaction in which a single compound breaks into simpler substances is a?

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen by a substance is called?
questions.explanation: Oxidation is gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why must a chemical equation be balanced?
questions.answer: To satisfy the law of conservation of mass — atoms are neither created nor destroyed in a reaction.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Give one example of a combination reaction.
questions.answer: C + O₂ → CO₂ (carbon burns in oxygen to form carbon dioxide).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: How can rancidity be prevented?
questions.answer: By adding antioxidants, packing in nitrogen, keeping food refrigerated and storing it in airtight containers.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Define and give one example each of displacement and double displacement reactions.
questions.answer: Displacement: a more reactive element replaces a less reactive one, e.g. Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu. Double displacement: two compounds exchange ions, e.g. AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl↓ + NaNO₃, forming a precipitate of silver chloride.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is a redox reaction? Identify the substance oxidised and reduced in CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O.
questions.answer: A redox reaction is one in which oxidation and reduction occur together. Here CuO loses oxygen (reduced to Cu), and H₂ gains oxygen (oxidised to H₂O). So CuO is reduced and H₂ is oxidised.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does the colour of copper sulphate solution change when an iron nail is dipped in it?
questions.answer: Iron is more reactive than copper and displaces it from copper sulphate, forming light-green iron sulphate. The blue colour fades and reddish copper deposits on the nail.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A magnesium ribbon is burnt in air. Why should it not be looked at directly?
questions.answer: Magnesium burns with a dazzling white light (forming MgO) that is bright enough to harm the eyes, so direct viewing is avoided.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Acids produce which ion in water?
questions.explanation: Acids release hydrogen ions (H⁺) in water.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A neutral solution has a pH of:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Acid reacting with a metal produces:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The reaction of an acid with a base is called:
questions.explanation: Acid + base → salt + water is neutralisation.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which of these is used to whiten clothes and disinfect water?
questions.explanation: Bleaching powder (CaOCl₂) is a bleaching and disinfecting agent.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Baking soda is chemically:
questions.explanation: Baking soda is sodium hydrogen carbonate, NaHCO₃.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Plaster of Paris is obtained by heating:
questions.explanation: Heating gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) gives plaster of Paris (CaSO₄·½H₂O).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: As acidity increases, the pH value:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Acid reacting with a carbonate releases which gas?

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Acids produce which ion when dissolved in water?
questions.explanation: Acids release H+ ions in water; bases release OH- ions.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The pH value of a neutral solution such as pure water is?
questions.explanation: A neutral solution has a pH of 7.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is an alkali?
questions.answer: A base that dissolves in water (e.g. NaOH, KOH).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Name the gas released when an acid reacts with a metal, and the test for it.
questions.answer: Hydrogen gas; it burns with a 'pop' sound when a burning splinter is brought near.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Give the chemical name and one use of washing soda.
questions.answer: Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃·10H₂O); used for removing permanent hardness of water and in cleaning.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain the preparation and two uses of bleaching powder.
questions.answer: Bleaching powder (calcium oxychloride, CaOCl₂) is prepared by passing chlorine over dry slaked lime [Ca(OH)₂]. Uses: bleaching cotton and paper, and disinfecting drinking water.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is plaster of Paris stored in moisture-proof containers?
questions.answer: Plaster of Paris (CaSO₄·½H₂O) reacts with water and sets into a hard solid (gypsum). If exposed to moisture, it would set in the container and become useless, so it is kept dry.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A farmer's soil is too acidic. What should be added and why?
questions.answer: A base such as quicklime (CaO) or slaked lime [Ca(OH)₂] is added to neutralise the excess acid and raise the soil pH to a range suitable for crops.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does dry HCl gas not turn blue litmus red, but its water solution does?
questions.answer: Dry HCl gas does not release H⁺ ions; only when dissolved in water does it ionise to give H⁺, which makes it acidic and turns blue litmus red.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which property allows metals to be drawn into wires?
questions.explanation: Ductility is the ability to be drawn into wires.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The only metal that is liquid at room temperature is:
questions.explanation: Mercury is a liquid metal at room temperature.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which non-metal is a good conductor of electricity?
questions.explanation: Graphite (a form of carbon) conducts electricity.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Metals react with dilute acids to release:
questions.explanation: Metal + dilute acid → salt + hydrogen gas.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The most reactive metal among these is:
questions.explanation: Potassium is at the top of the reactivity series.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Ionic compounds generally have:
questions.explanation: Strong electrostatic forces give ionic compounds high melting points.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Coating iron with zinc to prevent rusting is called:
questions.explanation: Galvanising is coating iron/steel with zinc.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Sodium is stored under kerosene because it:
options: reacts vigorously with air and water
questions.answer: reacts vigorously with air and water
questions.explanation: Kerosene keeps reactive sodium away from air and moisture.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A homogeneous mixture of metals is called a/an:
questions.explanation: An alloy is a mixture of a metal with other metals/non-metals.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Highly reactive metals like sodium and potassium are extracted by:
questions.explanation: Very reactive metals are obtained by electrolytic reduction.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which gas is usually released when a metal reacts with a dilute acid?
questions.explanation: Metal + dilute acid → salt + hydrogen gas.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why are metals sonorous?
questions.answer: They produce a ringing sound when struck, which is why they are used in bells and wires.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Name two metals that are found in a free state in nature.
questions.answer: Gold and silver (and platinum) — they are unreactive.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is an amalgam?
questions.answer: An alloy in which one of the metals is mercury.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain how the reactivity series helps in displacement reactions, with an example.
questions.answer: A metal higher in the reactivity series can displace a metal lower than it from the latter's salt solution. For example, iron (higher) displaces copper (lower): Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu. A less reactive metal cannot displace a more reactive one.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe how moderately reactive metals are extracted from their oxide ores.
questions.answer: Their ores are first converted to oxides (by roasting sulphide ores or calcining carbonate ores). The oxide is then reduced to the metal using a reducing agent such as carbon (e.g. ZnO + C → Zn + CO). Finally, the metal is refined, often by electrolysis.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does silver jewellery turn black over time?
questions.answer: Silver reacts slowly with sulphur compounds in the air to form a black coating of silver sulphide — a form of corrosion specific to silver.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Aluminium is reactive, yet it resists corrosion. Why?
questions.answer: Aluminium forms a thin, hard, unreactive layer of aluminium oxide on its surface that protects the metal underneath from further reaction.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Carbon achieves a stable octet by:
questions.explanation: Carbon shares electrons to form four covalent bonds.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The ability of carbon to form long chains is called:
questions.explanation: Catenation is self-linking of carbon atoms.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The simplest hydrocarbon is:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The functional group in ethanoic acid is:
questions.explanation: Ethanoic acid contains the carboxylic acid group –COOH.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Two successive members of a homologous series differ by:
questions.explanation: They differ by a –CH₂– unit (14 mass units).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which has a carbon–carbon double bond?
questions.explanation: Alkenes contain a C=C double bond.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Ethanol's molecular formula is:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Soaps are sodium or potassium salts of:
questions.explanation: Soaps are salts of long-chain fatty acids.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Diamond and graphite are ____ of carbon.
questions.explanation: They are different physical forms (allotropes) of the same element.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Detergents are preferred over soaps in hard water because they:
options: do not form scum with hard water
questions.answer: do not form scum with hard water
questions.explanation: Detergents do not form insoluble scum with the calcium/magnesium ions of hard water.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The ability of carbon to form long chains by bonding with its own atoms is called?
questions.explanation: Catenation is carbon's self-linking property forming chains and rings.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Define tetravalency of carbon.
questions.answer: Carbon has four valence electrons, so it can form four covalent bonds.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Name the gas formed when ethanol reacts with sodium.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is ethanoic acid called a weak acid?
questions.answer: It only partially ionises in water, releasing few hydrogen ions.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is a homologous series? Give two characteristics.
questions.answer: A homologous series is a family of organic compounds with the same functional group and a general formula, where consecutive members differ by –CH₂–. Characteristics: (i) members have similar chemical properties, and (ii) their physical properties (like boiling point) change gradually with increasing molecular size.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain the cleansing action of soap using the idea of micelles.
questions.answer: A soap molecule has a hydrophilic (water-loving) head and a hydrophobic (oil-loving) tail. In water, the tails point inward toward grease and the heads point outward, forming spherical clusters called micelles. The grease is trapped inside the micelles and carried away with the rinse water.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why do unsaturated hydrocarbons burn with a sooty flame?
questions.answer: They have a higher proportion of carbon, which does not burn completely in the available oxygen, releasing unburnt carbon particles (soot) that make the flame yellow and smoky.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Conversion of ethanol to ethanoic acid is called an oxidation reaction. Why?
questions.answer: Oxygen is added (hydrogen is removed) when ethanol (C₂H₅OH) is converted to ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH), and addition of oxygen is oxidation.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Green plants prepare food by:
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis makes food using sunlight, CO₂ and water.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The basic filtering unit of the kidney is the:
questions.explanation: The nephron filters blood to form urine.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Water and minerals in plants are transported by:
questions.explanation: Xylem carries water and minerals upward.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Anaerobic respiration in muscles produces:
questions.explanation: Muscle cells form lactic acid without oxygen.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Aerobic respiration releases ____ energy compared to anaerobic.
questions.explanation: Complete oxidation in aerobic respiration releases more energy.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart are:
questions.explanation: Arteries carry blood away from the heart.

questions.explanation: Alveoli are the tiny air sacs where gas exchange happens.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: During photosynthesis, plants take in ____ and release ____.
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ and give out O₂ during photosynthesis.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The process by which green plants prepare their food is?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis makes glucose using sunlight, water and carbon dioxide.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The red pigment in blood that carries oxygen is?
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin in red blood cells carries oxygen.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Name the products of anaerobic respiration in yeast.
questions.answer: Ethanol and carbon dioxide (with some energy).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is translocation?
questions.answer: The transport of food made in the leaves to other parts of the plant through phloem.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Where is bile produced and stored?
questions.answer: Bile is produced by the liver and stored in the gall bladder.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe the human double circulation.
questions.answer: Blood passes twice through the heart in one cycle. In pulmonary circulation, deoxygenated blood goes from the right side of the heart to the lungs and returns oxygenated. In systemic circulation, oxygenated blood is pumped from the left side to the whole body and returns deoxygenated. The complete separation keeps oxygenated and deoxygenated blood from mixing, which is efficient for warm-blooded animals.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration.
questions.answer: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen, occurs mainly in the mitochondria, completely breaks down glucose into CO₂ and water, and releases a large amount of energy. Anaerobic respiration occurs without oxygen, breaks glucose only partially (into lactic acid in muscles or ethanol + CO₂ in yeast), and releases much less energy.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why are the walls of the left ventricle thicker than those of the right ventricle?
questions.answer: The left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood to the entire body against high pressure, so it needs thicker, more muscular walls than the right ventricle, which only pumps blood to the nearby lungs.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does a plant not need a special excretory organ like the kidney?
questions.answer: Plants produce less harmful waste, can store wastes in leaves/bark that fall off, and release gaseous wastes through stomata, so they do not need a dedicated excretory organ.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The structural and functional unit of the nervous system is the:
questions.explanation: The neuron (nerve cell) is the basic unit.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The gap between two neurons is called a:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Reflex actions are controlled mainly by the:
questions.explanation: The spinal cord processes reflexes quickly.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The part of the brain that controls balance and posture is the:
questions.explanation: The cerebellum maintains balance and posture.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The hormone that controls blood sugar is:
questions.explanation: Insulin from the pancreas regulates blood glucose.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The plant hormone responsible for bending toward light is:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Growth of a plant root toward gravity is called:
questions.explanation: Geotropism is growth in response to gravity.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The 'fight or flight' hormone is:
questions.explanation: Adrenaline prepares the body for emergencies.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Thinking, memory and voluntary actions are controlled by the:
questions.explanation: The cerebrum is the centre for thought and voluntary action.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which hormone promotes growth in plants and is used to make stems longer?
questions.explanation: Gibberellins promote stem elongation and growth.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is a stimulus?
questions.answer: Any change in the environment to which an organism responds.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Name the gland that secretes insulin.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which plant hormone inhibits growth and causes wilting?

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe the path of a reflex arc when you touch a hot object.
questions.answer: Heat (stimulus) is detected by receptors in the skin. A sensory neuron carries the impulse to the spinal cord, which immediately passes it through a relay to a motor neuron. The motor neuron signals the arm muscles to contract, pulling the hand away — all before the brain consciously feels the pain.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain how hormonal coordination in plants differs from nervous coordination in animals.
questions.answer: Animals coordinate quickly using both electrical impulses (nervous system) and hormones, allowing fast, specific responses. Plants have no nervous system; they coordinate only through hormones, producing slower responses seen as growth movements (tropisms) rather than rapid actions.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A person's hand is on a hot plate. Why do they pull it away before they 'feel' the heat?
questions.answer: The protective reflex acts through the spinal cord and moves the hand instantly, while the slower signal to the brain that produces the conscious feeling of heat arrives a moment later.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why is feedback control important for hormones like insulin?
questions.answer: Feedback keeps hormone levels balanced: when blood sugar rises, more insulin is released; when it falls, less is released. This self-regulation prevents the level from going too high or too low.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Amoeba reproduces by:
questions.explanation: Amoeba divides into two by binary fission.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Reproduction involving two parents and gametes is:
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction involves two parents and the fusion of gametes.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The male reproductive part of a flower is the:
questions.explanation: The stamen (anther + filament) is the male part.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The transfer of pollen from anther to stigma is called:

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Yeast and Hydra reproduce by:
questions.explanation: Yeast and Hydra reproduce by budding.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The fusion of male and female gametes is called:

questions.prompt: Planaria can regrow its body parts by:

questions.explanation: The placenta allows exchange of nutrients and wastes.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is a zygote?
questions.answer: The single cell formed when the male and female gametes fuse during fertilisation.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Name the female reproductive part of a flower.
questions.answer: The carpel (or pistil), made of stigma, style and ovary.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Give one example each of fragmentation and regeneration.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Describe the steps of sexual reproduction in a flowering plant.
questions.answer: Pollen from the anther reaches the stigma (pollination). A pollen tube grows down the style to the ovary, carrying the male gametes. One male gamete fuses with the egg (fertilisation) to form a zygote inside the ovule. The ovule develops into a seed and the ovary into a fruit, while other floral parts usually wither.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain why variation is important for a species.
questions.answer: Variation gives the individuals of a species a range of traits. If the environment changes, some variants may be better suited to survive and reproduce, helping the species adapt over time. Without variation, a sudden change could wipe out a whole population.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why are offspring produced by asexual reproduction called clones?
questions.answer: They develop from a single parent by simple cell division, so they carry exactly the same genetic material as the parent and as one another — making them clones.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why do organisms that reproduce sexually adapt better to changing environments?
questions.answer: Sexual reproduction shuffles genes from two parents, producing varied offspring. This diversity raises the chance that some individuals can cope with new conditions, improving the species' survival.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The scientist who founded the study of heredity using pea plants was:
questions.explanation: Gregor Mendel is called the father of genetics.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A trait that appears with only one copy of its gene is:
questions.explanation: A dominant trait shows up even with a single copy.

questions.explanation: The father's sperm carries X or Y, deciding the sex.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The unit of inheritance carried on chromosomes is the?
questions.explanation: Genes are the units of heredity located on chromosomes.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: What is the difference between genotype and phenotype?
questions.answer: Genotype is the genetic make-up (e.g. Tt); phenotype is the visible trait (e.g. tall).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Which chromosome does a sperm need to carry to produce a male child?

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: State Mendel's law of segregation in one line.
questions.answer: The two factors of a trait separate during gamete formation, and each gamete carries only one of them.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain a monohybrid cross between a tall (TT) and a short (tt) pea plant up to the F₂ generation.
questions.answer: F₁: TT × tt gives all Tt — tall plants (T is dominant). F₂: crossing Tt × Tt gives TT, Tt, Tt, tt → genotype ratio 1 : 2 : 1 and phenotype ratio 3 tall : 1 short. This shows that the recessive (short) trait, hidden in F₁, reappears in F₂.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: How is the sex of a child determined in humans?
questions.answer: Of the 23 chromosome pairs, one is the sex pair. Women are XX and men are XY. Every egg carries X, but a sperm carries either X or Y. If an X-carrying sperm fertilises the egg, the child is XX (girl); if a Y-carrying sperm does, the child is XY (boy). Thus the father's sperm determines the sex.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Two black guinea pigs are crossed and produce some white offspring. What does this tell you about the parents?
questions.answer: White must be recessive and both parents must be heterozygous (Bb). Each carries a hidden white gene, so some offspring receive bb and appear white.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why did Mendel choose pea plants for his experiments?
questions.answer: Pea plants have clearly contrasting traits, grow quickly, produce many offspring, and can be self- or cross-pollinated easily, making them ideal for studying inheritance.

questions.explanation: The focal length is half the radius of curvature.

questions.explanation: A convex mirror always gives a virtual, erect, diminished image.

questions.explanation: Power of a lens is measured in dioptres (D).

questions.prompt: The refractive index of a medium is given by:
questions.explanation: n = c/v, the ratio of speed of light in vacuum to that in the medium.

questions.explanation: Bending at a boundary between media is refraction.

questions.explanation: By convention a concave (diverging) lens has negative focal length and power.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The bending of light as it passes from one transparent medium to another is?
questions.explanation: Refraction is the change in direction of light between media.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: State the two laws of reflection.
questions.answer: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, and the incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in the same plane.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Define the power of a lens and give its unit.
questions.answer: Power is the reciprocal of focal length in metres (P = 1/f); its unit is the dioptre (D).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Why does a convex mirror always give a virtual image?
questions.answer: Reflected rays from a convex mirror diverge, so they only appear to meet behind the mirror, producing a virtual, erect, diminished image.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: An object is placed 10 cm from a convex lens of focal length 15 cm. Find the image distance and state the nature of the image.
questions.answer: u = −10 cm, f = +15 cm. Lens formula: 1/v = 1/f + 1/u = 1/15 + (−1/10) = 2/30 − 3/30 = −1/30, so v = −30 cm. The image is virtual, erect and magnified (on the same side as the object), as expected when the object is within the focal length.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: Explain why we use a convex mirror as a rear-view mirror in vehicles.
questions.answer: A convex mirror always gives an erect, diminished image and has a wider field of view than a plane mirror, letting the driver see a larger area of traffic behind in a small mirror.

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: A concave mirror produces a three-times magnified real image of an object placed 10 cm in front of it. Where is the image?
questions.answer: For a real image, magnification m = −3, and m = −v/u with u = −10 cm. So −3 = −v/(−10) ⇒ v = −30 cm. The image is 30 cm in front of the mirror (real and inverted).

questions.subject: science
questions.prompt: The refractive index of medium A with respect to B is 1.5. What is the refractive index of B with respect to A?
questions.answer: They are reciprocals: n(B with respect to A) = 1 ÷ 1.5 = 0.67 (approximately 2/3).

questions.explanation: Scattering of shorter (blue) wavelengths makes the sky blue.
