questions.prompt: What was apartheid?
questions.answer: A policy of racial discrimination that oppressed South Africa's Black majority.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: Nelson Mandela A Long Walk to Freedom
questions.prompt: Whom did Mandela call the real heroes?
questions.answer: Those who suffered and sacrificed in the freedom struggle.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: Nelson Mandela A Long Walk to Freedom
questions.prompt: Explain Mandela's idea of freedom as expressed in the extract.
questions.answer: Mandela's idea of freedom deepens through his life. As a boy, he believed freedom meant simply doing as he pleased — running in the fields and enjoying himself. As a young man, he came to see that this was only a child's idea, and that his real freedom and that of his people had been taken away. He realised he was not free while his brothers and sisters lived in chains, and that the hunger for his own freedom became a hunger for the freedom of all. Most strikingly, Mandela concludes that freedom is indivisible and works both ways: the oppressed and the oppressor must both be set free, because a man who robs another of freedom is himself a prisoner of hatred and prejudice. True freedom, for him, is therefore to live in a way that respects and enhances the freedom of others.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: Nelson Mandela A Long Walk to Freedom
questions.prompt: How does Mandela view the relationship between suffering and courage in his country?
questions.answer: Mandela reflects that the cruel system of apartheid, which caused immense suffering, had an unexpected effect: it produced extraordinary courage and character. He observes that the depth of oppression in South Africa created great heights of human spirit, bringing forth men and women of remarkable bravery, wisdom and generosity. He honours the countless ordinary people who endured imprisonment, hardship and death so that others might be free, calling them the true heroes. Thus, while never excusing the injustice of apartheid, Mandela acknowledges that the very harshness of the struggle forged a generation of people whose courage and sacrifice made freedom possible, showing the triumph of the human spirit over injustice.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: Nelson Mandela A Long Walk to Freedom
questions.prompt: Why does Mandela believe the oppressor is also not free?
questions.answer: Because to keep others down, the oppressor must live behind walls of hatred and prejudice, losing his own humanity and freedom in the process.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: Nelson Mandela A Long Walk to Freedom
questions.prompt: What does the worldwide attendance at his inauguration signify?
questions.answer: It signified the world's recognition and support for the end of apartheid and the birth of a free, democratic South Africa.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the young seagull overcome his fear in 'His First Flight'?
questions.answer: The young seagull is paralysed by the fear that his wings will not hold him in the air, so he refuses to fly even when his parents, brothers and sister all soar around him. Left alone and hungry on the ledge, he watches the others eat and grows desperate. His family tries coaxing and even threatens to let him starve, but it is hunger that finally overcomes his fear: when his mother flies close with a piece of fish, the starving seagull, forgetting everything else, dives off the ledge to seize it. In that moment his wings spread and he finds himself flying. His fall becomes flight, and soon he is gliding over the sea joyfully. The story shows that necessity can push us past our fears and reveal abilities we did not know we had.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe the pilot's experience in 'The Black Aeroplane' and its mysterious ending.
questions.answer: In 'The Black Aeroplane', a pilot flying alone at night towards England encounters a vast, dark storm cloud. As he enters it, everything goes black, his instruments stop working, and he loses all sense of direction, becoming frightened and helpless. Just then, another black aeroplane appears beside him, its pilot calmly signalling him to follow. Trusting this stranger, he follows through the storm and is led safely down to a runway, his fuel almost gone. Relieved, he asks the control tower to identify the other plane that guided him — and is told that no other aircraft had been on the radar at all. The story ends in mystery, for the identity of his rescuer is never explained, leaving readers to wonder whether it was chance, imagination or something beyond explanation.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What do both stories suggest about how courage is found?
questions.answer: They suggest courage often emerges under pressure — through hunger or danger — rather than being present beforehand, and that acting despite fear can lead to success.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the unexplained ending make the second story memorable?
questions.answer: Because it leaves a sense of wonder and mystery, inviting readers to reflect on faith, luck and the limits of explanation.

questions.answer: To write essays on the subject of being a chatterbox.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: From the Diary of Anne Frank
questions.prompt: Why did Anne Frank decide to keep a diary, and what does this reveal about her?
questions.answer: Anne decided to keep a diary because, although she had loving parents, a sister and many friends, she felt she had no one with whom she could share her truly personal thoughts and feelings. She believed that paper had more patience than people, and so she chose to confide in her diary, which she treated as a close friend and named 'Kitty', writing to it as though to a companion. This decision reveals several things about Anne: she was a sensitive and reflective girl with a rich inner life, she felt a degree of loneliness despite being surrounded by people, and she had a strong need to express herself honestly. It also shows her thoughtfulness and maturity, as she turned to writing as a way to understand and record her own experiences.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: From the Diary of Anne Frank
questions.prompt: Describe the episode of Anne and her teacher Mr Keesing.
questions.answer: Anne was a talkative student, and her maths teacher, Mr Keesing, grew irritated by her constant chatter in class. To curb it, he punished her by setting an essay on the subject of being 'a chatterbox'. Far from being discouraged, Anne wrote a clever and witty essay arguing, with good humour, that talking was a part of her nature and that she could not easily change it. When her talking continued and he set further essays on the topic, she kept up her spirited, amusing arguments. Finally, with the help of a friend, she composed a humorous poem in response, which so amused Mr Keesing that he began to take her talking in good part and even allowed her to talk. The episode shows Anne's wit, confidence and likeable personality, and how humour can ease a difficult situation.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: From the Diary of Anne Frank
questions.prompt: Why might writing in a diary be comforting for someone in Anne's situation?
questions.answer: It offers a private, judgement-free space to express fears, hopes and feelings, providing companionship and relief when one cannot confide in others.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: From the Diary of Anne Frank
questions.prompt: What does the Mr Keesing episode show about handling conflict?
questions.answer: It shows that wit and good humour can turn a tense situation into a friendly one, winning over even a strict teacher.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does 'A Baker from Goa' show the importance of the baker in Goan life?
questions.answer: 'A Baker from Goa' presents the village baker, or pader, as a much-loved and essential figure in old Goan society, shaped by the long Portuguese influence in the region. The writer fondly recalls how the baker's fresh bread and sweet treats were not a luxury but a part of everyday life and especially of celebrations — no festival, wedding or feast was complete without them. The baker would visit regularly, greeted eagerly by children, and was recognised by his particular dress. Baking was a respected and profitable family profession passed down the generations, and the baker held a warm place in people's hearts. Through these memories, the piece shows how deeply the baker was woven into the social and festive fabric of Goan life.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What picture of India emerges from the three pieces of 'Glimpses of India'?
questions.answer: The three pieces together paint a picture of India as a land of rich cultural and natural diversity. 'A Baker from Goa' shows the cultural heritage of Goa, blending old Portuguese influence with cherished local traditions like the village baker. 'Coorg' presents a region of great natural beauty in Karnataka, with its coffee plantations, rainforests and wildlife, and a proud people known for bravery and hospitality. 'Tea from Assam' takes us to the vast green tea gardens of the north-east, one of the world's great tea-growing regions, woven together with charming legends about the origin of tea. Each piece highlights a different region, tradition or product, and together they celebrate how varied, colourful and culturally rich India is.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the writer of 'A Baker from Goa' look back at the baker with such fondness?
questions.answer: Because the baker was tied to happy memories of childhood, festivals and a warm community life, representing a cherished tradition the writer does not want forgotten.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How do legends, like those about tea, add value to a place's identity?
questions.answer: They give a place colour and a sense of history, making its products and culture more memorable and meaningful to people.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe Mij's playful nature and his bond with the author.
questions.answer: Mij is one of the most engaging characters in the prose section because of his lively, playful nature. From being shy at first, he quickly becomes friendly and brimming with energy. He has a passion for water, splashing and rolling in it whenever he can, and a remarkable talent for play — he turns ordinary objects such as a ball or marbles into toys and invents his own games, keeping himself happily occupied. Beyond his playfulness, Mij is affectionate and forms a close attachment to the author, following and trusting him. The author, in turn, grows deeply fond of Mij, going to great lengths to care for him and bring him safely to England. Their relationship beautifully illustrates the warm bond that can grow between a human and an animal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Narrate the difficulties the author faced in transporting Mij to England.
questions.answer: Bringing Mij from Iraq to England proved to be an adventure full of difficulties. The airline would not allow the otter in the cabin and insisted that he travel boxed, so the author had to obtain a suitable box and place Mij inside it. Confined and unhappy, Mij struggled and managed to free himself, causing commotion and damaging things on the aircraft. The situation was saved by a kind-hearted air hostess, who suggested that the author keep Mij on his lap, after which the otter settled down. Eventually they reached London safely, where Mij became a beloved companion and was even taken out on a lead, although passers-by were often puzzled about what kind of creature he actually was. The episode shows both the trouble and the affection involved in caring for an unusual pet.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does keeping a wild animal like an otter bring both joy and responsibility?
questions.answer: It brings joy through the animal's playfulness and affection, but responsibility because such creatures have special needs and can cause difficulties, as Mij did on the plane.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the air hostess's kindness reveal about helping others?
questions.answer: It shows how a small, thoughtful act of kindness can resolve a difficult situation and make a big difference to someone in trouble.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe the character of Valli with reference to the story.
questions.answer: Valli is a spirited eight-year-old whose chief qualities are curiosity, determination and independence. Fascinated by the world beyond her village, she longs to ride the town bus, and rather than simply asking for help she patiently saves small coins over time, denying herself fairs and toys to reach her goal — showing great self-discipline. On the journey she is observant, drinking in every sight, and fiercely self-respecting: she politely but firmly refuses the conductor's treats and the offers of help from fellow passengers because she wants to do everything herself and be treated as a grown-up. Yet she is also sensitive, being genuinely moved by the sight of a dead cow on the road. Valli thus combines a child's eager curiosity with surprising maturity and dignity.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Valli's bus journey become a lesson about life?
questions.answer: What begins as a simple childhood adventure quietly turns into a deeper lesson about life. As Valli rides the bus, she delights in the beauty around her — the green fields, the canal, the people and the moving scenery — experiencing the joy and wonder of the world she had long wished to see. But the journey also shows her another side of reality: she is troubled when she sees a dead cow lying on the road, a sudden glimpse of sorrow and death amid all the beauty. In this way, the trip exposes Valli to the truth that the world holds both happiness and sadness together. Her first independent encounter with the world thus becomes a small but meaningful step in understanding life as it really is.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does Valli insist on doing everything by herself on the journey?
questions.answer: Because she wants to prove that she is grown-up and capable, and her self-respect makes her dislike being treated like a small child.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the sight of the dead cow change the mood of Valli's adventure?
questions.answer: It brings a note of sadness into her joyful outing, teaching her that the world contains sorrow as well as beauty, and adding depth to her experience.

questions.answer: That death is universal and her sorrow is shared by all.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the Buddha teach Kisa Gotami to accept the death of her son?
questions.answer: The Buddha teaches Kisa Gotami not by lecturing her but by leading her to discover the truth for herself. When she comes to him carrying her dead son and begging for medicine to revive him, he does not refuse outright. Instead, he asks her to bring a handful of mustard seeds — but with the condition that they must come from a house where no one has ever lost a child, husband, parent or friend to death. Hopeful, Kisa Gotami goes from door to door, only to find that in every single home someone has died; death has touched every family. Through this gentle, practical lesson she comes to realise on her own that death is the common fate of all living beings and that her grief is shared by everyone. This understanding calms her, and she accepts her loss, finding peace instead of endless sorrow.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the message of 'The Sermon at Benares' about grief and death?
questions.answer: The lesson conveys a profound message about grief and death. It teaches that death is universal and inevitable — it comes to every person and every family, sparing none. While grief at the loss of a loved one is natural, the Buddha shows that endless lamenting cannot bring the dead back and only deepens one's suffering. True peace comes from accepting the reality of death as a part of life that all must face. By recognising that her sorrow is not hers alone but shared by all of humanity, Kisa Gotami is freed from her overwhelming grief. The message, therefore, is that wisdom and peace lie in accepting death calmly rather than fighting a truth that cannot be changed.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the search for mustard seeds a more effective teaching than a direct sermon would be?
questions.answer: Because Kisa Gotami discovers the truth through her own experience, which convinces her far more deeply and lastingly than simply being told that death is universal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How can the knowledge that suffering is shared help a grieving person?
questions.answer: Realising that others have faced the same loss reduces the sense of being singled out, brings a feeling of shared humanity, and makes the grief easier to bear.

questions.answer: He hurriedly joins the couple's hands and declares them engaged.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does 'The Proposal' use quarrels for comic effect?
questions.answer: The humour of 'The Proposal' lies almost entirely in its quarrels. Lomov arrives intending to propose to Natalya, but instead of a romantic scene the play gives us one absurd argument after another. First, before he can even propose, Lomov and Natalya fall into a heated dispute over who owns Oxen Meadows, with Chubukov joining in until Lomov storms off. No sooner is he brought back than a second quarrel erupts, this time over whose dog — Lomov's Guess or Natalya's Squeezer — is the better animal, and the shouting grows wilder than before. The comedy comes from the trivial nature of these disputes, the characters' exaggerated tempers, and Lomov's constant complaints about his health. The crowning comic touch is that the moment they are finally engaged, the couple immediately begin to argue again, suggesting their marriage will be one long quarrel.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the play satirise about marriage in the landed gentry?
questions.answer: Through its comedy, 'The Proposal' satirises the marriage customs of the landed gentry, where matches are driven by property and convenience rather than love. Lomov decides to marry Natalya not out of affection but because it is the sensible, suitable thing to do at his age, and because their estates are neighbours. Chubukov is delighted by the proposal largely for his own advantage, and is quick to flatter Lomov as a fine son-in-law one moment and abuse him the next. Natalya, too, is keen to marry once she hears of the proposal, even though she has just quarrelled bitterly with the suitor. The endless squabbling over land and dogs shows that pride, possessions and pettiness, rather than genuine feeling, govern these relationships, and Chekhov mocks this gently but pointedly.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the couple's constant quarrelling suggest about their future marriage?
questions.answer: It suggests their marriage will be full of arguments, but also that, since both are quarrelsome by nature, they may be oddly well-matched and continue together despite the bickering.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does Chekhov make the disputes so trivial?
questions.answer: The trivial subjects — a meadow and dogs — heighten the comedy and expose how easily pride and stubbornness can turn small matters into fierce fights.

questions.answer: Small moments in nature can change our mood and bring hope.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does a small moment in nature change the poet's mood in 'Dust of Snow'?
questions.answer: In 'Dust of Snow', Robert Frost shows how the smallest, most ordinary event can transform a person's state of mind. The poet is having a day full of regret and low spirits when a crow, sitting on a hemlock tree, shakes down a light shower of snow that falls on him. This tiny, accidental touch of nature lifts his heart and changes his mood, rescuing part of a day he had felt was wasted. The beauty of the poem lies in its suggestion that we need not wait for great events to feel better — even a fleeting, unplanned brush with nature can give us hope and lighten our sorrow. The message is to value such small moments, for they carry the power to heal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Discuss the symbolism and irony in 'Dust of Snow'.
questions.answer: Frost makes clever use of symbolism and irony in this short poem. The crow and the hemlock tree are both traditionally associated with darkness, sorrow and even death, so a reader expects them to deepen the poet's gloom. Instead, it is precisely these gloomy images that bring about a positive change: the snow shaken down by the crow from the hemlock lifts the poet's spirits and saves his day. This reversal creates a gentle irony — that something usually linked with sadness becomes a source of hope and renewal. Through this symbolism, Frost suggests that beauty and comfort can be found even in unlikely or unwelcome places, and that a change of heart can come from the most unexpected source.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why might Frost have chosen gloomy symbols like the crow and hemlock for a hopeful poem?
questions.answer: To show that even things usually linked with sadness can bring joy, making the point that hope can come from the most unexpected places.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the poem teach about dealing with a bad day?
questions.answer: That we should stay open to small moments of beauty around us, since even a tiny experience in nature can shift our mood and renew our hope.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: The destructive power of uncontrolled human emotions.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Explain the symbolism of fire and ice and the message of the poem.
questions.answer: In 'Fire and Ice', Robert Frost takes two ideas about how the world might end and turns them into symbols of human emotions. Fire stands for desire, greed and passion — feelings that burn intensely and, like a blaze, can consume and destroy. Ice stands for hatred, coldness and indifference — emotions that are cold and rigid but, like ice, are just as capable of ruin. The poet, having experienced desire, first inclines towards fire as the destroyer, but he adds that hatred, represented by ice, would also be powerful enough to end the world. Through these contrasting symbols, the message becomes clear: both the heat of desire and the chill of hatred are dangerous, and uncontrolled human emotions can bring about destruction. The poem is thus a warning to keep our feelings in check.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Frost convey a serious idea in such a short poem?
questions.answer: One of the remarkable features of 'Fire and Ice' is how much meaning Frost compresses into very few lines. He begins with a familiar debate about whether the world will end in fire or ice, which immediately catches the reader's attention. He then deepens it by linking fire to desire and ice to hatred, so that the literal question about the world becomes a comment on human nature. By using the simple, contrasting images of fire and ice as symbols, he avoids long explanations and lets the images carry the weight of his idea. The brevity actually strengthens the poem: its short, sharp lines mirror the swift, destructive force of the emotions it describes, leaving a strong and lasting impression of his warning about desire and hatred.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the poet consider both desire and hatred equally dangerous?
questions.answer: Because desire (fire) can consume us through greed and passion, while hatred (ice) can destroy through coldness and cruelty — both, when unchecked, lead to ruin.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the poem connect the end of the world with the end of relationships or societies?
questions.answer: By symbolising destruction through human emotions, it suggests that desire and hatred can destroy not just the world but also relationships and societies from within.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Leslie Norris contrast the caged tiger with the wild tiger?
questions.answer: The whole poem is built on a contrast between two images of the tiger. In the zoo, the tiger is shown trapped in a small cage, pacing softly along the same few steps, full of a quiet, suppressed rage. It pays no attention to the visitors and, at night, stares longingly at the stars, a picture of helplessness and lost majesty. Against this, the poet sets the tiger as it would be in the wild: sliding silently through long grass, lurking near water holes to hunt, and terrifying the villagers near the forest's edge with its strength and ferocity. By placing these two pictures side by side, Norris makes the reader feel keenly how unnatural and cruel the tiger's captivity is, for the same creature that should be free and powerful is reduced to restless pacing behind bars.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the message of 'A Tiger in the Zoo', and how does the poet convey it?
questions.answer: The message of the poem is that wild animals suffer deeply when they are taken from their natural homes and kept in captivity, and that caging them is a kind of cruelty. The poet conveys this not by directly lecturing the reader but by showing the contrast between the tiger's caged and wild states. The free tiger is depicted as strong, graceful and fearsome — living as nature intended — while the caged tiger is quiet, restless and ignored, its power and spirit crushed. The image of the tiger staring at the night sky suggests its yearning for the freedom it has lost. Through this sympathetic portrayal, Norris invites readers to feel the injustice of confinement and to reconsider keeping such majestic creatures locked away merely for display.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the caged tiger's silence more powerful than loud roaring would be?
questions.answer: Its silence shows deep, suppressed rage and helplessness, suggesting a spirit broken by captivity, which is more moving than open anger.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the tiger's gazing at the stars suggest?
questions.answer: It suggests the tiger's longing for the freedom and vastness of the wild that it has been denied.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Carolyn Wells create humour in 'How to Tell Wild Animals'?
questions.answer: Carolyn Wells creates humour by taking a serious-sounding idea — how to identify dangerous wild animals — and treating it in a completely absurd way. The poem pretends to give a traveller helpful tips for recognising creatures such as the Asian lion, the Bengal tiger, the leopard and the bear. But the joke is that every method of identification involves the animal attacking, biting, hugging or eating the person, by which time the knowledge would be quite useless. This mock-serious, instructional tone clashes comically with the ridiculous content. Wells adds to the fun with a bouncy rhyme and rhythm, playful wordplay and exaggeration. The result is a light-hearted nonsense poem whose only purpose is to make the reader laugh.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is 'How to Tell Wild Animals' called a nonsense poem, and what is its appeal?
questions.answer: The poem is called a nonsense poem because it is written purely for amusement and offers no sensible information, even though it pretends to instruct. Its 'methods' for telling wild animals apart are deliberately illogical and dangerous, depending on the animal harming the observer, so they could never be applied in real life. The appeal of the poem lies precisely in this absurdity: readers enjoy the gap between the calm, teacherly tone and the alarming, silly advice. The catchy rhythm and rhyme, the comic exaggeration, and the playful treatment of frightening animals all combine to delight rather than inform. It shows that poetry can be valued simply for the pleasure and laughter it gives.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the mock-serious tone make the poem funnier?
questions.answer: Because the calm, instructional manner clashes with the ridiculous, deadly 'advice', and this contrast between style and content produces the humour.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Can a poem be worthwhile if it teaches no lesson? Give your view.
questions.answer: Yes — poems like this show that literature can be valuable for the joy, humour and pleasure it gives, not only for moral lessons.

questions.answer: The boy's childhood and all cherished things that can be lost.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What lesson about loss does the boy learn in 'The Ball Poem'?
questions.answer: In 'The Ball Poem', a young boy loses his ball when it bounces into the water and is gone. Although it is only a ball, the boy is overcome with grief, staring at the place where it vanished. The poet deliberately does not console him by offering money or a new ball, because he understands that the boy is going through an important first experience — learning what it truly means to lose something he loved. Through this small loss, the boy begins to grasp the deeper truth that in life we will lose many things and people, and that lost things cannot always be replaced. He must learn to stand up, accept the loss, and carry on. This understanding of how loss works, which the poet calls the 'epistemology of loss', is a vital and unavoidable lesson of growing up.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Berryman use the lost ball as a symbol in the poem?
questions.answer: Berryman turns an everyday childhood mishap into a powerful symbol. The ball is not merely a toy; it represents the boy's childhood, his cherished possessions, and, by extension, all the things and people we hold dear and may one day lose. When the ball is swept away into the water, its loss stands for the first painful realisation that what we own and love is not permanent. By refusing to replace the ball, the poet allows this symbolic loss to teach the boy a real lesson, rather than masking it with a quick substitute. In this way, a single, ordinary moment becomes a meditation on grief, impermanence and the maturity that comes from learning to cope with loss — showing how a small symbol can carry a very large meaning.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is it important for the boy to feel the loss rather than be given a new ball at once?
questions.answer: Because facing the loss teaches him to understand grief and impermanence, an essential lesson that a replacement would only postpone.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the poem connect a child's small loss with adult life?
questions.answer: It suggests that learning to cope with a small loss prepares a person for the larger losses of people and things they will face as they grow up.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the poem's theme?
questions.answer: A child's longing for freedom away from constant nagging.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does 'Amanda' contrast the adult world with the child's inner world?
questions.answer: The poem is structured around a clear contrast between two worlds. On one side is the voice of an adult, almost certainly a parent, who keeps issuing a stream of instructions and reproaches to Amanda — telling her to sit straight, finish her homework, clean her shoes, stop biting her nails and behave herself. This represents the controlling, fault-finding world of adult demands. On the other side is Amanda's inner world, into which she escapes through daydreams: she imagines herself as a serene mermaid drifting in the sea, as a carefree orphan wandering quiet streets, and as Rapunzel living peacefully in her tower. These dreams are calm, free and beautiful, in sharp contrast to the dull nagging around her. By setting these two voices side by side, Robin Klein shows how a child retreats into imagination to escape the pressures of constant correction.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the poem suggest about the effect of constant nagging on a child?
questions.answer: Through Amanda's response, the poem suggests that constant nagging and over-strict control can be harmful to a child's spirit. Instead of making Amanda more attentive or obedient, the unending instructions only push her to withdraw into her own imagination, where she dreams of freedom and peace far away from the adult voice. Her fantasies of being a mermaid, an orphan or Rapunzel all share a longing to be left alone and free, which shows how stifled and weary she feels. The poem thus gently criticises parenting that relies on continual fault-finding, implying that children need space, freedom and understanding rather than ceaseless correction, and that too much nagging can make a child feel trapped and unhappy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why do all of Amanda's daydreams involve being alone and free?
questions.answer: Because she feels overwhelmed by constant instructions, so she imagines worlds where no one nags her and she can enjoy peace and freedom.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What might the poem be advising parents and adults?
questions.answer: That children need freedom, space and gentle understanding, and that endless nagging can stifle a child's spirit rather than help them.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the poem's theme?
questions.answer: Freedom and the urge to break free from confinement.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Explain the central image and symbolism of 'The Trees'.
questions.answer: The central image of the poem is striking and unusual: trees that have somehow been kept inside a house are shown struggling, through the night, to break free and move out into the empty forest, finally rejoining it by morning. While this image works on a literal level as nature in motion, its real power lies in its symbolism. The trees leaving the confining house can represent nature reclaiming the space that human beings have taken from it. More widely, the poem is read as a symbol of liberation — of women, or of any oppressed or confined person, breaking free from the walls that hold them and returning to where they truly belong. The straining roots and branches stand for the powerful, natural urge for freedom. Thus the poem uses a simple image of moving trees to express a profound idea about the desire to be free.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does 'The Trees' celebrate the desire for freedom?
questions.answer: The poem celebrates freedom by showing the determined, unstoppable movement of the trees from confinement to their natural home. Trapped inside a house, the trees do not remain passive; through the night their roots, leaves and boughs push and strain until they succeed in moving out into the forest. Adrienne Rich uses personification to give the trees a sense of purpose and will, making their escape feel like a deliberate act of liberation, and vivid imagery to make the struggle and release feel real. By the end, the trees have reclaimed their rightful place in the open forest, free at last. Whether we read this as nature freeing itself from human control or as the suppressed winning their freedom, the poem honours the deep, natural longing of living things to break free from confinement and to belong where they are meant to be.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why might a poem about trees actually be about human freedom?
questions.answer: Because the trees' escape from confinement mirrors the human urge to break free from restriction and oppression, so the trees become a symbol of any being seeking freedom.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does it mean that the speaker watches from inside the house?
questions.answer: It can suggest that the speaker too is confined, observing the freedom the trees achieve, which deepens the poem's theme of longing for liberation.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Explain the central metaphor of 'Fog' and why it is effective.
questions.answer: The entire poem 'Fog' is built on a single, vivid metaphor in which Carl Sandburg compares the fog to a cat. The fog is imagined arriving silently and softly, just as a cat steps on quiet paws; it settles over the harbour and the city, seeming to crouch and look around as a cat does; and then, after a short while, it moves on and disappears. This comparison is highly effective because it captures, in one simple image, the essential qualities of fog — its silent arrival, its quiet, watchful presence, and its sudden departure. By likening a natural phenomenon to a familiar animal, the poet allows the reader to see and feel the fog in a fresh and memorable way, proving how a single well-chosen metaphor can convey a great deal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the brevity and form of 'Fog' add to its effect?
questions.answer: 'Fog' is a very short poem written in free verse, with no rhyme or regular metre, and this form suits its subject perfectly. The shortness of the poem mirrors the brief, passing presence of the fog itself, which comes and goes quickly. The free-verse form, flowing without the constraint of rhyme, matches the soft, drifting movement of fog and of a cat. Rather than offering a long argument or an explicit moral, the poem simply presents one clear, beautiful image and then ends, leaving the reader with a lasting impression. In this way, Sandburg shows that poetry need not be long or elaborate to be powerful; a few carefully chosen words and a single striking comparison can capture a moment of nature completely.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is comparing fog to a cat more powerful than simply describing fog plainly?
questions.answer: Because the familiar image of a silent, watchful cat instantly conveys the fog's quiet movement and brief presence, making the description vivid and memorable in a way plain description could not.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does this poem suggest about the power of short poetry?
questions.answer: It suggests that even a very short poem, with one strong image, can capture a scene completely and leave a deep impression, showing that brevity can be a strength.

questions.answer: Appearances versus reality; true courage is proved by deeds.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: The Tale of Custard the Dragon
questions.prompt: How does 'The Tale of Custard the Dragon' show the difference between appearance and reality?
questions.answer: The poem cleverly overturns appearances to reveal reality. Belinda's pets — the kitten Ink, the mouse Blink and the little dog Mustard — are all praised for their great bravery, while the dragon Custard is constantly mocked as a coward who only wishes for a safe place. Appearances suggest that the boastful pets are the brave ones and Custard the timid one. But when a pirate suddenly breaks into the house, reality is revealed: the supposedly brave pets panic and run away or hide, while the 'cowardly' Custard charges fearlessly at the pirate and swallows him, saving everyone. The contrast exposes the truth that those who boast loudest of their courage may have none in a real crisis, while genuine bravery can be found in the one least expected to have it.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: The Tale of Custard the Dragon
questions.prompt: What is the message of the poem, and how does Ogden Nash deliver it humorously?
questions.answer: The message of the poem is that true courage is shown through actions rather than boasts, and that we should not judge others by appearances or loud claims. Ogden Nash delivers this serious idea in a thoroughly humorous way, writing the poem as a light, comic ballad. He gives the pets funny names and exaggerated qualities, and creates strong irony by making the mocked 'coward', Custard, the real hero who eats the pirate, while the boastful pets flee. The bouncy rhythm, playful rhymes, alliteration and gentle exaggeration keep the tone amusing throughout. Even at the end, when the other pets return to their bragging and humble Custard still admits to wishing for a safe corner, the comedy continues, leaving the reader entertained while quietly absorbing the lesson about real versus pretended bravery.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: The Tale of Custard the Dragon
questions.prompt: Why does the poet make the boastful pets flee while the timid dragon fights?
questions.answer: To create irony and humour and to drive home the lesson that boasting about courage means little, while quiet, genuine bravery shows itself in action.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: The Tale of Custard the Dragon
questions.prompt: Is Custard truly a coward? Give your view.
questions.answer: No — though he admits to wanting safety, he acts with real courage when it matters, proving that honesty about one's fears does not make one a coward.

questions.answer: Only God, according to the poem.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the central idea of 'For Anne Gregory'?
questions.answer: The central idea of the poem is the contrast between outer beauty and inner worth, and the question of whether a person can be loved for their true self rather than their appearance. In the poem, the speaker tells the beautiful young Anne Gregory that men are attracted to her for her lovely yellow hair, not for who she really is. Anne responds that she could change her hair colour so that men might love her for herself. But the speaker, citing an old religious teaching, concludes that only God can love a person for their inner self alone, and not for their physical beauty. Through this exchange, Yeats expresses the timeless idea that human love is usually drawn to outward appearance, and that perfect, selfless love — which values the true self — belongs to the divine.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Yeats use the symbol of 'yellow hair' and the dialogue form in the poem?
questions.answer: Yeats builds the poem around the symbol of Anne's 'yellow hair', which stands for physical beauty and outward appearance in general. The discussion about whether Anne should keep or change the colour of her hair is really a debate about whether people are valued for their looks or for their inner selves. By presenting the poem as a dialogue, Yeats makes this debate lively and natural: Anne and the speaker put forward different views, allowing the idea to unfold through conversation rather than statement. The speaker's final reference to an old religious teaching, and to God as the only one who could love Anne for herself alone, gives the poem a thoughtful, almost spiritual conclusion. In this way, the simple image of yellow hair and the back-and-forth of dialogue together carry a profound reflection on beauty, love and inner worth.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the poem say only God can love a person for themselves alone?
questions.answer: Because human love is usually drawn to outward beauty, while only perfect, selfless divine love can value a person's true self with no regard for appearance.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the poem suggest about basing relationships on appearance?
questions.answer: It suggests that love based only on appearance is incomplete, and that valuing a person's inner self is rarer and more meaningful.

questions.answer: Because no surgery was actually performed.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does 'A Triumph of Surgery' show that over-pampering can harm?
questions.answer: The story makes its point through the example of Mrs Pumphrey and her dog Tricki. Out of deep affection, Mrs Pumphrey constantly indulges Tricki with rich food, sweets and treats, ignoring the vet's advice to cut down his diet and give him exercise. The result is that Tricki becomes grossly overweight, lazy and seriously ill — harmed by the very love that was meant to care for him. When the vet finally takes the dog away and simply puts him on a sensible diet with regular exercise, Tricki quickly becomes healthy and energetic again. The contrast between the pampered, sick dog and the recovered, lively one clearly shows that over-pampering and overfeeding do real harm, and that true care means looking after an animal's health sensibly rather than spoiling it.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the title 'A Triumph of Surgery' humorous and ironic?
questions.answer: The title is both humorous and ironic because it suggests a great medical operation, when in fact no surgery of any kind takes place. Tricki's illness is caused simply by overeating and lack of exercise, and his cure is equally simple — a strict diet and plenty of running about with the other dogs at the vet's surgery. There is nothing surgical about it. The irony deepens because it is Mrs Pumphrey herself who, in her overflowing gratitude and tendency to exaggerate, hails the dog's natural recovery as a 'triumph of surgery'. This grand phrase for such an ordinary cure gently mocks her exaggerated devotion and her inability to see that her own indulgence had made the dog ill, adding warmth and comedy to the story.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the story suggest about the right way to show love to a pet?
questions.answer: It suggests that real love means caring for a pet's health sensibly — proper food and exercise — rather than spoiling it with treats that do harm.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why might the vet have chosen not to tell Mrs Pumphrey the plain truth about the cure?
questions.answer: Because she was so devoted and grateful that letting her believe in a 'triumph of surgery' kept her happy and avoided hurting her feelings, while the dog was healthy again.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Anil's kindness and trust change Hari Singh?
questions.answer: Hari Singh begins the story as a practised young thief who befriends Anil only to take advantage of him, even skimming small amounts from the daily shopping. But Anil's behaviour gradually works a change in him. Although not wealthy, Anil generously gives Hari food and, more importantly, begins to teach him to read and write, treating him with warmth and trust rather than suspicion. When Hari finally steals Anil's money and reaches the station to flee, he is stopped not by fear of the police but by his own awakened conscience: he realises that the education and respectability Anil is offering him are worth far more than the stolen cash. He returns the money quietly, and when Anil chooses to say nothing and continues teaching him, this act of trust completes Hari's transformation. Kindness, trust and the gift of learning succeed in reforming him where punishment never could.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the message of 'The Thief's Story' about trust and education?
questions.answer: The story conveys a powerful message about the transforming effect of trust and education. Hari Singh's life is changed not by force or fear but by Anil's compassion: by feeding him, teaching him to read and write, and trusting him even after the theft, Anil reaches the better self hidden within the young thief. Hari comes to value the opportunity to learn and to live honestly far above the money he could steal, and he chooses self-respect over crime. The story suggests that people, especially the young, can be reformed through kindness and the chance to better themselves, and that education in particular opens the door to a new and respectable life. It also shows that trust, freely given, can awaken a person's conscience more effectively than punishment.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does Anil pretend not to know about the theft?
questions.answer: Because he understands that quiet trust will reform Hari far better than anger or punishment, giving the boy a chance to change on his own.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What makes Hari value education over the stolen money?
questions.answer: He realises that being able to read and write can give him a respectable future and self-respect, which money gained by theft never could.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Ausable outwit Max in 'The Midnight Visitor'?
questions.answer: Ausable outwits his rival Max entirely through presence of mind and a cleverly told lie, without ever using force. Returning to his hotel room, Ausable and the writer Fowler find Max waiting with a pistol, demanding a secret report. Instead of panicking, Ausable remains calm and casually complains about a balcony outside his window, claiming that intruders have used it before and that he will report the matter to the hotel management. This makes Max believe such a balcony exists. When a knock comes at the door, Ausable coolly announces that it must be the police he had earlier summoned. The frightened Max, eager to avoid capture, decides to slip out of the window onto the balcony to wait — but there is no balcony at all, and he falls to the ground. The knock, in fact, was only a waiter delivering drinks. Through sheer quick thinking, Ausable removes the threat and recovers control of the situation.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the story suggest about intelligence versus appearance?
questions.answer: The story strongly suggests that intelligence and presence of mind are far more valuable than a glamorous appearance. At the start, the young writer Fowler is disappointed because Ausable, a real secret agent, is fat, ordinary-looking and stays in a plain hotel room, nothing like the dashing spies of films. Yet when genuine danger appears in the form of the armed Max, it is precisely Ausable's calm, quick-thinking mind that saves the day. He defeats a man holding a gun not with weapons or heroics but with a single, well-timed lie about a balcony. The contrast between Ausable's unimpressive looks and his brilliant handling of the crisis drives home the message that true ability lies in the brain, not in outward show, and that we should not judge people by their appearance.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does Ausable's lie about the balcony work so well?
questions.answer: Because he tells it calmly and convincingly as a complaint, so Max believes the balcony is real and, when frightened, uses it to escape — falling to his downfall.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the story challenge the popular image of a spy?
questions.answer: It replaces the glamorous, action-hero spy with an ordinary-looking man whose strength is his clever mind, showing that real skill is mental, not physical.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How is Horace Danby outwitted in 'A Question of Trust'?
questions.answer: Horace Danby, though an experienced thief, is completely outwitted by a cleverer one. Having broken into a house to rob the safe and fund his love of rare books, Horace is interrupted by a charming young woman who pretends to be the lady of the house. Instead of raising an alarm, she scolds him lightly and then cunningly claims that she has forgotten the key to the safe, persuading Horace to open it for her. Crucially, she gets him to take off his gloves so that he can work the lock. Trusting her completely, Horace opens the safe; she takes the jewels and lets him leave, promising to keep quiet. Only later does Horace realise that she was not the owner at all but another thief who had used him to do the dangerous work. Because he had removed his gloves, his fingerprints are on the safe, and he is arrested for the robbery, while the real thief escapes with the jewels.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the story titled 'A Question of Trust', and how does irony work in it?
questions.answer: The title 'A Question of Trust' points directly to the story's central concern: the matter of whom one can safely trust. Horace, a careful and rather respectable thief, makes the fatal mistake of trusting a stranger who seems charming and authoritative, and that misplaced trust destroys him. The story is rich in irony. It is ironic that a thief, who himself deceives others for a living, is so easily deceived by another; ironic that his trust in the young woman leads to his arrest; and ironic that he is punished for a robbery whose rewards were carried off by someone else. The reader gradually grasps the truth before or alongside Horace, which heightens the effect. Through this clever, ironic twist, the story warns that charm and appearances can be deeply deceptive, and that trust given without care can prove very costly.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does Horace believe the young woman so easily?
questions.answer: Because she behaves with the confidence and manner of the house's owner and is charming, so he does not suspect she is a fellow thief.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the poetic justice in Horace's fate?
questions.answer: A thief who profits by deceiving others is himself deceived and punished, which feels like a fitting, ironic justice.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Griffin misuse his scientific discovery in 'Footprints without Feet'?
questions.answer: Griffin's discovery of how to make himself invisible is an extraordinary scientific achievement, but he uses it almost entirely for wrongdoing. Instead of putting his genius to good use, the lawless and selfish Griffin turns invisibility into a tool for crime. He breaks into shops and steals goods and money, and when he needs to escape, he even sets a shop on fire. He uses stolen money to pay for a room at an inn in Iping, and when his thefts are discovered and people try to seize him, he removes his disguise to vanish and fights his way free, harming those who oppose him. At every turn, Griffin chooses theft, deception and violence over honesty or benefit to others. His misuse of invisibility shows how a remarkable scientific gift can become dangerous in the hands of someone without morals.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What warning about science does the story convey through Griffin?
questions.answer: Through the figure of Griffin, the story delivers a clear warning about the misuse of science and the danger of knowledge that is not guided by responsibility and good values. Griffin is undoubtedly brilliant — he achieves what others could only dream of by making himself invisible. Yet because he is lawless, selfish and quick to violence, his invention brings harm rather than good: it enables theft, arson and assault, and it makes him a menace to ordinary people. The story suggests that scientific power is neither good nor bad in itself, but becomes dangerous when wielded by someone without conscience. H.G. Wells thus reminds readers that science must be used wisely and responsibly, for the benefit of society, and that great ability without good character can be a curse rather than a blessing.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How might Griffin's discovery have helped people if used responsibly?
questions.answer: If guided by good values, such scientific genius could have been used for medicine, research or other benefits to society, rather than for crime.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the story make Griffin unsympathetic despite his brilliance?
questions.answer: Because he uses his gift only for selfish, harmful ends, showing that intelligence without morality earns no admiration.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What qualities made Richard Ebright a successful scientist?
questions.answer: Richard Ebright's success as a scientist grew from a combination of qualities. First, he had a first-rate mind, sharp and capable of deep thinking. Second, and most importantly, he possessed an intense curiosity that drove him from childhood to collect, observe and ask questions about the natural world, and to carry out real experiments to find answers — for example, into the purpose of the gold spots on a monarch pupa. Third, he had a strong will to win and to excel, but for the right reasons: his ambition was rooted in a genuine love of science rather than a hunger for mere fame. He also learnt valuable lessons from early experiences, including a science-fair setback that taught him the difference between flashy displays and true investigation. Together, these qualities — intelligence, curiosity, hard work and the right motivation — made him a true scientist.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does encouragement help in the making of a scientist, according to the story?
questions.answer: The story strongly emphasises that encouragement and guidance are vital in shaping a scientist. Richard Ebright's natural curiosity might never have flowered without the support he received, especially from his mother, who took him on trips, spent time with him, and gave him books, microscopes and other equipment to feed his interest. A simple book about a monarch butterfly proved to be a turning point, opening up the world of science to him. His teachers and the opportunities of science fairs further developed his abilities and taught him important lessons. The story thus shows that talent alone is not enough; it needs to be nurtured with encouragement, resources and the right opportunities. With such support, a child's curiosity can be guided into the disciplined inquiry that makes a real scientist.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is curiosity considered the most important quality for a scientist?
questions.answer: Because it drives a person to keep asking questions and seeking real answers through experiment, which is the heart of scientific discovery.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does Ebright's science-fair failure teach about learning?
questions.answer: It teaches that setbacks can be valuable lessons; his failure taught him to do genuine experiments rather than just neat displays, improving him as a scientist.

questions.answer: To appear rich and elegant at a grand party.

questions.answer: They toil for ten years to repay the debt for a replacement necklace.

questions.answer: The borrowed necklace was a fake, worth very little.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does Matilda's vanity lead to her downfall in 'The Necklace'?
questions.answer: Matilda Loisel's downfall springs directly from her vanity and discontent. Though she lives comfortably enough, she is never satisfied, constantly longing for the wealth, fine clothes and jewels she does not have. When her husband secures an invitation to a grand party, her craving to appear rich leads her to borrow a glittering diamond necklace from her friend Madame Forestier. She enjoys a glorious evening, but loses the necklace on the way home. Here her pride proves fatal: too ashamed to admit the loss, she and her husband secretly replace it with an extremely expensive one, plunging into crippling debt. To repay it, the couple endure ten years of grinding poverty and hard labour, and Matilda loses the very beauty she had prized. Her vanity, and her refusal to be honest, thus cost her the best years of her life — a heavy price for a moment of pride.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Discuss the irony in 'The Necklace'.
questions.answer: Irony lies at the very heart of 'The Necklace' and gives the story its powerful ending. The greatest irony is that the entire decade of suffering endured by the Loisels was completely unnecessary: the diamond necklace Matilda borrowed and so carefully replaced at enormous cost turns out, in the final lines, to have been a cheap imitation worth very little. There is further irony in the fact that Matilda, who longed for wealth and luxury, ends up far poorer and harder-worn than before, having lost her youth and beauty through years of toil. It is also ironic that her attempt to hide a small misfortune, out of pride, created a far greater one. This masterly twist, revealed only at the end, turns a simple tale into a memorable lesson on the dangers of vanity and dishonesty.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How might the story have ended differently if Matilda had told the truth at once?
questions.answer: Had she confessed the loss to Madame Forestier immediately, she would likely have learned the necklace was fake and been spared the ten years of needless hardship.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the story say about being content with what one has?
questions.answer: It suggests that discontent and craving for luxury can lead to ruin, and that being honest and content with one's means brings a more secure and peaceful life.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does education transform Bholi in the story?
questions.answer: Education completely transforms Bholi, whose real name is Sulekha. At the beginning she is a neglected, timid girl: a childhood attack of smallpox has scarred her face and she stammers, so her family considers her backward and dull, mocking and ignoring her. When a girls' school opens in the village and she is sent there, a kind and encouraging teacher patiently helps her, building her confidence and helping her learn to speak and study. Over the years, Bholi grows from a silent, fearful child into a confident, self-respecting young woman. The full extent of her transformation is revealed at her wedding, when she boldly refuses to marry the greedy Bishamber, who demands a dowry. The neglected girl who once dared not speak now stands up before everyone and claims her dignity — a change made possible entirely by education.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What social messages does the story 'Bholi' convey?
questions.answer: 'Bholi' carries strong social messages. Most importantly, it shows the transforming power of education, especially for girls: schooling turns the neglected, mocked Bholi into a confident woman able to defend her own dignity, suggesting that education can uplift even the most disadvantaged child. The story also sharply criticises the dowry system. The groom Bishamber's heartless demand for a large dowry exposes the greed and injustice behind the practice, and Bholi's refusal to marry such a man is a powerful protest against it. In addition, the story condemns the neglect and low value placed on girls within families, as seen in how Bholi is treated before her education. Through Bholi's example, K.A. Abbas urges society to educate its girls and reject evils like dowry, so that girls can live with self-respect.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is Bholi's refusal to marry Bishamber a turning point?
questions.answer: Because it shows that education has given the once-timid girl the confidence and self-respect to reject injustice and decide her own life, reversing her family's low expectations.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the story link a girl's education with social change?

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: The Book That Saved the Earth
questions.prompt: How does a book of nursery rhymes save the Earth in the play?
questions.answer: In this humorous science-fiction play, the Earth is saved by a book of Mother Goose nursery rhymes through a comic misunderstanding. A Martian crew lands and enters a library as part of a plan to invade Earth, taking orders from their proud, foolish leader Think-Tank back on Mars. The crew finds a book, but since the Martians do not know what books are, they badly misinterpret it. When the silly nursery rhymes are read out and reported to Think-Tank, he takes them literally: a rhyme about a cow jumping over the moon convinces him that Earthlings can leap into space, and other rhymes make him believe the people of Earth are extremely advanced and intend to attack Mars. Frightened by this imagined threat, Think-Tank hastily cancels the invasion and orders his crew to retreat. Thus a harmless children's book, completely misread, frightens the Martians away and saves the planet.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: The Book That Saved the Earth
questions.prompt: What does the play suggest about the power and importance of books?
questions.answer: Although it is a light comedy, the play makes a serious point about the power and importance of books. Its very title declares that a book saved the Earth, and the action bears this out, even if in a funny way. The Martians, who have no understanding of books, are so affected by a single volume of nursery rhymes that their entire invasion collapses. The play playfully suggests that books contain ideas powerful enough to change the course of events, and that a civilisation that values and understands books is to be respected — and even feared. By turning the salvation of the world on a book, the playwright reminds the audience, especially young readers, that books are precious and influential, holding knowledge and power far greater than their humble appearance might suggest.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: The Book That Saved the Earth
questions.prompt: Why is it funny that nursery rhymes frighten the Martians?
questions.answer: Because the rhymes are harmless and meant for children, yet the Martians' ignorance makes them read terrifying meanings into them, creating comic irony.

questions.subject: english
questions.chapterTitle: The Book That Saved the Earth
questions.prompt: What does Think-Tank's behaviour reveal about foolish leaders?
questions.answer: It shows that vain, self-important leaders who do not understand things can make foolish decisions based on misunderstanding and fear.

questions.answer: Formal/informal letters and articles (also notices, paragraphs, stories).

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Explain how to change direct speech into reported (indirect) speech.
questions.answer: To change direct speech into reported speech, we report what a person said without using their exact words or quotation marks. Three main changes are usually needed. First, the tense generally shifts one step back: present tenses become past (am/is becomes was, go becomes went), and 'will' becomes 'would'. Second, pronouns are changed to suit the point of view of the speaker and listener (for example, 'I' may become 'he' or 'she', and 'you' may become 'I' or 'me'). Third, words showing time and place are changed appropriately: 'now' becomes 'then', 'today' becomes 'that day', 'here' becomes 'there', and 'tomorrow' becomes 'the next day'. The reporting verb and structure also change with the type of sentence — statements use 'said that', questions use 'asked' with a change in word order, and commands use 'told' with 'to' before the verb. Following these rules turns a quotation into correct reported speech.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Distinguish between the active and passive voice and explain when the passive is used.
questions.answer: The difference between active and passive voice lies in what the subject of the sentence does. In the active voice, the subject performs the action — for example, 'The boy kicked the ball', where the boy is doing the kicking. In the passive voice, the subject receives the action, and the sentence is rearranged so that the object of the active sentence becomes the subject — 'The ball was kicked by the boy'. The passive is formed using the appropriate form of the verb 'be' followed by the past participle of the main verb, with the doer often introduced by 'by'. The passive voice is preferred when the action or its receiver is more important than the doer, when the doer is unknown or obvious, or when we wish to sound formal or impersonal, as in notices, reports and scientific writing. Knowing how to switch between the two voices accurately is an important grammar skill for the exam.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why must the verb form change when the subject changes from singular to plural?
questions.answer: Because subject–verb agreement requires the verb to match the number of its subject, so 'he goes' becomes 'they go' to keep the sentence grammatically correct.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why might a writer choose the passive voice in a notice or report?
questions.answer: To focus on the action or result rather than the doer, and to sound formal and impersonal, which suits official writing.

questions.answer: ১) অমল একটি ছোটো গ্রামে থাকে। ২) গরমকালে সে আমগাছের ছায়ায় বসে বই পড়ে।

questions.answer: না; উত্তর জানতে হলে অনুচ্ছেদটি মন দিয়ে পড়তে হবে।

questions.answer: না; উত্তর জানতে হলে অনুচ্ছেদটি মন দিয়ে পড়তে হবে।

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: A naming word for a person, place or thing is a ___.
questions.explanation: A naming word is a noun.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which one is a proper noun?
questions.explanation: Kolkata is a particular name, so it is a proper noun.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which one is a common noun?
questions.explanation: ‘river’ is a general name — a common noun.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: A proper noun always begins with a ___ letter.
questions.explanation: Proper nouns begin with a capital letter.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘dog’ is a ___ noun.
questions.explanation: ‘dog’ is a general name — a common noun.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Give one proper noun.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Is ‘teacher’ common or proper?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: From ‘Rahul lives in Delhi.’, pick the common and proper nouns.
questions.answer: Proper nouns: Rahul, Delhi. (There is no common noun here.)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does ‘Ganga’ start with a capital letter but ‘river’ does not?
questions.answer: ‘Ganga’ is a proper noun (a particular river), so it takes a capital; ‘river’ is a common noun.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Words like my, your and his show who something ___.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘This is ___ book.’ (it belongs to me) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘Rahul lost ___ pen.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘Mira found ___ bag.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which word shows belonging?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which possessive word is for many people (belongs to them)?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘The dog wagged ___ tail.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Fill in: ‘I have ___ bag, you have ___ bag, and she has ___ bag.’
questions.answer: I have my bag, you have your bag, and she has her bag.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is ‘Mira lost his bag’ wrong (if the bag is Mira’s)?
questions.answer: Mira is a girl, so it should be ‘her bag’ — ‘Mira lost her bag.’

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: A word that describes a noun is an ___.
questions.explanation: A describing word for a noun is an adjective.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In ‘a tall boy’, which is the adjective?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: To compare two things we often add ___ to the adjective.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The comparing word for ‘big’ (two things) is ___.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘This is the ___ tree of all.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Give an adjective for ‘mango’.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘small’ → comparing two?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write big, bigger and biggest in three short sentences.
questions.answer: This box is big. That box is bigger. This one is the biggest.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Ravi and Sam are being compared. Should we say ‘taller’ or ‘tallest’?
questions.answer: ‘taller’ — because only two people are being compared.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The present continuous shows an action happening ___.
questions.explanation: It shows an action happening now.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: It is formed with am/is/are + verb + ___.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘She is ___ a book.’ (read) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘They ___ playing.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘I ___ singing.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Add -ing: ‘jump’ → ?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘He ___ running.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write what is happening now using present continuous: (I / write), (they / sing).
questions.answer: I am writing. They are singing.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is ‘She reading a book’ not complete?
questions.answer: It needs a helper word: ‘She is reading a book.’ (is + reading)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: A word that tells us more about a verb is an ___.
questions.explanation: A word that tells more about a verb is an adverb.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In ‘She runs quickly’, which is the adverb?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Many adverbs of manner end in ___.
questions.explanation: quickly, slowly, softly — they end in -ly.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘He spoke ___.’ (in a soft way) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: An adverb can tell us how, when or ___ an action happens.
questions.explanation: Adverbs tell how, when or where.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Change ‘slow’ into an adverb.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Is ‘here’ an adverb?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Use ‘quickly’ and ‘today’ in two short sentences.
questions.answer: The boy ran quickly. We will meet today.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In ‘a quick runner’ and ‘runs quickly’, which has the adverb?
questions.answer: ‘runs quickly’ — ‘quickly’ describes the verb ‘runs’; ‘quick’ in the first describes the noun, so it is an adjective.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: A word that shows where something is — like in, on, under — is a ___.
questions.explanation: Such a position word is a preposition.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘The cat is ___ the mat.’ (touching the top) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘The ball is ___ the table.’ (below it) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘The pen is ___ the box.’ (inside) — fill in.

questions.prompt: Which one is a preposition?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which preposition means ‘inside’?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘The bag is ___ the door.’ (at the back) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Use ‘on’, ‘under’ and ‘between’ in three short sentences.
questions.answer: The book is on the desk. The shoes are under the bed. She sat between her friends.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: A cat is sitting on top of a box. Which is wrong — ‘in the box’ or ‘on the box’?
questions.answer: ‘in the box’ is wrong; the cat is on the box (on top), not inside.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘was’ and ‘were’ are the past forms of ___.
questions.explanation: was/were are the past of am/is/are.

questions.prompt: ‘She ___ happy.’ (past) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘They ___ playing.’ (past) — fill in.

questions.prompt: ‘I ___ at home yesterday.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘We ___ tired after the game.’ — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘He ___ late.’ (past) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘You ___ right.’ (past) — fill in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Fill in: ‘I ___ happy. She ___ here. They ___ busy.’ (past)
questions.answer: I was happy. She was here. They were busy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is ‘They was playing’ wrong?
questions.answer: ‘They’ is more than one, so it takes ‘were’ — ‘They were playing.’

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: To understand a passage, we should read it ___.

questions.explanation: The passage says Sam has a pet dog.

questions.prompt: (passage) What is the dog’s name?

options: run and play with a ball
questions.answer: run and play with a ball
questions.explanation: The passage says Rocky loves to run and play with a ball.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is a passage?
questions.answer: A short piece of writing with a few sentences.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: (passage) Where does Sam take Rocky?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: ‘Sam has a pet dog named Rocky. Every evening, Sam takes Rocky to the park.’ — Write two facts about Sam.
questions.answer: 1) Sam has a pet dog named Rocky. 2) Sam takes Rocky to the park every evening.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Can you answer ‘What is the dog’s name?’ without reading the passage?
questions.answer: No — we must read the passage to find that the dog is named Rocky.

questions.answer: ৩ দশ হাজার ৬ হাজার ২ শতক ৫ দশক ৪ একক।

questions.prompt: ২৩ × ১২ আর ১২ × ২৩ — গুণফল কি একই?
