questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Rahul exclaimed in glee that India is finally a ______ in cricket.
questions.explanation: After India beat England in a close finish, Rahul called India a superpower in cricket.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did Rahul's father hand him?
questions.explanation: His father had preserved a paper-cutting from a newspaper of 26th June 1983 since he was in Class Seven.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The 1983 World Cup final was played at ______.
questions.explanation: Lord's, the famous cricket ground in England, was fully packed for the final on 25th June 1983.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which team won the toss in the final?
questions.explanation: India lost the toss and was made to bat first — so the West Indies won the toss.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How many runs did India score in the final?
questions.explanation: India was all out for 183 runs in 54.4 overs; the West Indies made only 140.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who was the highest scorer for India in the final?
questions.explanation: Srikkanth's 38 runs from 57 balls was India's top score in the final.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Kapil Dev ran about 20 yards to take the catch of ______.
questions.explanation: Kapil Dev's running catch dismissed Vivian Richards, the West Indies' top scorer — the most memorable moment of the match.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who was declared the Man of the Match in the 1983 final?
questions.explanation: Amarnath scored 26 runs and took 3 wickets for just 12 runs — an all-round performance.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The word 'invincible' means ______.
questions.explanation: Word Trove: invincible = unbeatable. The West Indies were thought invincible before 1983.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: India won the final by ______.
questions.explanation: West Indies were bowled out for 140 chasing 184, so India won by 183 − 140 = 43 runs.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why was Rahul very excited at the beginning of the lesson?
questions.answer: He had just watched India beat England in a close cricket finish, so he exclaimed in glee that India is now a superpower in cricket.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: When and why did Rahul's father cut out the newspaper piece?
questions.answer: He cut it from a newspaper of 26th June 1983 — the day after India won its first World Cup — when he was a student of Class Seven, and kept it ever since as a memory.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: When and where was the 1983 World Cup final played?
questions.answer: On 25th June 1983, at Lord's, the famous cricket ground in England.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name the four great West Indies fast bowlers mentioned in the lesson.
questions.answer: Andy Roberts, Malcolm Marshall, Joel Garner and Michael Holding.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which three Indian batsmen hit sixes in the final?
questions.answer: Krishnamachari Srikkanth, Sandeep Patil and Madan Lal — one six each.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the Indian bowlers make the best use of?
questions.answer: The weather and the pitch conditions in England.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which two Indian bowlers took three wickets each?

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: True or False: (i) Rahul's father does not like sports. (ii) In 1983 the World Cup matches were played in England.
questions.answer: (i) False — he is a great cricket-lover. (ii) True — the final was played at Lord's in England.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What was the gap between two World Cup tournaments?
questions.answer: Four years — the 1983 final came four years after the 1979 final.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe India's innings in the 1983 World Cup final.
questions.answer: India lost the toss and was made to bat first against the world's strongest bowling attack — Roberts, Marshall, Garner and Holding. The batting largely crumbled; only Srikkanth (38 from 57 balls) and Amarnath (26 from 80) offered a real fight at the top, while the lower order showed courage. India was all out for 183 in 54.4 overs, with just three sixes — by Srikkanth, Sandeep Patil and Madan Lal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did India win the final despite scoring only 183 runs?
questions.answer: The Indian bowlers made the best use of the weather and pitch conditions and bowled the great West Indies line-up out for a mere 140 runs in 52 overs. Kapil Dev took a famous running catch of about 20 yards to dismiss top-scorer Vivian Richards, and Amarnath took 3 wickets for only 12 runs. India won by 43 runs — one of the most incredible upsets in cricket history.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why was Mohinder Amarnath declared the Man of the Match? Write four sentences describing his heroics (Activity 14).
questions.answer: 1) Amarnath fought hard at the top of the innings and scored 26 valuable runs from 80 balls against fearsome fast bowling. 2) With the ball, he took 3 important West Indies wickets. 3) He gave away only 12 runs in his seven overs of very tight bowling. 4) For this all-round performance with both bat and ball he was declared the Man of the Match.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What effect did the 1983 victory have on India, according to the lesson?
questions.answer: The Indian team stunned the whole world by defeating the invincible West Indies, and from then on cricket started gaining rapid popularity in India. The win filled a generation — like Rahul's father, then in Class Seven — with pride and excitement, a feeling so strong that he preserved the newspaper cutting for decades and passed the story on to his son.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the writer call the 1983 win 'one of the most incredible upsets in cricket history'?
questions.answer: Because the West Indies were considered invincible — unbeatable champions with the world's strongest bowling — while India had scored only a small total of 183. When the weaker side defeated the unbeatable side, it was an upset that was hard to believe, hence 'incredible'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The father kept a paper-cutting for so many years. What does this tell you about him? Do you keep any memory like this?
questions.answer: It shows his deep love for cricket and how precious that day was to him — the cutting is a treasured memory of his childhood joy. (Students may add their own example: tickets, photographs, certificates or newspaper pieces they have kept.)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: If India had won the toss, do you think the story might have been different? Give a reason.
questions.answer: Possibly — India was made to bat first on a difficult pitch. But the lesson suggests the real difference was effort: the batsmen's courage and the bowlers' clever use of conditions. Good teams turn even unfavourable situations into victories.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who led the British expedition to Mt. Everest in 1953?
questions.explanation: Colonel John Hunt led the expedition and selected the team of eleven experienced climbers.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Edmund Hillary was a climber from ______.
questions.explanation: Hillary was from New Zealand; Tenzing Norgay was a Sherpa from India.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How many climbers were chosen for the expedition?
questions.explanation: Hunt selected eleven experienced climbers; only four of them would attempt the summit.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The first summit team was made up of ______.
questions.explanation: Bourdillon and Evans formed the first team; Hillary and Norgay made up the second.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did the first team turn back?
questions.explanation: About 300 feet short of the summit, bad weather forced Bourdillon and Evans to turn back.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: On 29th May 1953, Hillary spent two hours ______.
questions.explanation: Hillary discovered his boots had frozen overnight and spent two hours removing the ice.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The particularly difficult rock face is now called ______.
questions.explanation: Hillary found a way up the difficult rock face, so it was named 'Hillary's Step' after him.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: At what time did Hillary and Norgay reach the summit?
questions.explanation: They left camp at 6:30 a.m. and stood on the summit at 11:30 a.m.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why could the two men stay only 15 minutes at the top?
questions.explanation: At the top of the world the air is too thin to breathe, and their bottled oxygen supply was low.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who became the youngest Indian to climb Mount Everest in 2011?
questions.explanation: On 20th May 2011, Arjun Vajpai climbed Everest at the age of 16 years and 11 months.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which is the highest mountain peak in the world, and where is it located?
questions.answer: Mount Everest (8,850 m) — it stands on the border of Nepal and China.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who was the leader of the British Everest expedition of 1953? (Activity 3a)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How many climbers were chosen for the expedition, and how many got the chance to climb to the top? (Activity 3b–c)
questions.answer: Eleven climbers were chosen; only four got the chance to attempt the summit, in two teams of two.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who were the members of the first team? (Activity 3d)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How high did the first team climb?
questions.answer: Up to about 300 feet short of the summit — at that time the highest point any human being had yet reached.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did Hillary and Norgay leave camp as late as 6:30 a.m.?
questions.answer: Because Hillary's boots had frozen overnight and he had to spend two hours defrosting them.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did Norgay respond when Hillary reached out to shake his hand?
questions.answer: Norgay gave him a hug in return — a warm embrace of shared joy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the two men do during their 15 minutes at the top?
questions.answer: They took photographs and enjoyed the view, and Norgay placed a food offering to God.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who is the oldest woman to climb Mt. Everest, and when did she do it?
questions.answer: Anna Czerwinska — on 22nd May 2000, at the age of 50.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe the events of 29th May 1953 in your own words.
questions.answer: At 4 a.m. Hillary and Norgay awoke and got ready for their climb, but Hillary found that his boots had frozen and spent two hours defrosting them. They left camp at 6:30 a.m. On the way they met a particularly difficult rock face; Hillary found a way up it, and it is now called 'Hillary's Step'. At 11:30 a.m. they reached the summit of Mt. Everest. Hillary offered a handshake, but Norgay hugged him. Because their oxygen supply was low they stayed only 15 minutes — taking photographs, enjoying the view, and placing a food offering to God — before making their way back down.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write about the first team's attempt. Why is it important even though it failed?
questions.answer: The first team of Tom Bourdillon and Charles Evans left on 26th May 1953 and climbed to about 300 feet short of the summit — higher than any human being had ever gone before. Bad weather forced them to turn back. Their attempt was important because it proved the route could be climbed, raised the courage of the second team, and showed that even a 'failed' attempt can break records and prepare the path for success.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write five sentences describing the route map of Tenzing and Hillary (Activity 8).
questions.answer: 1) Hillary and Tenzing started their final climb at 6:30 a.m. 2) The 1953 route began on the Nepal side of the mountain along the Khumbu Glacier. 3) The route passed through the Western Cwm towards the upper slopes. 4) The climbers crossed the South Summit, which stands at 8,748 metres. 5) Finally they reached the summit of Mount Everest, 8,850 metres high, at 11:30 a.m.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the lesson called 'A Feat on Feet'? What qualities helped Hillary and Norgay succeed?
questions.answer: A 'feat' is an achievement of great courage and skill, and this feat was performed entirely on foot — so the title is a clever play on the words feat/feet. The climbers succeeded through months of careful planning, the teamwork of eleven climbers supporting four, patience and perseverance (two hours spent defrosting boots instead of giving up), skill (finding a way up Hillary's Step), and humility — they shared the victory with a hug and a thanksgiving offering rather than boasting.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Only four out of eleven climbers got the chance to attempt the summit. Was the work of the other seven wasted? Give reasons.
questions.answer: No. The whole team carried supplies, set up camps and prepared the route for months; without their support no summit attempt was possible. The lesson shows that behind every famous achievement stands a team whose names are less known but whose work is vital.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Norgay placed a food offering to God at the summit. What does this action tell us about him?
questions.answer: It shows his humility and gratitude — instead of only celebrating his own triumph, he gave thanks. For the Sherpas the mountain is sacred, and Norgay treated the summit with respect, not just as a prize to be conquered.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Anna Czerwinska climbed Everest at 50 and Arjun Vajpai at 16. What lesson do these two records teach together?
questions.answer: Together they show that neither youth nor age is a barrier to great achievements — what matters is preparation, courage and determination. The mountain treats a 16-year-old and a 50-year-old alike; willpower decides who reaches the top.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Phulmani is a ______ girl.
questions.explanation: The lesson opens by telling us Phulmani is a Santhal girl who lives in a village.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The rock paintings of Bhimbetka are more than ______ years old.
questions.explanation: Bhimbetka's rock paintings, in red, white, green and yellow, are more than twenty thousand years old.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In which state are the Ajanta and Ellora caves located?
questions.explanation: Both the Ajanta and Ellora caves are in Maharashtra.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: A 'fresco' is ______.
options: a painting on the plaster of a wall
questions.answer: a painting on the plaster of a wall
questions.explanation: Word Trove: fresco = a painting on the plaster of a wall, like those at Ajanta.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Bhangra is the folk dance of which state?
questions.explanation: Bhangra is loved in Punjab; Dandiya–Gujarat, Bihu–Assam, Chhau–West Bengal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which dance belongs to West Bengal?
questions.explanation: Chhau is the folk dance of West Bengal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Emperor Akbar loved to listen to the songs of ______.
questions.explanation: Indian kings patronised music; Akbar loved to listen to the songs of Tansen.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The Taj Mahal was built by ______ in memory of Mumtaz Mahal.
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal at Agra beside the river Yamuna, in memory of Mumtaz Mahal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The Buland Darwaza, a huge gateway at Fatehpur Sikri, was built by ______.
questions.explanation: The Buland Darwaza is the huge gateway built by Emperor Akbar at Fatehpur Sikri.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In 'Phulmani is a girl. She loves to dance', the word 'She' is a ______.
questions.explanation: 'She' stands in place of the noun Phulmani, so it is a personal pronoun (feminine gender).

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who is Phulmani and where does she live?
questions.answer: Phulmani is a Santhal girl who lives in a village whose houses are painted with trees, flowers and birds.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which country has a rich heritage of classical dance? (Activity 5.1)
questions.answer: India has a rich heritage of classical dance.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is an essential part of Indian classical dance? (Activity 5.2)
questions.answer: Dramatics (acting out a mythological narrative) is an essential part of the Indian classical dance tradition.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who loved to listen to the songs of Tansen? (Activity 5.3)
questions.answer: Emperor Akbar loved to listen to the songs of Tansen.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which monument in India is among the seven wonders of the world? (Activity 5.4)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is Buland Darwaza? (Activity 5.5)
questions.answer: It is the huge gateway built by Emperor Akbar at Fatehpur Sikri.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name the folk theatres of (a) Maharashtra, (b) Bengal, (c) North India. (Activity 5.6)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name two caves famous for ancient Indian paintings and sculptures.
questions.answer: The Ajanta and Ellora caves in Maharashtra.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which musical instruments can Phulmani sing in harmony with?
questions.answer: The sitar, sarengi (sarangi), tabla and drum, among others.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe what Phulmani learns about ancient Indian painting.
questions.answer: Phulmani learns that even in ancient India people painted inside caves and on the rocks of hills. The rock paintings of Bhimbetka in Madhya Pradesh are more than twenty thousand years old, coloured red, white, green and yellow. Her teacher shows her a famous fresco — a painting on a wall's plaster — from the Ajanta caves, made almost two thousand years ago. The Ajanta and Ellora caves in Maharashtra also hold images of Hindu gods and goddesses, tales of Lord Buddha and sculptures of Jainism and Buddhism, showing how India's religions appear together.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write about the variety of dances in India mentioned in the lesson.
questions.answer: India has a rich heritage of classical dance, each based on a mythological narrative, danced in colourful dress in harmony with music: Kathak from North India; Bharatanatyam, Mohiniyattam, Kuchipudi and Kathakali from South India; and Manipuri and Odissi from Eastern India. India also has lively folk dances — Bhangra in Punjab, Dandiya in Gujarat, Bihu in Assam and Chhau in West Bengal. Such a wide variety of dance cannot be found anywhere else in the world.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write five sentences about the Taj Mahal using the hints (Activity 8): situated at Agra; beside river Yamuna; built of marble; tomb of Shah Jahan and Mumtaz Mahal; one of the seven wonders of the world.
questions.answer: 1) The Taj Mahal is situated at Agra. 2) It stands beside the river Yamuna. 3) It is built of beautiful white marble. 4) It was built by Emperor Shah Jahan as a tomb for his queen Mumtaz Mahal. 5) The Taj Mahal is one of the seven wonders of the world.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is Phulmani proud to be an Indian?
questions.answer: Phulmani is proud to be an Indian because she discovers how rich and varied her country's heritage is — ancient rock and cave paintings at Bhimbetka, Ajanta and Ellora; many classical and folk dances; folk theatre like jatra; great music patronised by kings such as Akbar; and wonderful monuments like the Taj Mahal, the Red Fort and the Buland Darwaza. She feels that such a wealth of art, music, dance and architecture, with many religions living together, cannot be found anywhere else, and she hopes one day to travel all over India to see it.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The lesson shows Hinduism, Buddhism and Jainism appearing together at Ajanta and Ellora. Why is this important?
questions.answer: It shows India's tradition of 'unity in diversity' — different religions sharing the same spaces and inspiring the same artists. It teaches respect: many faiths can live together and even enrich one another's art and culture.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Phulmani's own village houses are painted with folk art, yet she is amazed by Ajanta and the Taj Mahal. What does this tell us about art in India?
questions.answer: It shows that art in India is not only in famous monuments but also in ordinary homes and villages. From a mud wall painting to a marble wonder, creativity runs through everyday life — heritage belongs to common people too, not just to kings.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why does the lesson connect a village girl, Phulmani, with the great heritage of the whole country?
questions.answer: Because heritage belongs to everyone. By seeing India through a simple Santhal girl's eyes, the lesson tells every child — however ordinary their background — that this rich culture is theirs to enjoy, be proud of, and protect.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What task did Tarun's teacher give the class?
options: To write a paragraph on the Taj Mahal
To draw a map of India
questions.answer: To write a paragraph on the Taj Mahal
questions.explanation: The teacher asked the students to write a paragraph on the Taj Mahal, which led Tarun to ask his grandfather.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who told Tarun the story of the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: His grandfather, a retired teacher who knew a lot about the monument, told him the story.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Prince Khurram was the grandson of ______.
questions.explanation: Khurram was the son of Jehangir and the grandson of Akbar the Great.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where did Khurram first see the beautiful girl?
questions.explanation: Khurram caught a glimpse of Arjumand Banu Begum selling silk and glass beads at the Meena Bazaar.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: When Khurram became emperor in 1628, he was known as ______.
questions.explanation: He took the name Shah Jahan, meaning 'the brightest crown of the world'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the name 'Mumtaz Mahal' replace?
questions.explanation: Arjumand Banu Begum was renamed Mumtaz Mahal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: A 'mausoleum' is ______.
options: a building built to house the dead
questions.answer: a building built to house the dead
questions.explanation: Tarun's grandfather explains that a mausoleum is a building built to house the dead.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How long did it take to build the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: It took 22 years and 22,000 workers to build the Taj Mahal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The Taj Mahal was built entirely of ______.
questions.explanation: It was built entirely of white marble brought from all over Asia.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In 'It is a beautiful monument', the word 'beautiful' is a/an ______.
questions.explanation: 'beautiful' describes the noun 'monument', so it is an adjective.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did Tarun ask his grandfather about the Taj Mahal?
questions.answer: His teacher had asked the class to write a paragraph on the Taj Mahal, and Tarun did not know much about it.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: On what condition did the grandfather agree to tell the story?
questions.answer: He agreed if Tarun promised to be a good boy and not be naughty.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who was Khurram's father and grandfather?
questions.answer: His father was Jehangir and his grandfather was Akbar the Great.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who was the beautiful girl at the Meena Bazaar, and what was she selling?
questions.answer: She was Arjumand Banu Begum, and she was selling silk and glass beads.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In which year did Khurram marry her?
questions.answer: In 1612, five years after he first saw her.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What does the name 'Shah Jahan' mean?
questions.answer: It means 'the brightest crown of the world'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did Shah Jahan order the court to mourn for two years?
questions.answer: Because his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal had died and he was overcome with grief.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the Taj Mahal made of, and where was the marble brought from?
questions.answer: It is made entirely of white marble, brought from all over Asia.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the Taj Mahal counted as today?
questions.answer: It is counted as one of the seven wonders of the world.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did Khurram meet Arjumand Banu Begum, and what happened next?
questions.answer: One day prince Khurram went to the Meena Bazaar with his friends, where he caught a glimpse of an extremely beautiful girl selling silk and glass beads — Arjumand Banu Begum, of a Persian noble family. Falling in love, he went back to his father and said he wanted to marry her. Five years later, in 1612, he married her. When he became emperor in 1628 he was known as Shah Jahan, and she was renamed Mumtaz Mahal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why and how was the Taj Mahal built?
questions.answer: When his beloved Mumtaz Mahal died, Shah Jahan was heart-broken; he ordered the court to mourn for two years and promised to build the most beautiful mausoleum over her grave. Beside the river Yamuna in Agra he built the world's most beautiful mausoleum — entirely of white marble brought from all over Asia. It took 22 years and 22,000 workers, and the magnificent monument became known as the Taj Mahal, one of the seven wonders of the world.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write five sentences about the Taj Mahal (Activity 7 / paragraph type).
questions.answer: 1) The Taj Mahal is a beautiful monument situated in Agra. 2) It was built by the emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal. 3) It is a mausoleum — a building built to house the dead — beside the river Yamuna. 4) It is made entirely of white marble and took 22 years and 22,000 workers to build. 5) Today the Taj Mahal is counted as one of the seven wonders of the world.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the lesson titled 'Memory in Marble'? What does the Taj Mahal stand for?
questions.answer: It is called 'Memory in Marble' because the Taj Mahal is a memory made in stone — Shah Jahan built it to keep alive his love for Mumtaz Mahal long after her death. It stands as a monument of love and grief, showing how deep feelings can inspire great and lasting art. Made of pure white marble, this magnificent building has carried that memory across the centuries for the whole world to admire.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The Taj Mahal took 22 years and 22,000 workers. What does this tell us about it?
questions.answer: It shows the enormous effort, skill and resources that went into it — thousands of workers labouring for over two decades. Such a scale tells us how important the monument was to Shah Jahan and why it became one of the world's finest works of art.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Tarun learned more from his grandfather than from his textbook. What does this suggest?
questions.answer: It suggests that elders carry valuable knowledge and stories, and that listening to them can teach us things books do not. Sharing knowledge across generations makes learning richer and keeps memories alive.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why do you think people from all over the world come to see the Taj Mahal?
questions.answer: Because it is breathtakingly beautiful, made of shining white marble, and because it tells a moving story of love. Its beauty, history and craftsmanship together make it one of the seven wonders of the world.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: To which school was the author admitted?
questions.explanation: He went with his maternal uncle and was admitted to Ballygunge Government High School.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did the author show the teacher his work for admission?
options: He answered questions and solved sums
questions.answer: He answered questions and solved sums
questions.explanation: The teacher gave him questions and sums; he wrote the answers, solved the sums and showed them.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Seen from above, the school looked like the English letter ______.
questions.explanation: The building was 'L'-shaped: the vertical part was the hall, the horizontal part the classrooms.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The word 'gallery' in the lesson means ______.
questions.explanation: Word Trove: gallery = an upper floor of seats in a theatre or hall.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which Shakespearean play was performed in the school hall?
questions.explanation: The author remembers watching The Merchant of Venice, with scenes by foreign actors Greenberg and Salim.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did B.D. Roy take great care about?
options: That the boys pronounced English correctly
questions.answer: That the boys pronounced English correctly
questions.explanation: B.D. Roy taught English and made sure the boys pronounced it correctly.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Whose handwriting on the blackboard did the author admire greatly?
questions.explanation: The head pandit Bhattacharya Sir wrote Bengali so beautifully that the author thought no one could do better.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why was the author the favourite of the drawing teacher, Ashubabu?
options: Because he was good at sports
Because he could draw very well
questions.answer: Because he could draw very well
questions.explanation: The author was not good at sports but could draw very well, so Ashubabu favoured him.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did everything look smaller when the author returned after ten years?
options: Because he himself had grown much taller
questions.answer: Because he himself had grown much taller
questions.explanation: He had grown from 5 ft 3 in to about 6½ ft, so the same rooms seemed smaller.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In 'The children are singing', the verb is in the ______ tense.
questions.explanation: 'are singing' = am/is/are + verb-ing, which is the present continuous tense.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: With whom did the author go to the school, and why?
questions.answer: He went with his maternal uncle to get admitted to Ballygunge Government High School.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What was the school hall used for? (Activity 1b)
questions.answer: It was used for the annual prize-distribution ceremony and for festivals such as Saraswati Puja; plays and a film were also shown there.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What was the name of the Shakespearean play performed in the hall? (Activity 1c)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name the two foreign actors mentioned by the author. (Activity 1d)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What subject did B.D. Roy teach, and what did he take care about? (Activity 6a)
questions.answer: He taught English and took great care that the boys pronounced it correctly.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What event did Ashubabu conduct? (Activity 6c)
questions.answer: He conducted an event called 'Music drawing'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How long did the author spend at this school? (Activity 6d)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did everything look smaller when the author returned after ten years? (Activity 7d)
questions.answer: Because he himself had grown much taller — from five feet three inches to about six and a half feet — while the school had stayed the same.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: From which book is this lesson adapted?
questions.answer: From Satyajit Ray's 'Jokhon chhoto chhilam' (When I Was Small).

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How was the author admitted to Ballygunge Government High School, and what did the school look like?
questions.answer: One morning the author went with his maternal uncle to the school, where the class teacher gave him some questions and sums. He wrote the answers and solved the sums; the teacher nodded to show they were correct, and he was admitted. The school stood east of the Beltala Road Police Station, with the playground to the south and a lofty wall around it. Seen from above it looked like the English letter 'L' — the vertical part was the hall and the horizontal part was the row of classrooms.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write about the teachers the author remembers from his school.
questions.answer: He remembers B.D. Roy, a small man who taught English and took great care that the boys pronounced it correctly — he once told the story of 'The Ox and the Frog' and taught how to say 'the' before a vowel or a consonant. He fondly remembers the head pandit, Bhattacharya Sir, whose Bengali handwriting on the blackboard was so excellent that the author thought no one could do better. He also recalls the drawing teacher Ashubabu, whose favourite he became and who conducted the 'Music drawing' event.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe Ashubabu's 'Music drawing' event.
questions.answer: For 'Music drawing', a blackboard and coloured chalk were placed on a table. A student would sing a song, while another student drew a picture on the blackboard, trying to capture in lines and colour the essence of the song. It was one of the many memorable episodes of the author's six years at school, and shows how the school encouraged creativity.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did the school seem smaller when the author returned, and what does he conclude?
questions.answer: Ten years later the author returned for a reunion and was surprised that the hall, doors, classrooms, benches and corridor all looked smaller than before. The reason was natural: when he left he was five feet three inches tall, and he had since grown to about six and a half feet. He had grown a lot, but the school had not. He concludes that it is indeed a pleasure to look back and relive the past moments of joy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The author was poor at sports but excellent at drawing. What does his story teach us about talents?
questions.answer: It teaches that everyone has different strengths — being weak in one area (sports) does not mean one is weak everywhere. His drawing made him special to Ashubabu. We should value and develop our own talents instead of feeling discouraged by what we cannot do.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why do the small rooms of childhood feel so big in memory and so small when we return?
questions.answer: Because as children we are small and the world feels large; as we grow taller and older, the same spaces feel smaller. It is we who change, not the places. Memory keeps them grand because of the feelings and experiences attached to them.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The author calls looking back 'a pleasure'. Do you think remembering school days is always pleasant? Give your view.
questions.answer: Mostly it is pleasant — we remember friends, teachers and happy events. (Students may add that some memories may be sad or embarrassing, but even those become dear with time, because they are part of growing up.)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where did the monkey live?
options: On the bank of a river
questions.answer: On the bank of a river
questions.explanation: The monkey lived on the bank of a river and crossed over to the island for fruits.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did the monkey go to the island every day?
options: To eat the sweet, delicious fruits
questions.answer: To eat the sweet, delicious fruits
questions.explanation: The island's trees grew sweet, juicy, delicious fruits that attracted the monkey.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did the monkey cross the river?
options: By jumping from the bank to the rock to the island
questions.answer: By jumping from the bank to the rock to the island
questions.explanation: He leapt from the bank onto the rock and from there onto the island.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What was the crocodile's plan to catch the monkey?
options: To lie still on the rock and pretend to be the rock
questions.answer: To lie still on the rock and pretend to be the rock
questions.explanation: On his wife's advice, the crocodile lay still on the rock, pretending to be the rock itself.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What made the monkey suspicious?
options: The rock looked larger than usual
questions.answer: The rock looked larger than usual
questions.explanation: The monkey noticed the rock had 'become so large' and guessed the crocodile was playing a trick.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did the monkey test whether it was really the rock?

questions.explanation: He called out, 'Hello, Rock!… Why aren't you talking to me as you always do?' — and the crocodile foolishly replied.

questions.prompt: What had the monkey observed about the crocodile?
options: Whenever it opened its mouth, its eyes shut completely
questions.answer: Whenever it opened its mouth, its eyes shut completely
questions.explanation: The monkey had noticed that the crocodile's eyes closed whenever it opened its mouth — and used this to escape.

options: Open his mouth wide (and close his eyes)
questions.answer: Open his mouth wide (and close his eyes)
questions.explanation: He said he would jump into the crocodile's mouth, so the crocodile opened it wide and shut his eyes.

questions.subject: english

options: He leapt onto the crocodile's head and jumped to the bank
questions.answer: He leapt onto the crocodile's head and jumped to the bank
questions.explanation: With the crocodile's eyes shut, the monkey leapt onto its head and quickly jumped back to the riverbank.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The short form 'wasn't' means ______.
questions.explanation: Contracted forms: wasn't = was not (similarly didn't = did not, you're = you are).

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: On the bank of a river.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: Because there was hardly any other animal on the island.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What was so attractive to the monkey? (Activity 1c)
questions.answer: The sweet, juicy, delicious fruits that grew on the island's trees.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where did the crocodile live? (Activity 1d)
questions.answer: On the far bank of the river, with his wife.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who gave advice to the crocodile about catching the monkey? (Activity 1e)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who swam to the rock? (Activity 6a)
questions.answer: The crocodile swam to the rock and lay still, pretending to be the rock.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did the monkey become suspicious? (Activity 6b)
questions.answer: Because the rock looked much larger than it had been when he came.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What was the monkey's suggestion to the crocodile? (Activity 6c)
questions.answer: That the crocodile open his mouth wide so the monkey could jump straight in.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the monkey tell the crocodile from his safe shelter? (Activity 6d)
questions.answer: That the crocodile was a foolish crocodile who could never catch him — bigger in size, but the bigger fool.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did the monkey reach the island, and why did he visit it daily?
questions.answer: The monkey lived on the bank of a river. In the middle of the river was a small island whose trees grew sweet, juicy, delicious fruits. A huge rock stood between the bank and the island, part of it above the water. Attracted by the fruits, the monkey would jump from the bank onto the rock and from there leap onto the island. As there was hardly any other animal there, he was in no danger, so he decided to visit the island every day.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What plan did the crocodile and his wife make, and what happened when the crocodile tried it?
questions.answer: The crocodile's wife wished to taste the monkey's sweet flesh and advised the crocodile to lie still on the rock, pretending to be the rock, so that when the monkey jumped on him he could be caught. Next morning the crocodile swam to the rock and lay still with his back and tail above water. But the monkey noticed that the rock had become unusually large, suspected a trick, and tested it by greeting the rock — and the crocodile foolishly answered.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe how the clever monkey escaped from the crocodile.
questions.answer: Once the crocodile admitted he was no rock and said he would eat the monkey, the monkey made a plan. He had observed that whenever the crocodile opened its mouth, its eyes shut completely. So he said he was sorry and would jump straight into the crocodile's mouth — if only the crocodile opened it wide. The greedy crocodile happily opened his mouth wide and closed his eyes. At that moment the monkey leapt onto the crocodile's head and quickly jumped back to the safe riverbank, escaping unharmed.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the moral of 'The Clever Monkey'?
questions.answer: The moral is that intelligence and presence of mind are stronger than size and strength. The crocodile was big and powerful but greedy and foolish; the small monkey survived because he was observant and quick-witted. The story teaches us to use our brains, stay alert in danger, and not to be greedy or proud of mere strength.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The monkey noticed that the rock 'had become so large'. Why is being observant important?
questions.answer: Small observations can save us from big dangers. Because the monkey paid attention and noticed the change, he sensed the trick in time. Being observant helps us spot problems early and act wisely.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Both the crocodile and his wife wanted to eat the monkey. What does the story say about greed?
questions.answer: Greed makes one careless and foolish. The crocodile was so eager for the monkey's flesh that he fell for the monkey's trick and opened his mouth and shut his eyes. The story warns that greed often leads to loss.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The monkey called himself 'a bigger fool too'. Why do you think he said this?
questions.answer: He said it wittily, to mock the crocodile — pretending to call himself a fool while really telling the crocodile that HE was the bigger fool. It shows the monkey's cleverness and good humour even in a tense moment.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which song was Pritam asked to sing on Republic Day?

questions.explanation: In 1910 Nazrul met the revolutionary Nibaran Chandra Ghatak, who was his teacher.

Because he lived in a jail
questions.answer: Because he protested against British cruelty through his poems and songs
questions.explanation: He voiced his protest by writing poems, essays and songs that inspired the youth to fight for freedom.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In 'He inspired the Indian youth to boldly fight for freedom', the word 'boldly' is an adverb of ______.
questions.explanation: 'boldly' tells HOW the youth should fight — so it is an adverb of manner.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Was India independent when Nazrul was born? (Activity 2a)
questions.answer: No — India was under British rule when Nazrul was born in 1899.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name some of the folk plays that young Nazrul wrote. (Activity 2b)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who was Nazrul's teacher? (Activity 2c)
questions.answer: The revolutionary Nibaran Chandra Ghatak, whom Nazrul met in 1910.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: A poet, and the Headmaster of the school Nazrul joined next; he inspired Nazrul to write poems.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which poets influenced Nazrul? (Activity 6a)
questions.answer: Kumud Ranjan inspired him, and he was also influenced by Rabindranath and the Persian poets Hafez and Khaiyyam.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did Nazrul have to leave the army? (Activity 6b)
questions.answer: After the First World War his 49th Bengal Regiment was disbanded in 1920, so he came to Calcutta.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In which magazine was the poem 'Anandamoyeer Agamone' published? (Activity 6c)
questions.answer: In 'Dhumketu', the magazine Nazrul started in 1922.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did Nazrul start fasting? (Activity 6d)
questions.answer: It was his way of protesting against the torture of the British in the Hooghly Jail; he fasted for more than a month.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name two of Nazrul's works mentioned by the father for Pritam to read.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe the early life of Kazi Nazrul Islam.
questions.answer: Nazrul was born on 24th May 1899 in the village of Churulia, near Asansol in the Burdwan district of undivided Bengal, when India was under British rule. As a child he was attracted to folk theatre and later wrote folk plays such as 'Daata Karna' and 'Kabi Kalidas'. In 1910 he met the revolutionary Nibaran Chandra Ghatak, who became his teacher, and the poet Kumud Ranjan Mullick, Headmaster of his next school, inspired him to write poems. He was also influenced by Rabindranath and the Persian poets Hafez and Khaiyyam.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did Nazrul protest against British rule? Why is he called the Rebel Poet?
questions.answer: After his army regiment was disbanded in 1920, Nazrul came to Calcutta, where Indians had risen against British rule. He protested against the cruelty of the British in his own way — by writing poems, essays and songs that voiced his protest and inspired the Indian youth to boldly fight for freedom. The British feared his writings could instigate the freedom fighters. Because he rebelled against authority with his pen, he is called the Rebel Poet of India.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What happened after Nazrul started the magazine 'Dhumketu'?
questions.answer: Nazrul started 'Dhumketu' in 1922 and published the poem 'Anandamoyeer Agamone' in it. The perturbed British raided the office of 'Dhumketu' and arrested the poet from Kumilla. He was transferred to the Hooghly Jail, where he began fasting to protest against the torture of the British. He fasted for more than a month, and in December 1923 he was released from jail.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the lesson end, and what is its message?
questions.answer: After hearing Nazrul's story from his father and Uncle Rahim, Pritam is filled with pride and says he will now sing 'Karar oi louha kopat' with zeal and passion; the elders promise to sing along with him. The message is twofold: injustice can be fought with the pen rather than weapons, and when we know the story behind our patriotic songs and heroes, our love for the country grows deeper and more meaningful.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Nazrul fought the British with poems and songs, not weapons. Is the pen really mightier than the sword? Give your view.
questions.answer: Yes — words can reach thousands of hearts and inspire a whole generation, which no single sword can do. The British feared Nazrul's writings more than weapons, which is why they raided 'Dhumketu' and jailed him. Ideas, once spread, cannot be arrested.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why do you think Nazrul chose fasting as his protest in jail?
questions.answer: In jail he had no pen or press, so he protested with the only thing left — his own body and will. Fasting showed that his spirit could not be broken by torture, and it drew attention to the injustice without any violence.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Pritam wanted to sing the song with more zeal AFTER learning about Nazrul. Why does knowing a creator's story change how we feel about a work?
questions.answer: Because the story gives the words meaning: 'Karar oi louha kopat' is no longer just a tune but the voice of a poet who really faced prison bars. Knowing the struggle behind a song makes us sing it with true feeling.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where is the Charminar located?
questions.explanation: From the heritage chart: the Charminar is in Hyderabad, Andhra Pradesh.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How many students went on the trip to the Bandel Church?
questions.explanation: Mita and her classmates, a team of twenty-five, went to Bandel with their history teacher.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why didn't the teacher let Mita describe the church on the way?
options: So the place would still be a surprise to everyone
Because Mita had not really seen it
questions.answer: So the place would still be a surprise to everyone
questions.explanation: The teacher said that if Mita told everything, they would know the place beforehand and it would hold no surprise.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The Bandel Church can be seen as a memorial of the ______ settlement in Bengal.
questions.explanation: It is one of the oldest churches in West Bengal and a memorial of the Portuguese settlement.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In which year did the Augustinian monks build the church at Bandel?
questions.explanation: The Portuguese called Augustinian monks from Goa, who built the church at Bandel in 1599.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: During whose reign was the church destroyed in the siege of Hooghly?
questions.explanation: The church was destroyed during the Mughal siege of Hooghly under Emperor Shah Jahan.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the prisoners have to stand before as punishment?
questions.explanation: About a thousand Christians were taken to Agra fort and made to stand before ferocious elephants.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did one elephant surprisingly do?
options: It carried Father Joan da Cruz to the emperor and knelt
questions.answer: It carried Father Joan da Cruz to the emperor and knelt
questions.explanation: One elephant gently carried Father Joan da Cruz to Shah Jahan and knelt, as if asking for mercy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How much land did Shah Jahan donate to the church in 1633?
questions.explanation: He granted money to rebuild the church and donated 777 bighas of land to it in 1633.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Choose the correct past form: 'Last Sunday, Reba ______ the zoo.'
questions.explanation: 'Last Sunday' signals the past, so the simple past form 'visited' is correct.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where did the bus stop? (Activity 4.1)
questions.answer: It stopped in front of the Bandel Church.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What were the names of the two friends of Mita? (Activity 4.2)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How many Christians were taken to Agra fort? (Activity 4.3)
questions.answer: About a thousand (a few thousand) Christians.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who were made to stand before the ferocious elephants? (Activity 4.4)
questions.answer: The Christian prisoners taken from Hooghly to Agra fort.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the elephant do with Father Joan da Cruz? (Activity 4.5)
questions.answer: It gently carried him right up to the emperor and knelt before him, as if asking for mercy.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where was the fort of Shah Jahan located? (Activity 4.6)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why was the Bandel trip the students' first outing exciting?
questions.answer: Because it was their very first school outing, so everybody was eager and excited.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who had visited the Bandel Church before, and with whom?
questions.answer: Only Mita had visited it before, with her uncle who lives in Bandel.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name three things the students saw inside the church.
questions.answer: The Prayer Hall, the mast of a ship, and the statue of Mother Mary (also the cemetery, wall paintings of Christ, and a huge clock with four faces).

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the teacher say about the two reasons for visiting a place?
questions.answer: The teacher explained that sometimes we visit a place simply for fun and enjoyment, and at other times a visit helps us gather valuable information about the history and culture of a place. That is why she did not let Mita describe the church beforehand — she wanted the visit to be both a surprise and a chance to learn.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe the history of the Bandel Church up to the building of the church.
questions.answer: The Bandel Church is one of the oldest churches in West Bengal and a memorial of the Portuguese settlement in Bengal. Around 1571 the Portuguese began to use Bandel as a port. The Mughal Emperor Akbar gave them permission to build a town in Hooghly, and in 1579 they built a port on the banks of the Hooghly. They then called Augustinian monks from Goa, who built the church at Bandel in 1599.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Tell the story of the siege, the elephant and Shah Jahan's mercy.
questions.answer: The church was destroyed during the Mughal siege of Hooghly under Shah Jahan, and about a thousand Christians were taken as prisoners to Agra fort. To punish them, they were made to stand before ferocious elephants. But surprisingly, one elephant gently carried the priest, Father Joan da Cruz, up to the emperor and knelt before him, as if begging for mercy. This peculiar incident impressed Shah Jahan, who set the prisoners free, sent them back to Bandel, gave money to rebuild the church and donated 777 bighas of land to it in 1633.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the students see inside the church, and how did they feel at the end of the trip?
questions.answer: Inside, the students saw the Prayer Hall, the mast of a ship, the cemetery and the statue of Mother Mary, along with many wall paintings of Christ and a huge clock with four faces. At the end of the day they felt happy and enriched, and agreed that the trip had been most rewarding to all of them.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: The teacher stopped Mita from telling everything. Was she right? Give your view.
questions.answer: Yes — knowing every detail beforehand would have removed the joy of discovery. By keeping some surprise, the teacher made the visit more exciting and let the students observe and learn for themselves. Curiosity makes a trip far more rewarding.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why do you think the kneeling elephant changed Shah Jahan's mind?
questions.answer: The emperor saw something unusual and almost gentle in the elephant's act — as if even an animal was pleading for mercy. It moved him and made him reconsider the harsh punishment, showing that a single surprising act of kindness can soften even a powerful ruler's heart.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why are old buildings like the Bandel Church called 'buildings to remember'?
questions.answer: Because they store the memory of important events and people — settlements, sieges, acts of mercy and the cultures that built them. They are like history books made of stone; remembering them keeps the past alive for future generations.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which weapon did Duryodhana and Bhima favour?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana and Bhima favoured the mace — a heavy weapon with a spiked metal head.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did Dronacharya like Arjuna the most?
options: Because Arjuna practised with great concentration
questions.answer: Because Arjuna practised with great concentration
questions.explanation: Arjuna's great concentration made him the best archer, so the Guru liked him most.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did Dronacharya place on the tree for the test?
options: A wooden bird with a painted eye
questions.answer: A wooden bird with a painted eye
questions.explanation: He placed a wooden bird with a prominently painted eye and asked the princes to hit its eye.

questions.prompt: What did Arjuna say he could see?
options: Only the eye of the bird
questions.answer: Only the eye of the bird
questions.explanation: Arjuna replied that he could see only the eye of the bird, and nothing else.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What sound did the arrow make as it sprang from the bow?
questions.explanation: With a loud twang — the sound of a stretched string — the arrow struck the bird's eye.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the main lesson of the test?
options: Success needs full concentration on the target
questions.answer: Success needs full concentration on the target
questions.explanation: Dronacharya taught that only with intense concentration on the target can one succeed.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In 'Yudhisthira replied innocently', the word 'innocently' is a/an ______.
questions.explanation: 'innocently' tells HOW he replied — it qualifies the verb 'replied', so it is an adverb.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who was the Guru of Dronacharya? (Activity 4.1)
questions.answer: Parasurama — Dronacharya learnt the secret of powerful weapons from him.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where was the bird placed? (Activity 4.2)
questions.answer: On one of the trees in the woodland near Dronacharya's ashram.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did Yudhisthira see? (Activity 4.3)
questions.answer: He could see the Guru, the tree, the people around him, and the bird.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did Dronacharya like Arjuna the most? (Activity 4.4)
questions.answer: Because Arjuna practised archery with great concentration and became the best archer.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is most important in hitting a target? (Activity 4.5)
questions.answer: Concentration — looking only at the target and nothing else.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who were the royal students of Dronacharya?
questions.answer: The five Pandava brothers and the hundred Kaurava brothers, who were cousins.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why did Duryodhana and Dushasana criticize their Guru?
questions.answer: Because they were jealous that he favoured Arjuna, claiming they were no less skilful in archery.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What was the twins' (Nakula and Sahadeva's) favourite weapon?
questions.answer: The sword — they were most comfortable with swords.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did the test finally reveal?
questions.answer: That Arjuna was the best archer, because he alone concentrated only on the target.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who were Dronacharya's students, and what were their favourite weapons?
questions.answer: Dronacharya's students were the five Pandava brothers and the hundred Kaurava brothers, who were cousins. They learnt the skills of using weapons from him, each prince having a favourite: Duryodhana and Bhima favoured the mace, Yudhisthira chose the spear, Arjuna was fascinated by bows and arrows, and the twins Nakula and Sahadeva were most comfortable with swords. The Guru treated all equally but liked Arjuna the most for his great concentration.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe the test that Dronacharya arranged and how the princes answered.
questions.answer: To settle the Kauravas' complaint, Dronacharya placed a wooden bird with a painted eye on a far-off tree and told the princes to hit its eye. He asked each what he could see. Yudhisthira saw the Guru, the tree, the people and the bird; Duryodhana saw the bird, the tree, the leaves and the fruits; Bhima, Nakula and Sahadeva answered similarly — so all were asked to step aside. Only Arjuna said he could see nothing but the eye of the bird, so he was allowed to shoot.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did Arjuna pass the test, and what did Dronacharya teach the others?
questions.answer: When asked what he could see, Arjuna replied that he could see only the eye of the bird and nothing else, keeping his bow steady and his eyes fixed on the target. Dronacharya, pleased with his immense concentration, told him to shoot, and the arrow struck the bird's eye. He then explained that the others had failed because they noticed many things — the trees, leaves, fruits and people — instead of concentrating on the task, while Arjuna alone had truly concentrated. That is why Arjuna was the best student.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is the moral of 'The Bird's Eye'? How can you apply it in your life?
questions.answer: The moral is that success comes only with full concentration: when we aim for a goal, we must look at the target and nothing else. Distractions make us miss the mark. In studies, sport or any task, if we focus completely on what we are doing — like Arjuna fixing his eyes on the bird's eye — we are far more likely to succeed.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: All the princes had the same training, yet only Arjuna passed. Why?
questions.answer: Because skill alone is not enough — focus decides success. The others let their attention wander to the trees, fruits and people, while Arjuna shut out everything except his target. The story shows that concentration turns ability into achievement.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why do you think Dronacharya tested the princes instead of simply telling them Arjuna was the best?
questions.answer: A test let the princes discover the truth for themselves. By hearing their own answers, the jealous Kauravas understood WHY Arjuna was best, and the lesson about concentration was taught far more powerfully than mere words could.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How can you bring 'the bird's eye' kind of concentration into your studies?
questions.answer: By removing distractions (phone, noise), setting one clear goal at a time, and giving full attention to the task in front of me — for example, focusing only on the question I am solving, just as Arjuna focused only on the eye of the bird.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What is a social reformer?
questions.answer: A person who works to remove harmful customs and bring positive changes (reforms) in society.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name the four reformers in the opening chart and one work of each.
questions.answer: Vidyasagar (widow remarriage), Vivekananda (message of brotherhood), Raja Rammohan Roy (stopping Sati) and Derozio (the Young Bengal Movement).

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What was Begum Rokeya's full name, and what kind of family was she born into?
questions.answer: Begum Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain; she was born into an affluent (wealthy) Muslim family.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did Begum Rokeya educate herself?
questions.answer: Though she never went to school, she learnt Arabic and Persian at home and also took lessons in Bengali and English, through her own interest and efforts.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is Begum Rokeya called a pioneer?
questions.answer: Because she was the first (a path-breaker) to set up a school for the poor Muslim girls of Bengal.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name three famous books written by Begum Rokeya.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did Begum Rokeya write against?
questions.answer: Against social injustice and the restrictions suffered by women.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which association did she found, and what was it?
questions.answer: The Anjuman-e-Khawateen-e-Islam — a Muslim Women's Association.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What did Begum Rokeya believe about women and the family?
questions.answer: She had immense faith in women's power and proclaimed that without the help of women a family can never stand.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Who was Begum Rokeya, and what work did she do for women?
questions.answer: Begum Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain was a remarkable woman whose efforts considerably changed female education in Bengal. Kind and generous to disadvantaged women, she helped the poor and the helpless and was the pioneer in setting up a school for poor Muslim girls, because she strongly believed in the need for modern education for women. She also founded the Anjuman-e-Khawateen-e-Islam, a Muslim Women's Association, and gave lessons to women on various subjects.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Although she never went to school, Begum Rokeya became very learned. Explain how.
questions.answer: Born into an affluent Muslim family, Rokeya never attended a school, yet she educated herself through her own interest and efforts. She learnt Arabic and Persian at home and also took lessons in Bengali and English. Her self-education was so deep that she went on to write great books in both Bengali and English, which will be remembered forever.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: What were Begum Rokeya's ideas about women and society?
questions.answer: Rokeya wrote extensively on the need for social reforms, and against social injustice and the restrictions suffered by women. She had immense faith in women's power and proclaimed that without the help of women a family can never stand. She questioned the gendered division of labour and believed there should be no gender inequality. A strong humanist, she also had deep faith in the inherent unity of the nation.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is Begum Rokeya called 'a great social reformer'?
questions.answer: Because, like Vidyasagar, Raja Rammohan Roy and the others in the chart, she fought to change harmful customs and improve society. She opened the path of modern education for poor Muslim girls, wrote powerfully against injustice and the restrictions on women, founded a women's association, and worked all her life for education and equality. Her writings and her schools changed the lives of countless women, earning her a lasting place among Bengal's great reformers.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Begum Rokeya was wealthy and could have lived comfortably. Why do you think she chose to work for poor girls?
questions.answer: Because she believed education and dignity should belong to everyone, not just the rich. Having taught herself, she understood the power of learning and wanted the disadvantaged to share it. Her kindness and sense of justice mattered more to her than her own comfort.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Rokeya never went to school yet became a famous writer. What does this teach us?
questions.answer: That determination and self-effort can overcome the lack of opportunities. If we truly want to learn, we can teach ourselves and achieve great things. It also reminds us how unfair it was that girls of her time were denied schooling — which is exactly what she fought to change.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why are social reformers important for a country?
questions.answer: Because they notice what is wrong in society — harmful customs and injustice — and have the courage to change it. Reformers like Rokeya, Vidyasagar and Raja Rammohan Roy made society fairer and kinder, and the freedoms many enjoy today are the result of their struggles.

questions.answer: By 1/100th of a second, in a photo-finish
questions.explanation: She faltered in the finals and lost the bronze by just 1/100th of a second in a photo-finish.

questions.explanation: After retiring in 1991, she made a sudden comeback in 1998 at the age of 34 and won bronze in Japan.

questions.answer: The Arjuna Award (1983) and the Padma Shri (1985)
questions.explanation: She received the Arjuna Award in 1983 and the Padma Shri Award in 1985.

questions.explanation: Word Trove: sprint = a short, fast race.

questions.answer: P.T. Usha — Pilavullakandi Thekkeparambil Usha, the Sprint Queen of India.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: She was affected by ill health in her early childhood, yet her promise in sports could be clearly noticed.

questions.answer: On 27th June 1964, at Payyoli, a village in Kerala.

questions.answer: Silver medals in the 100 metres and the 200 metres sprint.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where and in which event did Usha win gold with a new Asian record?
questions.answer: At the Asian Track and Field Championship in Kuwait (1983), in the 400 metres race.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Name the four events Usha won at the 1986 Seoul Asiad. (Activity 6.2)
questions.answer: The 200 metres, the 400 metres, the 400 metres hurdles and the 4x400 metres relay.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How old was Usha when she participated in the Seoul Asiad? (Activity 6.1)

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: When did Usha make her comeback, and what did she win?
questions.answer: In 1998, at the age of 34, she won bronze medals in the 200 m and 400 m at the meet in Fukkowakka, Japan.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Which titles did the Indian Olympic Association give Usha?
questions.answer: 'Sportsperson of the Century' and 'Sportswoman of the Millennium'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write about the early life and first successes of P.T. Usha.
questions.answer: Pilavullakandi Thekkeparambil Usha, known as P.T. Usha, was born on 27th June 1964 at Payyoli, a village in Kerala. Though affected by ill health in early childhood, her promise in sports was clear, and against all odds she became one of India's greatest athletes. She made her first national record in the 100 metres sprint in 1977, at just 13. After a quiet 1980 Moscow Olympics and silver medals at the 1982 New Delhi Asiad, she won gold in the 400 metres with a new Asian record at the Kuwait ATF Championship in 1983.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics photo-finish and what it shows about Usha.
questions.answer: At the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics, Usha finished first in the semi-finals but faltered in the finals. In a nail-biting photo-finish she lost the bronze medal by just 1/100th of a second — the same heartbreak that Milkha Singh had faced in the 1960 Olympics. Though she won no medal, the closeness of the result showed how great an athlete she was and how narrowly India missed Olympic glory; it also showed her courage, for she came back stronger afterwards.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did Usha become 'Asia's sprint queen', and what honours did she receive?
questions.answer: In the 1986 Seoul Asiad, at just 22, Usha became Asia's sprint queen by winning four events — the 200 m, 400 m, 400 m hurdles and the 4x400 m relay. Over the next five years she grew from strength to strength and became an icon for Indian women and a living legend in Kerala. She was honoured with the Arjuna Award (1983) and the Padma Shri (1985), and the Indian Olympic Association gave her the titles 'Sportsperson of the Century' and 'Sportswoman of the Millennium'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the lesson called 'The Finishing Point', and what does Usha's life teach us?
questions.answer: 'The Finishing Point' is the finishing line of a race, and also stands for the spirit of finishing strongly what one begins. Usha's life teaches courage and perseverance: a sickly child became the Sprint Queen; she lost a medal by 1/100th of a second yet returned to win many more; she even made a comeback at 34. As the poem says, 'Winners are people who just never quit.' Her story tells us that hard work and never giving up can turn disappointments into greatness.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Usha lost the 1984 bronze by 1/100th of a second, yet she is remembered as a champion. Why?
questions.answer: Because greatness is measured by effort, courage and consistency, not by one result. Losing by such a tiny margin showed she was among the very best in the world. Her response — coming back to win many more medals — is what makes her a true champion.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: In Kerala, newborn babies were named after Usha. What does this tell you about her place in people's hearts?
questions.answer: It shows she was loved and admired as a living legend and a role model. Parents hoped their children would have her qualities — determination, talent and pride for the country. Few sportspersons earn such deep affection.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Usha came back to the track at 34, after retiring. What lesson does her comeback teach?
questions.answer: That it is never too late to try again, and that passion and self-belief can overcome age and setbacks. Her surprise bronze medals proved that determination keeps a champion's spirit alive long after others would have given up.

questions.explanation: Rakesh Sharma, whose picture was on the cover of Barun's book, was the first Indian in space.

questions.explanation: On 3rd April 1984 he went to space aboard Soyuz T-11, spending about seven days there.

questions.explanation: Barun read that Kalpana Chawla was the first Indian woman to go to space.

questions.explanation: Kalpana was born in 1962 in Karnal, in the state of Haryana, and was nicknamed 'Montu'.

questions.explanation: She studied Aeronautical Engineering at Punjab Engineering College, then in the USA.

questions.subject: english

questions.explanation: Kalpana flew aboard the space shuttle Columbia; it was lost on re-entry on 1st February 2003.

options: A hill on Mars and a star in deep space
questions.answer: A hill on Mars and a star in deep space
questions.explanation: A hill on Mars and a star in deep space have been named after Kalpana, keeping her memory alive.

questions.explanation: 'am reading' (am/is/are + verb-ing) shows an ongoing action — the present continuous tense.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: Because he was sick and lay in bed; his friends Ravi, Pinky and Osman went without him.

questions.answer: A book on India's space travels, to lift his spirits and take his mind off his sadness.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: He was born in 1949 in Patiala, in the state of Punjab.

questions.answer: Seven days, twenty-one hours and forty minutes, aboard Soyuz T-11 (from 3rd April 1984).

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: When asked how India looked from space, he replied 'Sare Jahan Se Achcha' — the best in the world.

questions.answer: She was born in Karnal, Haryana, in 1962, and was nicknamed 'Montu' by her family.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Where did Kalpana go in 1982? (Activity 4b)
questions.answer: She went to the USA to study at the University of Texas at Arlington.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: The space shuttle Columbia, lost on 1st February 2003.

questions.subject: english

questions.answer: A hill on Mars and a star in deep space.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How did a book help Barun on a sad Sunday?
questions.answer: Barun was miserable because he was sick and could not join his friends for a picnic. To cheer him up, his mother gave him a book on India's space travels, with Rakesh Sharma on the cover. As soon as Barun began reading, he forgot all about his sadness and became absorbed in the stories of Rakesh Sharma and Kalpana Chawla. The book carried him 'beyond barriers', showing how reading can lift our spirits and open up a whole world.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Write about the life and achievement of Rakesh Sharma.
questions.answer: Rakesh Sharma, the first Indian in space, was born in 1949 in Patiala, Punjab. He studied at St. George's Grammar School and Kendriya Vidyalaya Tirumalagiri in Hyderabad, and joined the National Defence Academy as an Air Force cadet in July 1966. On 3rd April 1984 he travelled to space aboard Soyuz T-11 and spent seven days, twenty-one hours and forty minutes there. When the Prime Minister, Mrs. Indira Gandhi, asked how India looked from space, he proudly replied, 'Sare Jahan Se Achcha'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Describe Kalpana Chawla's journey from Karnal to space.
questions.answer: Kalpana Chawla, the first Indian woman in space, was born in 1962 in Karnal, Haryana, and was nicknamed 'Montu'. She studied Aeronautical Engineering at Punjab Engineering College, then went to the USA in 1982 to study at the University of Texas at Arlington, and in 1988 became a research scientist at NASA. In 1997 she flew on the space shuttle Columbia and spoke with India's Prime Minister from orbit. She flew again on Columbia on 16th January 2003, but the shuttle was lost with its crew on re-entry on 1st February 2003. A hill on Mars and a star are named after her.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Why is the lesson called 'Beyond Barriers'? What does it teach us?
questions.answer: It is called 'Beyond Barriers' because Rakesh Sharma and Kalpana Chawla crossed the great barrier of the earth's atmosphere to reach space, and because they overcame barriers of difficulty through hard work, courage and sincerity — Kalpana rose from a small town in Haryana to NASA. Even Barun, sick in bed, travelled beyond his barriers through a book. The lesson teaches us to dream big and work hard, because determination can carry us past every barrier, and that sincere work is remembered even after we are gone.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: How does the book change Barun's mood, and what does this tell us about reading?
questions.answer: The book turns Barun's misery into excitement — he forgets his sadness and eagerly reads on. It shows that reading can comfort us, take our minds off our troubles, and carry us to places we cannot physically go. A good book is itself a journey 'beyond barriers'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Kalpana Chawla's mission ended in tragedy, yet she is celebrated. Why?
questions.answer: Because she is remembered for her courage, hard work and achievements, not for how her life ended. She broke barriers as the first Indian woman in space and inspired millions. Honours like a hill on Mars and a star named after her show that sincere effort is remembered 'even when one is no more'.

questions.subject: english
questions.prompt: Both astronauts came from ordinary Indian towns. What does this say about big dreams?
questions.answer: It shows that where you start does not limit where you can go. With hard work and determination, a child from Patiala or Karnal can reach space. Their stories tell every student that big dreams are possible, whatever one's background.
